reflection:
This online public-goods game pits private incentives (withdrawing coins for sure private payoff) against group incentives (leaving coins in the shared pot which are multiplied by 1.8 and evenly returned). Because players do not see others' contributions before deciding, conditional cooperation (matching others' visible choices) is limited; players must rely on priors, their own social preferences, and the sparse feedback they receive (their own net payoff and any rewards they gave or received). Rewarding is costly (3 coins per reward unit) and gives only 2 coins to the recipient, so rewards are used rarely and usually to signal appreciation for high contributors or to try to sustain cooperation. Over 29 rounds we expect an initial period of moderate cooperation from more pro-social players (here: GORILLA and DUCK), while more self-interested or low-engagement players (SLOTH, CHICKEN) contribute less. Without strong, frequent rewarding or communication, the typical dynamic is a gradual decline in contributions as the private incentive to withdraw becomes more salient and trust decays. Reward events will be sparse and targeted to high contributors early on; later rounds will show low contributions and little rewarding.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 23 rounds and punishment. Each round each player either keeps the 20 coins privately (defect: contribution = 0) or leaves them in the shared pot (cooperate: contribution = 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly, so a group's total number of cooperators k gives everyone 12.5*k from the public good. Defection gives a private 20 plus the public share; cooperation gives only the public share. That creates temptation to free-ride (defect) while group payoffs are highest when everyone cooperates.

Punishment is costly (1 coin per punishment unit sent) and reduces the target by 2 coins per unit. Punishment is anonymous in the round summary (players see amounts spent and amount deducted but not who punished them). With 23 rounds, early rounds often establish norms: a few punishments early can stabilize cooperation, but as the end approaches, incentives to defect increase because future retaliation loses force; therefore we expect some cooperation in early-to-mid rounds, occasional defections to test enforcement, targeted punishments sparingly to enforce norms, and a decline in cooperation toward the final rounds (final-round defection often common). Chats are usually short and used to warn, apologize, or note behavior.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game creates a tension between private extraction and group benefit. Each coin a player leaves in the pot increases the total pot (multiplied by 2.1) but returns only 0.525 coin to the contributor themselves (2.1/4), so the individually rational move is to withdraw (free-ride) while the group optimum is to leave more coins in the pot. Because decisions are simultaneous, players face uncertainty about others’ choices; conditional cooperators may contribute more when they expect others to do the same, while selfish players will withdraw more. Punishment (cost 1 to sender, -3 to target) and reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target) introduce social enforcement: modest, targeted sanctions or rewards can sustain contributions but are themselves costly to the sender.

Given the player priors: GORILLA is the most pro-social/active enforcer, CHICKEN leans toward lower contributions, DUCK is sensitive to context and may react to low contributors, and SLOTH drifts toward moderate contributions. In a 10-round repeated setting with identification of who acted, I expect contributions to start moderately high for some players, then drift downward (typical decline) as endgame approaches. Punishments and rewards will be used sparingly — mostly to deter persistent low contributions (targeting CHICKEN or others who contribute little) or to thank consistent contributors (small 1-unit rewards). Retaliatory cycles are possible but limited given the costly nature of sanctions.

game_explanation:
This public goods game (PGG) with 4 players over 6 rounds pits the incentive to free-ride (keep 20 privately) against the group benefit of contributing the full 20. Each contributed 20 raises the group pot, but the multiplier (1.6) spreads gains thinly: a sole cooperator loses value relative to free-riders, while full cooperation benefits everyone. Because players can punish or reward after seeing contributions, we expect a dynamic where:
- Early rounds include testing and signaling (some cooperate to signal willingness; some free-ride).
- Punishment will be used sparingly to discourage persistent free-riding; rewards will appear occasionally to reinforce cooperators.
- Once punishment is used, some free-riders may switch to cooperating to avoid future penalties.
- In the final round, incentive to defect is highest (no future rounds to enforce reputation), so we may see opportunistic defection.

Chats will be sparse and purposeful: brief commitments, reactions to punishment, or short appeals for cooperation. Punishment and reward actions will be limited in number each round.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game pits four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) against the temptation to withdraw coins privately each round versus contributing to a shared pot that is multiplied and distributed. Early rounds typically show modest-to-moderate cooperation because contributions benefit everyone, but individual incentives push players to withdraw more (free-ride), especially as the end of the 10 rounds approaches. Because players do not see others' choices before acting, decisions are based on priors, impressions of others' tendencies, and past observed behavior. Punishment and reward are costly to the sender but can change others' incentives: punishment (1 coin cost per unit, -3 to the target) is used sparingly to deter low contributions; reward (1 coin cost per unit, +1 to the target) is used to reinforce cooperators. Uncertainty about others' future behavior, and the finite horizon, typically produces some cooperation early and possible decline late; occasional punishments and sparse rewards can stabilize cooperation mid-game. Given the personas, expect SLOTH to lean toward lower contributions (occasionally responsive to sanctions), GORILLA to be an enforcement/cooperative type, DUCK to cooperate and sometimes reciprocate, and CHICKEN to be conditionally cooperative but responsive to framing (and to punish/reward modestly). Punishment/reward activity should be limited and targeted rather than widespread.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players and 29 rounds. Each round players decide privately how much of 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can spend costly reward units (cost 3 to sender, +2 to target). Rewards are sparse in real experiments because they are costly, so small, targeted rewards are used mainly to reinforce cooperation or reciprocate generous choices. Uncertainty arises from lack of pre-decision visibility (decisions are simultaneous) and from heterogenous motives across players: some will try to sustain cooperation by rewarding generous contributors, some will test the system by defecting occasionally, and some will react to perceived norms.

Using the provided personas as priors:
- CHICKEN tends to be cooperative and proactive in shaping norms (likely to contribute relatively high and reward cooperators sparingly).
- SLOTH is exploratory/strategic (likely to contribute fairly high and reciprocate visible generosity).
- GORILLA is framing-sensitive and will follow emerging norms but reward rarely.
- DUCK is anchor-driven and more likely to drift or test anchors (occasional lower contributions), copying salient prior amounts when uncertain.

I therefore predict moderate-to-high contributions on average, occasional dips when DUCK (or another player) tests freeriding, and sparse reward use primarily from CHICKEN and SLOTH to reinforce contributors; GORILLA will occasionally send a single small reward. Rewards mainly go to steady high contributors (SLOTH and CHICKEN), and are used selectively rather than every round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players, 23 rounds, and a binary choice each round: either leave the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw the full 20 for private use (contribute 0). Because the pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, the per-player return from total contributions C is 0.625 × C. A defector (withdraw 20) gets that private 20 plus the public share; a contributor (leave 20) gets only the public share that round.

Key likely dynamics:
- There is a strong short-run incentive to free-ride (withdraw 20) because defectors keep 20 while still receiving the public return. If others contribute, defectors earn more that round.
- Players with conditional-cooperator tendencies (respond to others' behavior) will likely start by cooperating and then reduce contributions when they see repeated free-riding.
- Persistent cooperators (here SLOTH is likely to be the most stable contributor) will try to sustain cooperation by messaging, but repeated exploitation will erode contributions.
- Communication can temporarily revive cooperation (anchoring statements like “I will always contribute” or “let’s all contribute 20” often help), but end-game effects make unravelling likely: as the final round approaches, rational players often switch to defection.
- Uncertainties include whether CHICKEN or GORILLA will exploit cooperative pledges, and whether DUCK will follow conditional reciprocity or punish by defecting.

I therefore predict phases: early cooperation with at least one exploiter, mid-game oscillations as players try to punish or rebuild cooperation, occasional spikes of full cooperation after coordinated messages, and a likely collapse to defection in the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 29-round public goods game with costly punishment and costly rewards. Individually, each coin a player keeps yields a private benefit of 1 while each coin contributed returns 0.45 to each player (1.8/4), so there is a private incentive to withdraw (free-ride) but a group-level incentive to contribute. Uncertainty about others' choices and the ability to punish or reward mean early rounds typically reveal baseline tendencies (moderate contributions), occasional defections invite targeted punishment, and the availability of sanctions/rewards can sustain or restore cooperation midgame. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so expect it to be used sparingly and mainly against obvious low contributors; rewards are also costly and used selectively to reinforce cooperation. Toward the final rounds, end-game effects (declining cooperation) are likely, though intermittent punish/reward signals can produce temporary recoveries.

game_explanation:
This 10-round public goods game pits four players with different behavioral priors against the classic tension: each coin left in the shared pot benefits everyone by 2.1 total (0.525 per person) but costs the contributor a full coin privately. The dominant incentive is to withdraw (free-ride), but social preferences, reciprocity, and the ability to punish/reward can sustain contributions. Expect initially mixed contributions (people often start moderately cooperative), occasional small rewards for higher contributors to signal approval, and sparse punishment targeted at clear under-contributors. Punishment is costly to the sender and very costly to the recipient (1 coin spent vs. 3 coins deducted), so it will be used rarely and usually only to curb repeated low contributions. Because the game has a known finite horizon, cooperation may drift down toward the end (endgame effect), with fewer sanctions in the final round. The four avatars' priors (SLOTH: less active/anchoring-neutral; GORILLA: norm-sensitive and consistent; DUCK and CHICKEN: more deliberative/variable) produce modest variation: Gorilla tends to be reliably cooperative and to reward/police, Sloth drifts and sometimes needs prompting, Duck and Chicken hover around moderate cooperation and respond to sanctions/rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round every player effectively chooses either to contribute 20 to the shared pot (withdraw 0) or to keep 20 privately (withdraw 20), so contributions are always 0 or 20. The group pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally: with k contributors the per‑player return from the pot is 12.5·k. That means defecting (keeping 20) gives a private advantage of 20 relative to contributing, but if more players contribute everyone benefits. Punishment is available: sending 1 punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and reduces the target's payoff by 2. Punishments are anonymous to targets, and players see round summaries that list each player's net payoff, how much they spent punishing others, and how much was deducted from them by punishment.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: conditional cooperation emerges — a mix of contributors and defectors. Contributors tend to punish visible defectors occasionally to enforce cooperation, but punishment is used sparingly because it is costly.
- Middle rounds: some cycles of cooperation and retaliation. Punishment sometimes stabilizes cooperation when a defector is singled out, but if punishments are inconsistent the group may revert to mixed behavior.
- Late rounds (final 3–5 rounds): standard endgame pressure leads to more defection; punishments decrease since retaliation is less future‑relevant. Because players cannot identify who punished them, retaliation is diffuse and punishers bear costs without clear deterrence.

Uncertainties:
- Who will punish consistently (enforcers) vs who will free-ride on enforcement. GORILLA is more likely to monitor/punish given the persona; SLOTH is least likely to punish. DUCK and CHICKEN are conditional actors and may punish sometimes. These tendencies shape the predicted play: occasional punishments by GORILLA and DUCK to discourage persistent free-riding, intermittent coordination failures, and a decline in cooperation near the end.

game_explanation:
This 4-player public-goods game gives each player a binary choice each round: withdraw the full 20 for private use (contribute 0) or leave it in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split evenly, so a group's contributions generate a fixed per-player return: (total contributions * 1.6) / 4. After redistribution, players may spend costly points to punish or reward others; punishments are expensive to the target (–7 per unit) and cheaper-to-source (–3 per unit), rewards give +4 to the target at a cost of –3 to the source. Players see round summaries but cannot identify who punished or rewarded them. The game lasts 6 rounds; this makes behavior dynamic — early rounds often feature testing and signaling, middle rounds may see stabilization around cooperation once sanctions/rewards steer incentives, and the final round tends to have reduced cooperation because there is no future to condition on.

Given the player personas:
- SLOTH (high purchase-yes, strong anchor pull) will often cooperate early and prefer simple signals (punish/reward sparingly but tied to norms).
- GORILLA (high search willingness) is likely to try cooperation and respond conditionally (rewarding reciprocators, occasionally punishing persistent free-riders).
- DUCK (lower purchase-yes) is more likely to defect early and may retaliate when punished.
- CHICKEN (high framing sensitivity) may switch behavior after direct costs (punishments) or messages — conditional cooperation is likely.

I therefore predict: initial mixed cooperation (some test the waters), a few sparse punishments in early rounds to signal enforcement, conditional reciprocation and gradual convergence toward cooperation in the middle rounds, and a slight breakdown in the final round as endgame incentives kick in. Chat stays sparse and purposeful (short signals, complaints, or encouragement). Punishments and rewards are rare and focused on clear norm violations or to reinforce reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game where each round players choose whether to keep the full 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or put the full 20 into the group pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally across the 4 players. After payoffs from the public good are distributed, players may spend reward units (3 coins each) to give 4 coins per unit to others. Players see round summaries (their own net payoff, how much they spent on rewards, how much they received; and the same numbers for peers) but cannot see who gave specific rewards.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Free-riding (keeping 20) yields a higher immediate private payoff when others contribute. With no enforcement, defecting is a tempting default.
- Positive rewards can sustain cooperation by rewarding contributors and thus compensating them for the private cost; but rewards are costly to give, so players will use them sparingly.
- Early rounds often show experimentation: some players contribute, some free-ride. Players who care about future returns or fairness (or who react strongly to framing/messages) will try to coordinate on contribution with chat and selective rewarding.
- Mid-game, cooperation can increase if contributors reward those who contribute (and withhold rewards from defectors). In the final round, typical endgame logic pushes more players to defect because future punishment or reciprocation is not possible; however, a few may still give final gestures/messages or rewards.
- Given the four personas: DUCK (exploratory, likely to try cooperation and to respond to social cues), CHICKEN (framing-sensitive, likely cooperative and communicative), GORILLA (somewhat anchored but can switch), and SLOTH (anchored, likely to stick with a default selfish choice), I predict initial mixed behavior, movement toward cooperation by mid-game via rewards, and some final-round defection.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated, finite public goods game (23 rounds) with binary choices each round: either contribute all 20 coins to the shared pot (contribution = 20) or withdraw all 20 for private use (contribution = 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and divided equally among the four players, so each contributed 20 increases every player's share by 12.5. Because a player who withdraws receives the private 20 plus the pot share from others, withdrawing strictly dominates contributing for an individual (withdrawing always yields 7.5 coins more than contributing, given the payoff math). That makes pure self-interested free-riding the dominant strategy and predicts a drift toward universal withdrawal (contribution = 0) over time.

However, cheap talk (messages) and social preferences can produce temporary cooperation early on. Based on the player personas:
- DUCK (high search, high anchor pull) is the most likely to try to coordinate or test cooperation (sends more messages, sometimes contributes early).
- CHICKEN (high framing sensitivity, low willingness-to-pay) may be responsive to appeals but is cautious; might try a short-lived cooperation if convinced, then revert once exploited.
- GORILLA (low anchor pull/purchase) and SLOTH (low search; medium tendencies) are more likely to default to withdrawing; they are quiet and opportunistic in this setup.

So I predict some early promises and a small amount of cooperation (possibly two players contribute in round 1), then rapid erosion as others realize pledges are not credible and free-riding pays better. DUCK will make repeated, sparsely-spaced attempts to re-start cooperation (testing behavior), occasionally joined once by CHICKEN after a message, but for most of the 23 rounds contributions decline to all 0s. Chat will be sparse and focused on short pledges or complaints after summaries.

game_explanation:
This is a 3-player repeated public goods game (6 rounds). Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from a 20-coin endowment. Contributions are summed, multiplied by 1.9, and split equally among the three players; private payoff = 20 - your contribution + equal share of the multiplied pot. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 — less than 1 — so the one-shot incentive is to contribute 0, but group welfare is maximized when everyone contributes more.

Likely dynamics:
- Round 1 often starts relatively cooperative if players can coordinate via messages. DUCK (high anchor sensitivity) may propose a focal number early; GORILLA (moderate anchor pull) may follow anchors to some degree; SLOTH (high framing sensitivity, low exploratory behavior) is more likely to accept a clear suggestion but may be cautious about deviating.
- Over repeated rounds, a gradual decline in contributions is typical as incentive to free-ride and distrust grow, unless strong reciprocity or enforcement appears in messages. Because players see each other's round net payoffs after each round, they can infer whether others cooperated and adjust.
- Uncertainty remains about message credibility (players can announce contributions but might lie), and about how fast others will defect. In the final round, rational incentive to free-ride is strongest, so contributions are likely to fall further.

Given the personas and the 6-round horizon, I predict an initially coordinated moderate contribution that slowly declines across rounds as players test and respond to one another.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round everyone decides how many coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution 0–20); the pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split evenly. The private incentive is to withdraw coins (free-ride) while the group incentive favors higher contributions. Because the game lasts a known finite number of rounds (10), there is tension between short-run selfish incentives and longer-run conditional cooperation or reciprocation.

Expectations and uncertainties:
- Conditional cooperation is likely: players who see others contribute more will tend to maintain or raise their own contributions; those facing free-riders may reduce contributions.
- Punishment and reward are available but costly to the sender. In real PGGs these are used sparingly: punishment is most often directed at clear low contributors and rewards at clear cooperators. Expect a few targeted punishments (usually -1 unit) and occasional single-unit rewards.
- Individual tendencies from the provided personas make SLOTH most likely to under-contribute initially and change slowly, GORILLA most likely to contribute consistently and to monitor others, DUCK somewhat sensitive to framing and may adjust more, and CHICKEN likely to be conditionally cooperative and active in a few sanctions or rewards.
- Over rounds we expect some convergence toward moderate cooperation as players respond to others' behavior, with a final-round decline possible because of the known end.

game_explanation:
This public goods game strongly favors withdrawal (private take) in each round: every player can either leave the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw all 20 for private use (contribute 0). Given the payoff rule (the pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally), not withdrawing strictly dominates contributing for any given beliefs about others — you always gain the extra 20 by withdrawing. That makes the one-shot best response to withdraw. The game still allows social dynamics because players see each round's net payoffs for everyone after choices are made; from those payoffs they can infer who contributed. This lets conditional cooperators and norm-builders (here especially DUCK and sometimes CHICKEN) try to reward or punish contributors, but because withdraw is dominant, free-riding will tend to persist and usually increase toward the end of the 23 rounds (an end-game collapse).

Using the supplied personas as priors: SLOTH has a very low tendency to cooperate and is unlikely to send many messages; GORILLA is somewhat exploratory and may test contributing occasionally but still leans toward private-taking; DUCK is the most cooperative and will try contributing repeatedly as a moral gesture; CHICKEN may try conditional strategies or coordination proposals early, but will respond to repeated exploitation by switching to private-taking. Expect sparse chat, with DUCK and CHICKEN attempting short coordination messages early, and otherwise short complaints or "I'll keep contributing" / "I won't" statements. Over the 23 rounds we should see a few isolated contributions (mostly from DUCK, sometimes CHICKEN or GORILLA experimenting), but by the later rounds almost everyone will be withdrawing 20 every round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 29 rounds, a 1.8 multiplication of the group pot, and a costly peer punishment/reward stage. Rational self-interest implies each coin contributed returns only 0.45 to the contributor (because 1.8 is split among 4), so myopic selfish incentives push toward 0 contributions. However, with repeated interaction, visible identities, and the ability to punish and reward, conditional cooperation and social enforcement can sustain above-zero contributions early on. Expect:

- Early rounds: tentative cooperation from more pro-social players (CHICKEN, DUCK), some moderate contributions from others (GORILLA, SLOTH) but with temptation to free-ride.
- Middle rounds: occasional targeted punishments of blatant free-riders and sparse rewards for cooperators. Punishments are costly and therefore used sparingly — mostly single units — to signal disapproval; rewards are also sparse and generally small.
- Late rounds: an endgame decline in cooperation as the horizon approaches (players cut contributions toward zero), with much less punishment/reward activity because enforcement is costly and future incentives fade.
- Uncertainties: exact timing and magnitude of punishments/rewards are noisy; some players (e.g., SLOTH) respond slowly to sanctions, and CHICKEN tends to be the most consistent cooperator and occasional enforcer/rewarder.

Now the round-by-round transcript. Use the avatar order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN for every contribution list.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with three players, 16 known rounds, and private endowments of 20 coins each round. Each round players choose an integer contribution (0–20). The group pot is doubled and split equally: this creates a tension — contributions raise the shared return but each player privately forgoes 1 coin to add 2/3 coin to each player, so the individual marginal private return on giving is negative (private incentive to give 0). Because the horizon is finite and common knowledge (16 rounds), there is an expectation of declining cooperation over time (end-game effect), with faster decline if players see that free-riding is rewarded.

Use of personas: SLOTH and GORILLA are framing-sensitive and show some tendency to follow norms (they start moderately cooperative and adjust based on observed summaries). DUCK has a moderate anchor pull and lower tendency to buy/cooperate, so is more likely to contribute less and exploit cooperative tendencies of others. Because players see each round’s summary (their own net payoff and the corresponding information for peers), behavior is responsive: if free-riding yields higher net payoff, conditional cooperators will cut contributions. I therefore predict moderate contributions early, a relatively rapid decline once free-riding appears profitable, and near-zero contributions by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 29 rounds. Each round every player decides how many of their 20 coins to withdraw (private use) and how many to leave in the shared pot (contribution). The contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players can spend coins to punish or reward others (punishment: source pays 3 per unit, target loses 4 per unit; reward: source pays 3 per unit, target gains 2 per unit). Players see their own round payoff and who punished/rewarded them, but not peers' decision details.

Likely dynamics and incentives here:
- Early rounds usually show relatively high contributions as players test each other and try to build cooperation. Because the multiplier makes contributions socially beneficial but individually costly, free-riding is tempting.
- When someone consistently contributes much less (a visible defector), others may use costly punishment sparingly to enforce norms. Rewards are used even more sparingly to encourage cooperators.
- Over many rounds, contributions tend to drift downward (end-game effect) unless punishment/rewards sustain cooperation. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it is used selectively; reward is also costly and used rarely.
- Uncertainty arises from not seeing peers' decisions before choosing each round and from limited observability of who is willing to punish. With 29 rounds, mid-game enforcement can keep cooperation for a while, but toward the final rounds contributions commonly decline.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a 10-round public goods game where each player keeps some private coins and contributes the rest to a shared pot that gets multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. The social return to contributing (2.1/4 = 0.525 per coin to each player) is less than 1, so unilateral incentives favor taking money out (contribute less). However, with the ability to punish (cost 1 to sender, -3 to target per unit) and reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target per unit), players can shape behavior. Expect mixed dynamics:

- Early rounds: cooperatively inclined players (SLOTH, DUCK, CHICKEN) start with moderate-to-high contributions; a more selfish player (GORILLA) contributes little. Cooperators will sometimes punish low contributors and occasionally reward high contributors.
- Middle rounds: punishments tend to reduce severe free-riding; some gradual convergence toward moderate contributions follows, with punishments used sparingly because they are costly to the sender.
- Late rounds: with the end approaching, incentives to punish decline slightly (no future enforcement), and some players may defect more in the final round. Overall punishment and reward activity is low and targeted (mostly directed at repeated low contributors or as small positive feedback to consistent cooperators).

I simulate plausible, sparse punishment/reward patterns and evolving contributions across the 10 rounds given the four player personas (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, short (6-round) public goods game with costly peer punishment and rewards. The key incentives:
- Contributing (leaving the 20 coins in the pot) reduces your private take today but raises everyone’s shared payout; defecting (withdrawing 20) gives an immediate private gain but reduces group payoffs.
- Punishment is costly to impose (cost 3 per unit) but inflicts a larger cost on the target (7 per unit), which can deter free-riding; rewards cost 3 to give and add 4 to the receiver, encouraging cooperation.
- Because the game is finite and known to end after 6 rounds, there is pressure to free-ride in later rounds (backward induction), but social preferences, reputation concerns, prior punishments/rewards, and messages can sustain cooperation earlier.
Uncertainties:
- Players don't see others' choices before deciding, so decisions are based on expectations, prior behavior, and messages.
- Punishment is anonymous in the summary (players see amounts but not who targeted them), which reduces direct retaliation but still allows for conditional behavior across rounds.
Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: some cooperation, with defectors punished sparingly to enforce norms.
- Mid game: cooperation may stabilize if punishments and occasional rewards create enforcement and positive reciprocity.
- Final round: some players will defect, expecting no future consequences; punishments then become less frequent but may still occur out of spite or norms.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 29 rounds, four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), and the option to punish or reward after seeing contributions. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- The public good is produced from contributions (players keep 20 - contribution privately). The group benefit from contributions is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so an individual’s marginal per-coin return to the group is 1.8/4 = 0.45 — less than the private return of keeping a coin (1). Thus free-riding (keeping more coins) is individually tempting every round.
- Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation as players test each other’s behavior; contributions often start above the selfish level (0) but below full contribution (20).
- Absent enforcement, contributions tend to decay over time as people respond to free-riding. That’s likely here: cooperation will be highest early, then drift downward.
- Punishment and reward allow conditional enforcement. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 per unit) but can deter low contributions by subtracting 4 from the target; rewards are also costly (3 to giver) but benefit the target (adds 2). In real games these tools are used sparingly. Expect occasional targeted punishments when a player defects strongly and occasional small rewards to sustain high contributors.
- Uncertainties: players only see their own summary and who targeted them, not others’ summaries, and they don’t observe choices before deciding. This produces social inference and occasional retaliatory patterns when punished. The long horizon (29 rounds) encourages strategic shifts: some will try to prospectively enforce cooperation mid-game, and many will begin to defect more in the final rounds when future retaliation is irrelevant.
- Given the provided personas: GORILLA tends to stick to anchors (so may be relatively persistent in their chosen level), SLOTH and DUCK are moderately conditional cooperators, and CHICKEN is variable and more likely to fluctuate or defect episodically. Overall I predict moderate initial contributions, some isolated punishments when a player drops sharply, a few small rewards to steady contributors, and a gradual decline toward lower contributions with more defection in the final rounds.

This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players and 23 rounds. Each round a player either contributes the entire 20 coins (contribute = 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribute = 0). The public pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so free-riding gives a private gain at the expense of others (defection yields a higher immediate payoff). Punishment is available: paying 1 coin to reduce a target's payoff by 2. That creates a deterrent against defection, but punishment is itself costly and noisy (players can't see who punished them). Given the personas:

- GORILLA and DUCK are attentive and likely to favor cooperation and enforcement; GORILLA is most likely to signal and punish early to deter free-riding.
- CHICKEN is somewhat sensitive to framing and may be cooperative but can lapse.
- SLOTH is more likely to economize and initially free-ride but can be coerced into cooperating by targeted punishment.

Typical unfolding: early rounds contain probing behavior and a mixture of cooperation and defection. If a defector is punished, they often switch to cooperation. Once a cooperative norm forms (with occasional lapses), most rounds show full contributions and few punishments. Lapses are punished sporadically. Near the final round some end-game defections may reappear because future retaliation is limited.

game_explanation:
This three-player public goods game is a repeated, finite-horizon interaction with costly punishment and private, integer contributions each round. Incentives: contributing benefits the whole group via a 2x multiplier split equally, so group-optimum is high contributions while individual incentive is to free-ride. Punishment lets players impose costs on low contributors, but it is costly to the punisher and anonymous to the punished (they see only the deduction, not who sent it). Uncertainties: players do not observe others' choices before acting and only learn round summaries (own net payoff, how much they spent punishing, and how much punishment they suffered).

Given the provided personas, I predict a pattern of relatively high, steady cooperation from GORILLA (consistently high contributions and intermittent punishment of perceived free-riders), conditional and somewhat variable cooperation from SLOTH (moderate contributions, occasional punishment when free-riding is repeated), and frequent low contributions from DUCK (attempts to free-ride, sometimes increased contributions after being punished). Punishment will be used sparingly and mainly targeted at DUCK when s/he persistently contributes little; this will sometimes bring DUCK back toward higher contributions, but not fully eliminate exploitation. Toward the final rounds contributions drift downward as backward-induction incentives and fatigue kick in; punishments remain sparse because they are costly and have limited future return in late rounds.

game_explanation:
This 4-player repeated public goods game balances private withdrawal (immediate private payoff) against contributing to a shared pot that is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. The dominant short-run incentive is to withdraw everything (contribute 0) since any private coin kept yields 1 privately while each contributed coin returns only 2.1/4 = 0.525 to its donor in expectation. However, because contributions benefit the whole group, social welfare is higher when players contribute. With a 10-round finite horizon, backward induction predicts declining contributions over time, but human subjects typically begin with some cooperation and then gradually reduce contributions rather than collapsing immediately to zero. The optional reward stage (cost 1 to give 1 to another) lets players incent or reciprocate contribution behavior: small positive transfers can sustain cooperation early on, but rewards are usually sparse because they are costly and visible.

Using the provided personas: CHICKEN is the most pro-social/cooperative and likely to contribute relatively more and reward cooperators; GORILLA and DUCK are moderately engaged and likely to contribute moderately and occasionally reward; SLOTH tends to contribute little and reward rarely. I predict starting-round moderate contributions (especially from CHICKEN), a few small reciprocal rewards early on that selectively reinforce contributors, and a gradual decline in contributions across the 10 rounds with rewards becoming rarer toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a three-player repeated public-goods game (16 rounds). Each round every player privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot. The pot is doubled and split equally among the three players. That means each coin you contribute reduces your private holdings by 1 but increases total group payout by 2, so the per‑person return on one coin is 2/3 (≈0.67). Because 2/3 < 1, contributing is socially beneficial but individually costly — the standard public-goods tension: the group does best if everyone contributes, while individuals are tempted to free-ride.

Given the player priors:
- SLOTH tends to conserve resources, is unlikely to search for complex strategy cues, and is more likely to favor low contributions (free-riding or minimal contributions).
- GORILLA searches and adapts more; is relatively sensitive to framing and likely to be a conditional cooperator (matching others or trying to stabilize cooperation).
- DUCK is moderately willing to contribute and also sensitive to frames; likely to try cooperation early but will back off if exploited.

With no communication and no punishment, I expect an early period where GORILLA and DUCK contribute moderately (attempting cooperation) while SLOTH gives little. Seeing SLOTH benefit disproportionately will push the others to reduce contributions (conditional cooperation). Near the final rounds, standard endgame logic predicts more defection (lower contributions), often ending with everyone contributing zero.

That means each coin you contribute reduces your private holdings by 1 but increases total group payout by 2, so the per‑person return on one coin is 2/3 (≈0.67). game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a six-round public-goods game with small group size (4) and strong incentives to free-ride: each player can either keep all 20 coins (withdraw 20 → contribute 0) or leave them in the pot (withdraw 0 → contribute 20). The group return is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so when many contribute everyone benefits, but an individual who defects gains privately in the short run.

Key dynamics I expect:
- Early rounds: some players will contribute to signal cooperation; a visible free-rider will likely be punished (costly for punishers but intended to enforce norms).
- Middle rounds: if punishments successfully make defecting costly, defectors may switch to contributing; cooperators may reward those who switch to reinforce cooperation.
- Endgame (rounds 5–6): forward-looking players anticipate fewer future consequences and are more likely to defect, so cooperation typically unravels. Punishment becomes less effective late in the game because retaliation or reputation-building is limited.

Given the supplied personas, I predict SLOTH and CHICKEN are inclined to contribute early, GORILLA tends to track norms (initially cooperates), and DUCK is the most likely to free-ride initially. Punishments and rewards will be sparse: a few targeted punishments early to deter free-riding, occasional small rewards to reinforce cooperation, and escalation of defection near the end.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with three players. Individually, the dominant incentive is to free-ride (keeping your coin avoids a sure loss), because each coin you contribute returns only 1.9 total to the group and thus only 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 to you — less than 1. However, group payoffs are higher when contributions are higher, so conditional cooperation, signaling, or short-term reciprocity can sustain contributions early on.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Round 1: Some players (more prosocial or optimistic) start with relatively high contributions; others experiment or free-ride. With messaging allowed, a short cooperative message may increase initial contributions from some.
- Middle rounds: Players observe their and peers' round nets. If some players free-ride while others contribute, contributors tend to reduce their contributions (conditional cooperation). Free-riders often maintain low contributions because they see the immediate payoff advantage.
- Late rounds (end-game): Expect declining contributions as foresight (knowing the game ends) reduces the incentive to sustain cooperation. A final-round bump can happen if players attempt a “last-minute” cooperative gesture, but usually cooperation decays.
- Messaging can temporarily coordinate or shame free-riders; expect sparse, strategic messages rather than constant chat.

Given the player personas:
- DUCK (most donation-oriented) will start relatively high and try to encourage cooperation.
- GORILLA (sensitive to anchors and framing) will respond to signals and may adjust contributions more boldly.
- SLOTH (anchoring tendencies and mixed purchase willingness) may experiment or exploit others’ cooperation — likely to contribute lower amounts and respond less to appeals.

Now the simulated transcript follows round-by-round.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits four players against the standard temptation to free-ride. Each round everyone chooses a contribution (0–20); contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so the socially efficient choice is high contribution but the individually dominant move (if others' contributions are fixed) is to withdraw everything. Because players can reward others after seeing contributions, small bilateral rewards can sustain higher cooperation by selectively rewarding contributors and withholding rewards from free-riders. Uncertainty early on will lead to a mix of testing and cautious cooperation; if some players reliably contribute, conditional cooperators will increase their contributions and reward them. Toward the end of the 10-round horizon we should see some decline (endgame effect) as backward induction predicts less incentive to sustain cooperation, but the presence of direct, identified rewards can moderate that decline. Players are likely to reward sparingly (a few 1-2 unit transfers) and mainly to reciprocate or reinforce high contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This 6-round public goods game combines a binary contribution choice (contribute all 20 or withdraw all 20) with costly punishment and reward. Key incentives:
- Contributing benefits the group: each contributed 20 yields 1.6*20 = 32 to the pot, so each of 4 players gets 8. The private return to a single cooperator is therefore smaller than the private gain from free-riding in the same round, so unilateral defection is tempting.
- Punishment (cost 3 to sender, -7 to target) and reward (cost 3 to sender, +4 to target) allow players to shape behavior, but these are costly to the sender and anonymous to targets. Because of these costs, players tend to punish and reward sparingly.
- Early rounds often see mixed behavior; punishments can encourage cooperation in middle rounds. In the final round, backward-induction and the lack of future consequences make defection more likely.
Uncertainties: who will pay the cost to enforce cooperation, whether players will use rewards instead of punishments, and heterogeneity in players' tendencies (some are more likely to keep cooperating; others tend to exploit).

I predict an initial mix of cooperation and defection, targeted punishment of clear free-riders, some reciprocal rewards to new cooperators, recovery toward cooperation in middle rounds, and partial unraveling in the final round. Chat will be sparse and purposeful (requests to cooperate, short acknowledgements).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with four players and six rounds. Each round each player either contributes their whole 20 coins to the public pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The public pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each player always gets the same share from the pot regardless of their own contribution. That makes withdrawing (defecting) the dominant short-run strategy, because a defector keeps 20 privately while still receiving the same public share as cooperators.

However, players can send costly rewards after seeing contributions: each reward unit costs 3 to the sender and gives 4 to the recipient. Rewards can be used to encourage cooperators (offsetting their private loss) or to signal appreciation; because players cannot see who gave them rewards in the summary, rewards operate as private transfers that nonetheless shape future expectations. Messages can also be used to coordinate or signal intent.

Typical dynamics I expect here: some players will try cooperating initially (either out of pro-social tendencies or to test reciprocity). Early cooperators are vulnerable to exploitation by defectors, so the group may use small mutual rewards to sustain cooperation. Mid-game, sparse rewarding and messages can temporarily raise cooperation rates (possibly achieving full cooperation for a round), but with a known six-round horizon there is pressure toward unraveling: cooperation tends to decline in later rounds as players exploit others or secure private payoffs. Rewards will be used sparingly (a few 1-unit transfers) and chat will be sparse and purposeful (requests, thanks, short coordination messages).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with punishment. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 to each player (less than 1 to the contributor), creating an individual incentive to free-ride while a group incentive favors contribution. Because choices are simultaneous and private, players face uncertainty about others' intentions; cheap-talk (chat) can partially coordinate expectations. Punishment is available after payoffs are revealed: sending 1 punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and reduces the target by 12 coins. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it will be used sparingly—mostly to discipline clear free-riders or retaliate.

Typical dynamics predicted here:
- Early rounds often see moderate cooperation as players test each other.
- If someone free-rides strongly, others may use a small punishment to signal enforcement; punished players often increase contributions subsequently.
- Because punishments are costly, they will usually be low (1 unit) when used.
- Communication is sparse but can be used to announce intentions or call out behavior.
- The final round can show more selfish moves (less fear of retaliation later), but players sometimes still punish out of fairness even at a cost.

Uncertainties: individual variation matters (the three personas suggest mixed tendencies), and cheap-talk credibility is limited because contributions are private. Also punishers cannot be identified in summaries, so social signaling via chat can change deterrence even if the summary is anonymous.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player effectively decides how much of their 20 coins to put into the shared pot (contribution 0–20); the pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 2.1/4 = 0.525, which is less than 1: contributing a coin reduces your private payoff by 1 but raises your own share by 0.525, so purely self-interested players prefer to withdraw everything. However, social return is higher if players contribute. In practice, we expect moderate initial contributions from a mix of conditional cooperators and more selfish types, some exploitation by low contributors, and occasional targeted punishment or sparse rewarding to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly to the punisher (1 coin per unit) and punitive to the target (3 coins per punishment unit); rewards cost 1 to the giver and give 1 coin to the recipient. Because punishments and rewards are expensive, they tend to be used sparingly and usually targeted at clear deviators or to reinforce cooperative behavior. Over 10 rounds we expect some initial cooperation that may erode when free-riders benefit, with intermittent punishment/reward cycles that can stabilize cooperation somewhat. In the final round contributions often drop because there's no future to influence.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public-goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) playing six rounds. Each round each player either keeps their 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leaves them in the group pot (contribute 20). The group pot is multiplied by 1.6 and shared equally, so the public return each round depends only on the number of contributors. Keeping coins always yields an immediate private advantage in that round, but cooperating (contributing 20) increases the total public return for everyone. After the public-return stage, players can send costly pro-social transfers ("rewards"): each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 4 coins. Messages are allowed at any time, but rewards are anonymous to recipients.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Individual incentives favor free-riding: taking the 20 privately often gives a higher immediate payoff than contributing, especially when others contribute. In short-run, defecting dominates contribution.
- However, repeated interaction and the reward mechanism create a path for conditional cooperation: contributors can be rewarded, making cooperation more attractive over time for those who expect reciprocal or third-party rewards.
- Rewards are costly, so they will be used sparingly. Expect cooperators to reward other cooperators occasionally, and defectors to be largely unrewarded. Since rewards are anonymous to recipients, players only see aggregate received amounts, making precise tit-for-tat harder.
- Uncertainty is important early: players don't know others' types. Over rounds, patterns (e.g., SLOTH consistently free-rides) become visible in payoffs and reward flows, and players may adapt — conditional cooperators remain with cooperation if they see it pays off via rewards; some players may experiment with switching.
- Toward the final rounds, some players may revert to self-interest (defect), but social preferences and reciprocity often sustain at least some cooperation across rounds.

Expect sparse, purposeful chat, few reward transfers per round, and a mixture of stable free-riding by at least one player and steady cooperation by one or two others, with some occasional switches from the middle players.

This repeated public-goods game typically starts with some conditional cooperation: a few players contribute a moderate portion of the 20 coins (trying to support the group pot), while one or two players test the waters by contributing much less. Because contributions are not shown directly, players infer others' generosity indirectly from their own payoffs and from whether they get rewarded — and rewards are identifiable (you know who rewarded you) but costly to give (3 coins cost to give 1 reward unit that grants the recipient 2 coins). That makes rewards an occasional signal or encouragement rather than a constant enforcement tool.

Given the four personas:
- DUCK looks likely to under-contribute and test others (low purchase/low cooperation tendency).
- GORILLA is information-seeking and moderately cooperative, willing to send a sparse reward to sustain collaborators.
- CHICKEN has strong anchoring and a higher baseline tendency to cooperate; they often contribute more early.
- SLOTH is slower to react but sensitive to norms/framing and will try small occasional rewarding gestures.

I therefore predict an opening with mixed-to-moderate contributions: CHICKEN and GORILLA more cooperative, DUCK contributing little, and SLOTH in the middle. Over many rounds, absent reliable enforcement or clear public signals of fairness, average contributions drift downward as free-riding is profitable. Rewards remain rare: occasional 1-unit rewards (each costing giver 3 coins) used sparingly to encourage cooperators or reciprocate signals, not as continuous policing. By the late rounds contributions tend toward low or zero (endgame unraveling), with only sporadic, small rewards.

The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so any contribution benefits everyone but is costly to the contributor. After payoff redistribution, players can spend coins to punish others: each punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and subtracts 2 coins from the target. Punishment is anonymous (players cannot see who targeted whom) but summary information shows how much each player spent punishing and how much each lost to punishment.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players are uncertain. Some will try to "seed" cooperation; others may test free-riding. Punishment in early rounds tends to be targeted but small (1–2 units) as players signal willingness to enforce norms.
- Middle rounds: if punishment successfully reduces the gains from free-riding, cooperation will stabilize (many rounds with 20 contributions). Punishments remain sparse and used mainly against repeated defectors.
- Late rounds: with a known finite horizon, cooperation typically erodes as the end approaches. Players may defect more often in the final rounds and punishment rates drop (less incentive to enforce norms when there are few or no future rounds).
- Chat will be sparse and used mainly to warn, threaten, or express disappointment; players rarely reveal exact punishment actions.

Given the provided personas, expect DUCK and CHICKEN to be more proactive in establishing cooperation and punishing defectors (DUCK is high-clarity/searching; CHICKEN has higher willingness), while SLOTH may be inclined to free-ride sometimes and be slower to respond. GORILLA may be more anchored and occasionally stubbornly defect but can switch when repeatedly punished.

game_explanation:
This is a 23-round repeated public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally across the 4 players, so a single player's private gain from keeping 20 is immediately attractive, but universal cooperation would give higher collective payoffs. Because players do not see decisions before choosing and the horizon (23 rounds) is known, we expect:

- Early rounds: some attempts at cooperation (messages proposing coordination, a few players contributing 20), but unilateral defection is tempting and yields visibly higher round payoffs, encouraging free-riding.
- Middle rounds: cooperation tends to unravel as players who see they were exploited switch to withdrawing 20. Occasional attempts to re-establish cooperation may appear in messages or one-off contributions, but trust is fragile.
- Endgame: with the finite known horizon, classic backward-induction pressures and declining incentives mean most players will defect in late rounds. Messages late in the game will be sparse and often pessimistic or resigned.

Given the provided personas: SLOTH tends to stick to anchors and may try cooperative heuristics occasionally; GORILLA is framing-sensitive and may attempt coordination early but switch after being exploited; DUCK is more likely to defect consistently (less search/coordination); CHICKEN is anchor-prone and may oscillate but is ultimately pragmatic. Expect sparse, purposeful chat (invitations to cooperate, short complaints, occasional proposals) and an overall decline in contributions toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game for 6 rounds with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round each player chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from a private endowment of 20. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.9, and divided equally. Because the multiplier (1.9) is less than the number of players (3), the individual incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride), while the group optimum is for all to contribute the full 20. Social dynamics typically show an initial mix (some testing cooperation) followed by downward drift if free-riding is observed. Messaging can create tentative coordination or promises, but without binding commitments and with repeated free-riding by a player, cooperators usually reduce contributions in later rounds. Uncertainties: whether any player will sustain cooperation despite exploitation; whether messaging will successfully coordinate conditional cooperation; and whether any player will opportunistically increase contribution late (e.g., to signal fairness or for intrinsic preference).

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits private incentives (each coin kept yields full private value) against a social multiplier (contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and shared). With 4 players, each contributed coin returns 0.45 to each player on average, so the selfish one-shot best reply is to withdraw all 20 (contribute 0). But in repeated play, players often start with above-myopic contributions, then adjust based on observed behavior; occasional costly rewards (3 coins paid to give 2 coins) can be used sparingly to reinforce cooperators. Expect an early phase of moderate cooperation (testing and reciprocal goodwill), followed by gradual erosion as free-riding appears and endgame decline toward low contributions. Rewards will be rare and targeted (small 1-unit tokens) to visibly high contributors; they will not fully prevent the downward trend because rewards are costly and information about peers' payoffs beyond contributions is limited.

game_explanation:
This three-player public goods game balances a tension between private incentives to keep coins and group gains from contributions. Each round players choose how many of 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. That makes full cooperation (high contributions) good for the group but gives each individual an incentive to free-ride. The punishment stage creates a deterrent: players can pay 4 coins per punishment unit to reduce a target's payoff by 12 coins, making punishment an effective (but costly) way to discipline low contributors.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test each others' willingness to cooperate. Expect moderate contributions, some short messages to signal intent, and infrequent punishment (used mainly in response to perceived free-riding).
- Middle rounds: norms may form. If someone consistently under-contributes, others will punish lightly to enforce cooperation.
- Final round: because there is no future, contribution usually falls (some players free-ride) and punishment incentives weaken; so expect more defection and little punishment in round 6.
Uncertainties: players' individual tendencies (here summarized by personas) mean contributions can vary; punishment is costly and thus used sparingly. Messages are typically brief and aim to request cooperation or register displeasure.

This repeated 6-round public goods game pits short-term incentives to withdraw the full private amount (defect) against longer-run incentives to cooperate by leaving the 20 coins in the common pot. Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), each round’s group outcome hinges on how many of the four players choose to contribute. The group return is 1.6× total contributions split equally, so more contributors raise everyone’s public payoff but defectors also keep their 20 private coins, giving defectors a one-round material advantage. Rewards allow players to transfer value to others (cost 3 to the giver, +4 to the recipient) and so can support reciprocity: cooperators can reward other cooperators, and some players may try to use rewards to steer behavior. Rewards are costly and anonymous, so we expect sparse rewarding targeted mostly at visible cooperators. Over six rounds we expect some conditional cooperation: early rounds see testing and a mix of cooperation and defection; mid-game may sustain cooperation among reciprocating players (via mutual small rewards); final round will likely see more defection since future retaliation isn't possible. Chats are likely sparse and used to request cooperation or thank reciprocators.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 3-player repeated public goods game with 6 rounds. Each round players privately choose how much of 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Because 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 < 1, each coin you personally keep yields more private value than the private return from contributing — so the strict selfish incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) every round. However, cheap talk and social preferences can produce cooperation early on; when some players contribute and others defect, cooperation typically decays across rounds as conditional cooperators reduce contributions in response to free-riding. There is also uncertainty: messages may be promises (cheap talk) or signals of intent, and players differ in trust and willingness to experiment. Given the provided personas, I expect modest cooperation in round 1 driven by SLOTH and DUCK talk, early free-riding by GORILLA, and a gradual decline toward near-zero contributions by the middle rounds, with one attempted revival that fails.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round repeated public goods game with three players. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Incentives: contributing reduces your private holdings but raises the common return for everyone. With a multiplier below the group size (1.9 < 3), the individually dominant action is to free ride (contribute 0), because each coin you keep yields more privately than the extra personal share from contributing. However, conditional cooperation and reputation-building can lead to positive contributions early on: players who value cooperation or expect reciprocation will contribute more, while self-interested players will contribute little. Uncertainty arises from private decisions and only seeing round-level payoffs (not others' intended motivations). Expect some early willingness to signal cooperation (promises or short messages) by more prosocial players (DUCK), limited reciprocation from more self-interested players (GORILLA), and moderate or conditional contributions from the ambivalent player (SLOTH). Over rounds we typically see decline of contributions as reciprocal expectations erode, and a marked drop in the final round as “end-game” free riding becomes common.

game_explanation:
This four-player public goods game lets each player either keep the full 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the public pot (contribute 20). Contributions are multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so cooperation raises total payoffs but creates a strong one-shot incentive to withdraw (free-ride). Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding, expectations, social preferences, and visible past punish/reward behavior drive responses. Punishment is costly to send (3 coins per unit) but costly to the target (7 coins per unit), so a few targeted punishment units can reduce the payoff advantage of defecting; rewards are also available (cost 3 to giver, +4 to receiver) to reinforce cooperation.

Typical dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test each other. Cooperators may try to signal cooperation and threaten punishment. Free-riding in round 1 often triggers modest punishment.
- Middle rounds: if punishment/reward stabilize, cooperation may rise; if punishments are insufficient, defectors can persist.
- Endgame (round 6): incentives to free-ride increase (no future consequences). Some players still punish in the final round out of fairness/retaliation, but overall group returns tend to fall.

Uncertainties:
- Who will defect early vs. cooperate (we use the player personas as priors).
- How strongly enforcement players will punish (punishment is costly, so typically sparing).
- Messaging can encourage cooperation but does not perfectly prevent free-riding, especially near the end.

Given personas, I predict SLOTH will generally favor cooperation and small positive reinforcement; GORILLA will enforce norms with occasional punishment; DUCK will mostly cooperate but may opportunistically defect once; CHICKEN is the most likely early exploiter but can be moved to cooperate by targeted punishment and rewards.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 29-round public goods game with 4 players, a marginal per-capita return of 1.8/4 = 0.45 per coin contributed, and a reward stage where 1 reward unit costs the giver 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Standard incentives: selfish rational play predicts zero contributions (private withdrawal of all 20), because each coin contributed returns less than one coin to the contributor. However, social preferences, reciprocity, and the availability of rewards typically produce positive initial contributions that often decline over time as cooperation is eroded or as players react to others' behavior. Rewarding can sustain cooperation early if cooperators use small rewards to reinforce high contributors; rewards are costly and therefore usually sparse. Uncertainties include individual heterogeneity (some players are more consistently cooperative or conditional cooperators), the degree to which players reciprocate rewards, and end-game effects (players anticipate the finite horizon and often reduce contributions near the last rounds). Given the four personas: CHICKEN looks most inclined to cooperate persistently, GORILLA is conditional and may reward reciprocally, DUCK is variable/medium cooperative, and SLOTH is slower to update and drifts toward freeriding over time. Expect moderate initial contributions, occasional small reciprocal rewards concentrated on visible cooperators, gradual decline in contributions, and a collapse toward low contributions in the last several rounds.

This is a finite (16-round) public-goods game. Each coin a player contributes costs them 1 private coin but adds 2 coins to the group pot; that pot is split evenly among the three players, so each contributed coin returns 2/3 of a coin to the contributor. Because 2/3 < 1, the one-shot incentive is to contribute 0; however, groups can raise total payoffs if players contribute. With repeated play and visible round-by-round summaries, we typically expect an initial phase of some positive contributions (from conditional cooperators or otherwise motivated players), followed by gradual erosion as free-riding is rewarded and, toward the end, an “endgame” collapse to low or zero contributions. Uncertainties include individual variation (some players may persist in contributing despite low immediate return), response sensitivity to peers’ behavior, and anchoring effects.

Using the provided personas as priors:
- SLOTH: relatively low cooperative tendency, high anchor/framing sensitivity — likely to start modest or low and adjust based on observed anchors.
- GORILLA: moderate/low giving tendency, medium anchor pull — likely to give modest amounts early, then drift downward.
- DUCK: relatively more framing-sensitive and somewhat more willing to give — likely to contribute relatively more early on but decline if peers defect.

Given those tendencies and the finite horizon, I predict modest cooperative contributions in early rounds, falling gradually and converging to near-zero by the late rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round every player effectively decides how much of 20 coins to leave in the shared pot as a contribution; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally among the four. Because the multiplication factor (1.8) is less than group size (4), each individual is privately better off by withdrawing (free-riding) while the group as a whole would earn more if everyone contributed more. Rewards are the only targeted interpersonal action: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins — this is a net cost to the group but can serve as a costly signal or reciprocity mechanism. Players do not have a group chat and receive limited feedback (they can see who rewarded them and their own round payoff / reward amounts), so social signaling via occasional rewards is especially important for building cooperation.

Given the personas:
- CHICKEN is the most cooperative and likely to contribute consistently high amounts and to send occasional rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- DUCK is moderately cooperative and responsive; will often contribute fairly high early but may gradually reduce contributions if cooperation appears unrewarded.
- SLOTH tends to contribute modestly but may increase if rewarded or drift toward moderate contributions.
- GORILLA is the most likely free-rider and stay low on contributions; occasional small adjustments are possible but large shifts are unlikely.

Because rewards are individually costly and reduce total group payoff, they will be used sparingly — primarily by CHICKEN (to reward high contributors) and sometimes by DUCK. Over 29 rounds we therefore expect CHICKEN to lead cooperative behavior, DUCK to follow then slowly decline if insufficiently reinforced, SLOTH to hover in the low-to-mid range, and GORILLA to remain a low contributor. Reward events will be few and selective (single-unit rewards are most likely), with a slightly higher chance of reward in early rounds to establish norms and one small end-game reciprocity.

This 3-player public goods game (6 rounds, endowment 20 each, multiplier 1.9 split equally) creates tension between individual incentives to free-ride and group incentives to contribute. Early rounds are uncertain: conditional cooperators may start moderately high but adjust when others free-ride; strongly pro-social or anchored players may stick to a declared level; low-engagement or opportunistic players often contribute little and benefit in the short run. Over six rounds we expect:
- GORILLA (high anchoring, generally cooperative) to start relatively high and try to steer the group by pledging a contribution; still responsive to peers' behavior.
- SLOTH (cautious/conditional) to contribute moderately, respond to observed payoffs, and try limited coordination.
- DUCK (low engagement, high anchor sensitivity) to contribute little early (free-riding), possibly nudged upward later by social pressure or explicit requests.
Messages will be sparse and primarily promises, complaints, or brief coordination attempts. Net payoffs each round (20 - own contribution + (1.9 * total contributions)/3) give visible feedback, so players can infer others' behavior and adjust.

game_explanation:
This is a 23-round online public goods game among four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the public pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribute = 0). The pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally. After redistribution, players may punish others: each punishment unit costs the punisher 1 coin and deducts 2 coins from the punished player's round payoff. Players see only round summaries (their net payoff, how much they spent punishing others, and how much punishment was deducted from them) and the corresponding numbers for the peers, but not who punished whom.

Incentives and likely dynamics:
- Free-riding (withdrawing 20) strictly yields higher immediate payoff if others contribute: private withdrawal plus share of the multiplied pot usually beats the individual share when you contribute. So unilateral defection is tempting.
- Punishment is costly but can deter repeated defection; punishers pay a cost and cannot identify who punished them later. Punishment tends to be used sparingly and strategically to influence behavior.
- With a known finite horizon (23 rounds) we expect cooperation to be easier early on (people may test and reciprocate) and to erode toward the end. Given the player priors: DUCK and GORILLA have higher purchase/coop tendencies and may cooperate early and punish free-riders; SLOTH and CHICKEN are more likely to defect initially, but repeated punishment may coax one or both into cooperating mid-game. In practice, expect:
  - Early rounds: mixed contributions (some cooperate, some defect) with modest punishment directed at defectors.
  - Middle rounds: if punishments are credible, movement toward full cooperation for several rounds.
  - Late rounds: cooperation likely unravels, culminating in widespread defection by the final rounds; punishment drops off as players anticipate final-round free-riding.

This 4-player public goods game gives each person a simple binary choice each round: keep all 20 coins (contribute 0) or leave them in the pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and shared equally, so cooperation raises group payoff but individual incentives favor keeping the money. Rewarding is costly to the sender (3 coins per unit) but benefits the target (4 coins per unit). Players cannot identify who rewarded them, and they know the game lasts six rounds.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' tendencies. A few players (here DUCK and sometimes SLOTH) are more likely to try cooperating to build a norm; others (GORILLA, CHICKEN) are more opportunistic and may test defection. Expect low-to-moderate cooperation early.
- Rewards will be used sparingly, mostly to reward cooperators rather than to directly punish defectors (because only positive transfers are available). When cooperation is observed, small mutual rewards (1 unit) between cooperators will appear occasionally to reinforce behavior.
- As the game approaches the final rounds, incentives to free-ride grow. Some players who were cooperating may defect late; others may maintain cooperation as a social preference. Rewarding will remain sparse; final-round behavior typically shifts toward defection.
- Chats will be occasional, concise appeals or comments used to encourage reciprocation or to comment on outcomes.

Transcript follows round-by-round predictions.
