game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with three players for 16 rounds. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a public pot; the pot is doubled and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish others (1 coin spent removes 2 coins from the target). Key incentives:
- Contributing helps the group: each coin contributed yields 2 coins to the pot and therefore a 2/3 coin return to each player (so personal return per contributed coin is 2/3 < 1), giving an individual incentive to free-ride.
- Punishment allows enforcement: contributors can deter low contributions by inflicting costly penalties on defectors, but punishing itself is costly.
- Uncertainty and private decisions mean players infer intentions from past behavior. Early rounds often reveal tendencies; punishment tends to be sparse but targeted when defection is perceived.
Given the personas:
- GORILLA tends toward strong anchoring and is relatively cooperative (likely to contribute high amounts and to enforce cooperation via occasional punishment).
- SLOTH is more conservative about costly commitments (more likely to test/defect early) but responsive to feedback/punishment.
- DUCK is somewhat sensitive to framing and anchors, often moderate and responsive to targeted punishment.
Overall, expect an initial mix of contributions with light punishment to discipline clear free-riders, then a generally cooperative plateau with occasional deviations and targeted, sparing punishment. In the last round, because future enforcement is impossible, modest end-game drops are possible.

game_explanation:
This PGG places each player in tension between private gain and the group return: each coin left in the shared pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split among 4 players, so each contributed coin returns 0.525 to the contributor but 2.1 to the group. That makes the individual incentive to withdraw (keep) coins strong (private return 1 > public return 0.525), while the group optimum is to leave coins in the pot. Expect contributions to be below the group optimum (and often well below full contribution).

Because players see only after-the-fact who punished or rewarded them, but cannot communicate, targeted sanctions and limited positive reciprocity are possible. Punishment is costly to the punisher (1 coin) and severe for the target (−3 coins per punishment unit), so in realistic play punishments are used sparingly and primarily to discipline clear free-riders. Rewards are cheap (cost 1, benefit 1) and used rarely to reinforce cooperation or reciprocate. Conditional cooperators (players who respond to others) will often start with moderate contributions; persistent free-riders invite occasional punishments, which can raise contributions later but also cause oscillations. In the final round punishments and rewards are less likely (end-game effect).

Given the provided personas: DUCK is likely to free-ride most; GORILLA leans toward lower contributions but can respond to sanctions; CHICKEN is a conditional cooperator and will punish occasional defection; SLOTH is moderately cooperative but not highly active in searching for strategies. I therefore predict moderate initial cooperation with DUCK low, some sparse punishment early to discipline DUCK and GORILLA, occasional small mutual rewards among cooperators, and some end-game decline in round 10. Below is a round-by-round transcript following these tendencies.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 29 known rounds. Each round each player effectively chooses how much of their 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (their contribution) versus withdraw for private use. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.45 to each contributor (1.8/4). Because 0.45 < 1, a single player always gets a lower private return from their own contribution than from keeping the coin — the classic private incentive to free-ride. However, group welfare is highest when players contribute more.

The punishment and reward stage introduces second-order incentives. Punishment can deter free-riding but is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) and expensive for the punished (4 coins lost per unit). Rewards also cost the giver (3 coins per unit) and benefit the receiver (+2 per unit). Players are identified as sources of punishments/rewards, so reputational enforcement is possible. Because the horizon is finite and known (29 rounds), standard endgame reasoning predicts cooperation will tend to unravel toward the final rounds as the incentive to punish or reward declines.

Uncertainties: players do not see others' current-round choices before choosing, so behavior is guided by beliefs about others' tendencies, prior rounds' signals (who punished/rewarded), and risk preferences. In many empirical PGGs, initial rounds show moderate cooperation, occasional targeted punishments of low contributors, then a gradual decline over time — often accelerating near the end. Punishments and rewards are used sparingly in real experiments; they pop up in response to perceived defection or to reinforce cooperation, but they are not extremely frequent. Given these mechanics and the four-player set, I expect early-to-mid moderate contributions, some targeted punishment of persistent low contributors (mainly towards the player(s) perceived to free-ride), intermittent small rewards for steady cooperators, and a slow drift toward lower contributions as the game progresses, culminating in low or zero contributions in the final rounds.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with three players each round receiving 20 coins. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 2 and split equally among the three players, so the individual marginal return on contributing is 2/3 (<1), making free-riding individually optimal absent social enforcement. However, the punishment stage (1 coin to send, removes 2 coins from the target) allows conditional cooperators to deter free-riding by imposing costs on low contributors. Expectations and observed histories will guide behavior: some players are predisposed to cooperate (Duck tends to give more), others are anchor-sensitive and may respond to perceived norms (Gorilla), and some are conditional reciprocators who search for stable strategy patterns (Sloth). Because punishment is anonymous, it is typically used sparingly and mostly against clear under-contributors; retaliation can occur but is costly and less frequent. Over repeated rounds we expect: initial moderate-to-high contributions; occasional defections by opportunistic players; sparse targeted punishments that sometimes restore cooperation; and a gradual breakdown of cooperation toward the final rounds as endgame effects emerge.

This is a repeated, finite public-goods game with 23 rounds. Each round players simultaneously decide whether to keep the 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave them in the group pot (contribute 20). The group pot is multiplied by 2.5 and shared equally; that creates a social incentive to contribute (collective benefit) but a private incentive to defect (keep 20 and still get a share of the public pot). Because the game is finite and players know the horizon (23 rounds), there's some incentive to defect toward the end; however, the ability to punish (pay 1 coin to deduct 2 from another) and to send messages supports conditional cooperation and norms enforcement earlier in the game.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players try cooperating to build mutual gains, with occasional one-shot defection(s).
- Punishment is used sparingly and targeted at visible defectors; cooperators punish defectors 1–2 units to signal norm enforcement.
- Defectors gain short-term advantage but may face punishment and switch back to cooperation. Retaliatory punishment can appear when a punished player responds, causing short cycles of defection and re-establishment of cooperation.
- Toward the very last round, some players may defect (end-game) because punishment cannot credibly be used after the final payoff; this tends to reduce contributions in the final round.

I predict moderate cooperation overall with occasional single-player defections and limited, targeted punishment. Chat is sparse and mostly exhortative or reactive.

After seeing contributions and payoffs, players can punish others: each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 12 coins. Players can send messages at any time, and punishment is anonymous (players see totals but not who punished them).

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds typically feature some positive contributions as players test each other and signal cooperation. Messages can encourage coordination.
- If a clear free-rider appears (very low contribution), others may use small punishments (1 unit) to enforce norms; punishments are usually sparse because they cost the punisher.
- After being punished, free-riders often increase contributions in subsequent rounds.
- In the final round, strategic incentives to free-ride are strongest (no future rounds to enforce), so contributions commonly drop; some players may punish low contributions even in the last round, but overall punishment frequency is low.
- Uncertainty: messages and prior tendencies can lead to modest variation — some players stick to consistent cooperating, others opportunistically defect.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players for 23 rounds. Each round every player faces the same binary choice: withdraw all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0 to the public pot) or leave all 20 in the public pot (contribute 20). The public pot is multiplied by 2.5 and divided equally, so an individual who defects (keeps 20) benefits if others contribute, while mutual contribution yields the highest group payoff but individual contributions are costly if others do not reciprocate. Because players see only round-level payoffs for themselves and peers (not the raw choices before deciding) and the horizon (23 rounds) is common knowledge, we should expect:

- Early rounds: some players try to signal cooperation (contribute 20) to build a norm, while others test the incentive to free-ride. Communication (sparse) can boost early coordination.
- Middle rounds: if cooperation is exploited (cooperators get lower net payoffs than free-riders), cooperation tends to collapse — players switch to withdrawing privately.
- Late rounds / endgame: toward the end, standard strategic logic predicts widespread defection; occasional attempts to re-start cooperation can appear but often fail without strong reciprocity.
- Given the provided personas, SLOTH is passive and likely to withdraw (free-ride) often; GORILLA and CHICKEN are more likely to try to coordinate and to send messages; DUCK is mixed and may follow social cues but will defect if exploited.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with an efficiency multiplier (2.1) that yields a group return less than 1 per contributor for each individual (2.1/4 = 0.525 < 1). That creates the classic tension: every coin kept privately yields 1 to the chooser, while every coin contributed yields 0.525 to that same chooser but 2.1 total to the group. So the individual incentive is to withdraw (free-ride), while the group incentive is to contribute.

Given the player priors, I expect patterned heterogeneity rather than identical play: one or two players (here DUCK and sometimes CHICKEN) lean toward higher contributions; SLOTH tends to withdraw a lot (free-ride); GORILLA varies but often contributes moderately when it sees cooperation. Rewarding is allowed but costly (1 coin per unit) and players reward sparingly in real games. So rewards will be occasional, small, and targeted at the consistent contributors (mostly DUCK). Over the ten rounds we should see moderate contributions early from cooperators, persistent low contributions from SLOTH, some responsiveness from GORILLA, and a gradual overall decline in contributions as endgame approaches — punctuated by occasional positive rewards aimed at sustaining or thanking high contributors.

So the individual incentive is to withdraw (free-ride), while the group incentive is to contribute.

Given the player priors, I expect patterned heterogeneity rather than identical play: one or two players (here DUCK and sometimes CHICKEN) lean toward higher contributions; SLOTH tends to withdraw a lot (free-ride); GORILLA varies but often contributes moderately when it sees cooperation. Rewarding is allowed but costly (1 coin per unit) and players reward sparingly in real games. So rewards will be occasional, small, and targeted at the consistent contributors (mostly DUCK). Over the ten rounds we should see moderate contributions early from cooperators, persistent low contributions from SLOTH, some responsiveness from GORILLA, and a gradual overall decline in contributions as endgame approaches — punctuated by occasional positive rewards aimed at sustaining or thanking high contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated 16-round public goods game with three players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20; the pot of contributions is doubled and split evenly among the three players. The private marginal return on one coin given to the public pot is 2/3 of a coin (because 2 is split among 3 players), which is less than 1, so individually it is always incentive-compatible to keep coins rather than give them. However, the group-level payoff is maximized when everyone contributes more.

Typical dynamics: many groups start with moderate contributions (some cooperation), then drift down as free-riding is individually profitable and players react to peers' observed contributions and payoffs. Without communication, players infer others' tendencies from past rounds and adjust: cooperative players may try to sustain contributions; those who see free-riders tend to reduce their own contributions. Near the final rounds, "end-game" effects make contributions especially likely to fall.

Uncertainties here: personalities matter — SLOTH is highly anchor-sensitive and may stick to simple heuristics (e.g., round-1 anchors), GORILLA is relatively pro-social and responsive to framing (may sustain cooperation for longer), DUCK is mixed and inclined to experiment/defect if it yields higher payoff. Because players see each round's contributions and payoffs, behavior will evolve through learning: if free-riding consistently yields higher net payoffs, contributions will tend to decline. Expect an initial moderate contribution profile, gradual decline, occasional short-lived attempts to raise contributions, and a likely drop toward low contributions by the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 23 rounds, four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player either leaves all 20 coins in the common pot (contribute 20) or withdraws all 20 for themselves (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally; after that players may spend costly punishment units (1 coin each) that remove 2 coins from targets. Messages are allowed but identity of punishers is not revealed in the summary.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds will test norms: cooperators (especially DUCK and GORILLA) are inclined to contribute to build the group return; SLOTH is slower to cooperate and may free-ride initially; CHICKEN is conditional and may switch based on others' behavior.
- Punishment is costly to the sender, so it will be used sparingly — mainly to deter persistent free-riding or as brief retaliation. Expect a few low-intensity punishments in early rounds to signal norms, which then raise cooperation.
- Mid-game tends to stabilize at higher contribution levels if punishments successfully discipline defectors. End-game (final few rounds) may see some defection as the shadow of the future weakens; the final round often has widespread defection.
- Messages will be sparse and used to threaten or call out behavior; players who punish may justify it in chat, while punished players may complain.

game_explanation:
With four players and six rounds, the main tension is between short-term private gain from withdrawing the whole endowment (defecting) and the longer-run benefit of mutual cooperation (leaving the 20 in the pot). Because each player's contribution is all-or-nothing (0 or 20), outcomes tend to polarize: cooperators either lose 20 immediately but share the multiplied pot, while defectors keep 20 and still get a share. The multiplication factor (1.6) gives each contributed 20 a return of 8 to each player (0.4 × 20 = 8), so a lone cooperator is heavily exploited. Rewarding is available but costly (3 to giver for 4 to receiver); it's useful to incentivize cooperators but is costly and anonymous to recipients, so it typically appears sparsely and often among players who already favor cooperation.

Given the provided personas:
- CHICKEN is relatively pro-social and likely to attempt cooperation early.
- DUCK may follow anchors and reciprocate social signals when they exist.
- GORILLA and SLOTH are more likely to prioritize private payoffs and defect, especially as the game progresses and the incentive to free-ride becomes clear.
- Because the game is finite (6 rounds), expect cooperation to break down over time, with a few early rounds showing mixed cooperation and occasional small reward gestures between cooperators.

I simulate a plausible unfolding: a tentative cooperative start (CHICKEN and DUCK contribute in rounds 1–2), small mutual rewards among cooperators, then a decline as defectors earn more and cooperators get exploited, leading to a cascade into full defection by the middle rounds and little rewarding in later rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, 23-round binary-contribution public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot (by withdrawing 0) or takes the full 20 privately (withdraws 20), so contributions are 0 or 20 per player. The total contributions are multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly. That creates a tension: if everyone contributes, everyone gets the highest group payoff (each 50). But for a given round, a single defector who takes 20 while others contribute earns more that round (e.g., 57.5 when three others contribute). Repeated interactions and messaging can create norms of cooperation, but temptation to defect and short-term incentives often produce occasional deviations.

Given the provided personas:
- SLOTH is talkative and tends toward cooperation and providing cues (will often support cooperation).
- DUCK is norm-sensitive and likely to advocate cooperation and respond to anchors/promises.
- GORILLA is less communicative and may test cooperative norms but can respond to explicit appeals.
- CHICKEN is pragmatic and sometimes defects to exploit cooperation; may justify or alternate.

I expect early rounds to show mixed cooperation (some free-riding), then an attempt at coordinated cooperation via messages, occasional one-off tests (by GORILLA or CHICKEN), and gradual convergence toward frequent cooperation after social reinforcement. Chats will be sparse and purposeful — short appeals, one apology/explanation after tests, and a few reminders.
reflection:
This is a repeated, 23-round binary-contribution public goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot (by withdrawing 0) or takes the full 20 privately (withdraws 20), so contributions are 0 or 20 per player. The total contributions are multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly. That creates a tension: if everyone contributes, everyone gets the highest group payoff (each 50). But for a given round, a single defector who takes 20 while others contribute earns more that round (e.g., 57.5 when three others contribute). Repeated interactions and messaging can create norms of cooperation, but temptation to defect and short-term incentives often produce occasional deviations.

Given the provided personas:
- SLOTH is talkative and tends toward cooperation and providing cues (will often support cooperation).
- DUCK is norm-sensitive and likely to advocate cooperation and respond to anchors/promises.
- GORILLA is less communicative and may test cooperative norms but can respond to explicit appeals.
- CHICKEN is pragmatic and sometimes defects to exploit cooperation; may justify or alternate.

I expect early rounds to show mixed cooperation (some free-riding), then an attempt at coordinated cooperation via messages, occasional one-off tests (by GORILLA or CHICKEN), and gradual convergence toward frequent cooperation after social reinforcement. Chats will be sparse and purposeful — short appeals, one apology/explanation after tests, and a few reminders.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with four players and six rounds. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot (cooperate) or withdraws the 20 for private use (defect). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each contributing player helps raise the per-player share (8 coins per contributor). That creates a tension: cooperating lowers your private balance that round but increases everyone’s share; defecting gains the private 20 plus the group share. Punishment and reward are available but costly: each unit sent costs the sender 3 coins; a punishment unit deducts 7 coins from its target, a reward unit adds 4 coins to its target. Because punishments are much stronger in effect than their cost to the sender, cooperators can deter defectors, but sending sanctions is itself costly. Messages are visible and can shape expectations, but punishers remain anonymous in the summaries.

Likely dynamics: early rounds often feature attempts at cooperation by some players and sparse targeted sanctions to discourage free-riding. Players who are repeatedly punished may switch to cooperation briefly or continue defecting if punishments are too rare to offset private gains. Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce cooperation. As the game approaches the final rounds, incentives to defect strengthen (endgame effect), so cooperation often decreases. Because punishments are costly, we expect only a few targeted punishment actions each round rather than widespread sanctioning.

game_explanation:
This is a one-shot repeated public-goods game over 6 rounds with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round every player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Because 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 < 1, any single coin gives the contributor less private return than keeping it, so the static incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Communication and costly punishment (4 coins paid by the punisher to remove 12 coins from a target per punishment unit) are the only enforcement tools.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show some cooperative coordination through chat: one or two players propose a cooperative target (e.g., ~10–15 coins). Some players follow; some do not.
- Punishment is used sparingly to discipline clear free-riders because punishment is costly. Expect 0–1 punishment events early unless someone clearly defects; if punishment is effective, the punished player tends to increase contributions in subsequent rounds.
- Because punishment is anonymous (players see amounts deducted but not who punished), retaliation is diffuse: a punished player may respond by increasing contributions, or by punishing others later (not necessarily the original punisher).
- In later rounds (especially round 6) the “end-game” temptation to free-ride usually increases, and punishment tends to decrease since there is no future to enforce. So contributions often fall in the final round.

Below I simulate a plausible transcript consistent with these tendencies: a bit of coordination, a first-round punishment for a low contributor, recovery in rounds 2–3, a sharp defection in round 4 that provokes punishment, brief retaliation in round 5, and unraveling in round 6.

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game is a short (6-round) repeated setting with binary contribution choices (either contribute 20 or withdraw 20). The public pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each contributing player increases the per-player share by 8 for each additional contributor. With costly punishment and modest rewards available, we expect social enforcement to matter early: cooperators may signal and punish defectors to encourage contribution, and others may switch to cooperation to avoid costly punishments. Rewards are slightly pro-social (cost 3 to giver, +4 to receiver) and often used sparingly to reinforce cooperation; punishments are costly to the group (cost 3 to punisher, -7 to target) and are used selectively to discipline persistent free-riders or to signal disapproval.

Given the short horizon (6 rounds), typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds: talk and a mix of cooperation/defection. A cooperative player who is exploited may use punishment/rewards to shape behavior.
- Middle rounds: if punishment/reward successfully enforces norms, cooperation may increase and be reinforced with occasional rewards.
- Endgame (final rounds): incentives to free-ride grow because future retaliation is limited; cooperation often unravels somewhat in the last round(s). Punishments may still be used for norm signaling or spite, but are costlier when there's no future benefit.

I will simulate a plausible path consistent with these tendencies and the provided player priors (SLOTH more likely to free-ride or be slow to change, DUCK more proactive/cooperative, GORILLA responsive to norms, CHICKEN somewhat unpredictable). Chat is sparse and purposeful; punish/reward actions are fairly sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player has 20 coins and must either keep them (contribute 0) or put them all (contribute 20) into a pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players. That means the group benefit from each contributing player is large (each 20 contributed becomes 124 total, yielding +12.4 to every player), but contributors forfeit their private 20 that round. In one-shot logic a self-interested player should free-ride (contribute 0), because a single contribution increases everyone’s share but costs the contributor more in private money than the individual benefit they receive from their own contribution. Over repeated rounds with visible round-by-round payoffs and sparse messaging, we typically see some initial cooperation (pledges or conditional cooperation), followed by strategic responses: contributors who are exploited tend to reduce contributions; free-riders often keep defecting because they get higher immediate payoffs; occasional coordination and “return to cooperation” can occur when enough players promise to rejoin. The final round usually shows a drop in contributions (end-game effect) because there is no future punishment or reciprocity incentive.

Given the provided personas:
- Some players (e.g., SLOTH, RABBIT, DUCK, CHICK) are moderately or strongly inclined to cooperate initially.
- Others (OWL, PARROT, MOOSE, CHICKEN) are less likely to contribute consistently or are more likely to defect for payoff reasons.
- Some players (MOOSE, RABBIT) are more likely to try to coordinate through messages; others will respond by exploiting cooperation if it benefits them.

Expectations: early rounds will show mixed cooperation (~3–6 contributors), mid-game may dip as free-riding is tempting, occasional rebounds if players coordinate with messages, and a sharp drop on round 6.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round each player's choice is binary (contribute 20 or withdraw 20), so contributors raise the shared pot while withdrawers (free-riders) keep 20 privately. The group total is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so individual incentives favor withholding (you get the full 20 plus a share of the public return) while group efficiency favors full contribution. Because players do not see others' choices before deciding and do not know how many rounds remain, early rounds usually involve testing and signaling: a few players may contribute to signal cooperation, some will free-ride to exploit contributors, and a small number of reward actions (positive transfers) can be used to encourage contributors or welcome new cooperators. Rewards are costly (2 coins per unit to sender, +2 to recipient), so they are used sparingly — mainly to thank or reinforce cooperative behavior, not to punish. Over rounds we expect a mix: early partial cooperation, gradual conditional increases if contributors are recognized/rewarded, and relatively sparse chat (short messages announcing intentions or thanking others).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round players choose either to keep the full 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or to leave the whole 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20). The group pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so contributors lower their private take but increase everyone's share. After payoffs are realized, players can transfer (reward) coins to others at a 1:1 transfer that costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins per unit. Rewards thus re-allocate payoffs but do not change total group earnings. Players do not know the total number of rounds, so they may experiment, test cooperation, and try to establish norms using both messages and small rewards.

Likely unfolding: early rounds will be exploratory. Some players (conditional cooperators) will try contributing to see if others reciprocate; others (short-term self-interested players) will free-ride early. Because rewards are transfers (not costly punishment), they are most useful for encouraging cooperators rather than deterring defectors. Over rounds we therefore expect cooperation to decline as free-riders gain larger immediate returns unless a stable norm is established by coordinated rewarding. Chats will be few and focused (promises, short calls for cooperation, brief thanks or complaints). Rewards will be sparse and mostly directed to players who contributed; senders often reward cooperators to signal approval or to prop up a cooperative partner.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game where each player must either contribute all 20 of their private coins to the group pot or keep them. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and then split evenly among all 10 players. After contributions are revealed, players may spend coins to punish others (each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and subtracts 2 coins from the target). Players see who punished them and get a round-by-round summary.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often feature conditional cooperation: many players will try contributing, while a few test by free-riding. Visible punishments for free-riding tend to increase contribution in subsequent rounds because they make defection costly.
- Punishment is typically sparse and modest because it has a cost; it is used mainly to deter repeated defectors or express retaliation.
- As the game approaches the final round, the incentive to punish or to cooperate declines (end-game effect). Expect more free-riding in the last round and fewer punishments, though some spiteful or norm-enforcing punishments may appear even at the end.
- Personas suggest a majority willing to contribute (PARROT, DOG, CHICK, MOOSE often cooperate), while a few (RABBIT, DUCK, OWL sometimes) are more likely to test by defecting. Players who are punished (like GORILLA or RABBIT in early rounds) are likely to switch to contributing in later rounds to avoid further punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 6-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20), a large multiplier (6.2) and the ability to punish after seeing contributions. With ten players, full cooperation yields very large per-player returns; however, the one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because you keep your 20 while still getting a share of the multiplied pot. Over rounds, punishments can sustain cooperation by making defection costly, but punishment itself is costly to the punisher, so it tends to be used sparingly and mostly to target persistent or salient defectors.

Likely dynamics here:
- Round 1: players are uncertain and "test the waters." We expect a mix of contributors and defectors as people probe others' tendencies. A few cooperators may punish immediately to signal that defection is costly, but punishment will be limited.
- Middle rounds (2–4): players respond to prior actions. Those punished often switch to contributing; persistent defectors may be targeted by a small number of punishers. Cooperation tends to rise if punishment is used credibly but can wobble if someone exploits the group.
- Late rounds (5–6): forward-looking incentives fade. Some players may defect in the final round(s) since future retaliation is impossible; punishment becomes mostly expressive and therefore less frequent. Overall, we expect cooperation to increase after early punishments, with occasional opportunistic defections and sparse punishment responses.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round repeated public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone privately gets 20 coins and chooses either to contribute all 20 to the group pot or to keep 20. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split evenly across the 10 players. Socially, everyone is best off if all contribute every round (200 contributed → each gets 124 per round). Individually, however, contributing 20 yields a personal return of only 12.4 (0.62 × 20) — a net personal loss of 7.6 relative to keeping the 20 — so the dominant one-shot incentive is to free-ride.

Because the game repeats for 6 rounds, two forces compete: (1) conditional cooperation and social preferences can sustain contributions early if players coordinate or trust each other; (2) exploitation by free-riders reduces cooperators’ payoffs and tends to erode cooperation over time, especially near the end (final-round defection). Chat allows coordination attempts; players with high anchor-sensitivity and messaging tendency (e.g., OWL, DOG, RABBIT, SLOTH) are likely to try to lead or follow cooperative proposals early. Players with lower inclination to search or anchor may stick to free-riding. Expect an initial attempt at coordination with some contributors, followed by falling contributions as defectors get higher payoffs, occasional brief revivals due to appeals, and low contributions in the final round.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with voluntary contributions and optional peer punishment/reward after contributions are revealed. Each player gets 20 coins each round and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally among six players, so each coin contributed returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to each player. Because 0.633 < 1, a single player maximizes immediate private payoff by contributing 0 (free-riding), but the group maximizes total welfare if everyone contributes 20.

The punishment technology is powerful: each punishment unit costs the punisher 3 coins and subtracts 12 coins from the target. Rewards cost 3 to the sender and add 2 to the receiver. Because punishments are highly costly to recipients relative to cost to senders, even a few punishment actions can strongly deter low contributions; rewards are a weaker but positive signal.

Given the player personas, I expect an initial mix of moderate contributions with a few low contributions (especially from more self-interested or low-search personas), followed by a small number of punishments targeted at clear low contributors. Those sanctions should raise cooperation in subsequent rounds. Rewards will appear sparsely to reinforce high contributors. Once cooperation norms form (many players contributing near 20), the group mostly sustains high contributions with only occasional small deviations and sparse punishment/reward follow-ups. Chat will be sparse—short requests, reminders, thanks, or brief apologies.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 7 players, a binary contribution choice each round (contribute all 20 coins to the public pot or withdraw them all), a public-pot multiplier of 2.6 and equal sharing, and a cheap, identity-visible reward stage (1 reward unit costs 2 coins for the sender and gives 2 coins to the receiver). Players do not know how many rounds the game will last, so early rounds tend to be testing rounds: some players will try cooperating to see whether others reciprocate, while others will free-ride to exploit the returns. Because the multiplier (2.6) yields a return per contributed coin of 2.6/7 ≈ 0.371 per player share, contributing is socially efficient but individually costly: contributors typically get less than withdrawers unless almost everyone contributes. That creates a strong temptation to free-ride. Rewards can be used to sustain cooperation by transferring coin directly to cooperators, but rewards are costly to the sender and transfer only between players (they don't create extra aggregate wealth). I expect initial rounds to show some cooperation (curiosity / social preferences from a few players), followed by a decline as free-riders earn higher immediate payoffs; a few players (especially CHICKEN, who appears persistent) will keep attempting cooperation and will receive occasional rewards from sympathetically-minded players. Chat will be sparse and used to thank or to accuse defectors. Rewards will be used sparingly and targeted mainly at contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with voluntary contributions each round and an optional costly reward stage. Early rounds typically show moderate-to-high contributions as players test cooperation; some will try to build a pro-social norm (by contributing and rewarding others), while others test for free-riding. Because rewards are costly (3 coins to give 1 reward unit that gives 2 to the target), players will use them sparingly and mainly to reinforce above-average contributors or to encourage conditional cooperators. Uncertainty about the total number of rounds and the presence of identifiable reward sources encourages reputation-building: active rewarders (who both contribute and give rewards) will tend to sustain cooperation, while persistent free-riders will be starved of rewards and face social messages. Expect DUCK to be a reliable high contributor, GORILLA and DOG to be conditional cooperators who also reward to sustain cooperation, CHICKEN to be sensitive to framing and move with the group, PARROT to fluctuate and sometimes free-ride, and SLOTH to contribute relatively less and act slowly. Chats will be sparse and targeted; rewards will be selective and limited in units.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players and binary contribution choices each round (contribute all 20 or withdraw all 20). When many contribute, the multiplied public pot gives everyone a larger shared return; when more withdraw, defectors earn a larger private payoff. Punishment and reward are available but costly to the sender: each punishment unit costs the sender 2 and removes 3 from the target; each reward unit costs the sender 2 and gives the target +2. Players do not know the total number of rounds, so long-run strategic considerations are uncertain.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds will include testing: some players announce intentions and a mix of cooperation and defection appears.
- Conditional cooperators (who respond to peers' behavior) will often switch toward others' average behavior. Those who benefited from defecting may continue to defect.
- Punishment and reward appear sparsely and strategically: cooperators may punish obvious free-riders, and some players will reward cooperators. Because punishment is costly and often lowers the punisher's payoff relative to defectors, punishment tends to be limited and used primarily as a deterrent rather than frequent retaliation.
- Over rounds we expect partial cooperation to emerge sometimes, then erode if cooperation is costly to maintain via punishment. Chat will be sparse and used to signal intentions or call out behavior.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game pits short-term temptation to withdraw coins against group benefits from leaving coins in the shared pot. Each player effectively chooses between contributing the full 20 coins (cooperate) or withdrawing them privately (defect). The pot return (2.6× total contributions) is split equally, so when many contribute the per-person share can exceed the private 20, encouraging cooperation. But because the private benefit of defecting (keeping 20 plus the shared return) can exceed the cooperative payoff, some players will test free-riding. Punishment (costly to the sender, harsher to the target) and reward (costly and beneficial) allow social enforcement: cooperators can punish repeat defectors or reward steady contributors, nudging behavior toward cooperation over rounds.

Key uncertainties:
- Players do not know the total number of rounds, so "end-game" unraveling is less certain.
- Different personas make some players more likely to experiment, punish, or switch strategies. Overall I predict mixed initial behavior with modest punishment early, increasing cooperation as defectors are sanctioned, and occasional defections later (tests or temptations). Chat will be sparse, usually short reproaches or thanks.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, CHICK, RABBIT, OWL </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public-goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and costly punishment. Individually, contributing costs 20 but yields only 12.4 back to the contributor (20*6.2/10 = 12.4), so free-riding (contributing 0) strictly dominates if players only care about immediate payoff. However, because players can punish others (inflicting -2 per punishment unit at a cost of -2 to the punisher per unit) and the game lasts 6 rounds, we expect social enforcement to shape behavior:

- Early rounds: mixed behavior is likely. Some socially inclined players (high "purchase-yes" tendency or conformity) will contribute to signal cooperation; others will test free-riding. Early punishments will target clear free-riders and should raise cooperation in subsequent rounds.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is applied, cooperation tends to increase as players internalize norms and avoid costly fines. Punishment will be sparse but targeted.
- Final rounds: backward induction predicts more free-riding as future retaliation loses value, so defections become more frequent in rounds 5–6. Some players still punish in the last round out of fairness or spite, but punishment intensity typically falls.

Uncertainties: individual heterogeneity (some players are more likely to be conditional cooperators or persistent defectors), occasional "mistake" contributions, and how many players are willing to pay to punish in the final round. I predict an initial round with a majority of contributors but a few defectors; punishment will be targeted and modest, producing near-universal cooperation in middle rounds, with some relapse in rounds 5–6 and light punishment still applied.

game_explanation:
This will be a repeated public-goods game among six players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round every player privately decides how many of 20 coins to contribute to a multiplier pot (sum of contributions × 3.8, split equally). Because each coin yields 3.8/6 ≈ 0.63 back to its donor, the dominant private incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride), while the group optimum is full contribution. With punishment (cost 3 per unit, -12 per unit to the target) and reward (cost 3 per unit, +2 per unit to the target) available after seeing contributions, we should expect:

- Initial contributions will be mixed/moderate as players have different priors (some more cooperative, some more likely to defect).
- Punishment will be used sparingly but strategically to deter repeated free-riding — typically 1-unit punishments from cooperative or enforcement-minded players when someone contributes very little.
- Rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce higher contributions or to thank a player who increased contributions after a sanction.
- Chat is sparse and purposeful: short appeals to cooperate, apologies after being punished, occasional praise.
- Over time contributions may drift downward if enforcement is weak; with occasional targeted punishment and occasional rewarding, cooperation can be partially sustained.
- Players do not know the number of rounds; this uncertainty tends to sustain cooperation longer than a known final round would, but end-game decline is still likely.

I simulate the likely path through rounds 1–26 below: for each round I give a short explanation, any chat messages, the contribution vector (in the order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT), the punish/reward actions that actually occurred that round (tuples (source, target, unit); positive = reward unit, negative = punishment unit), and then the round summary as seen by players (net payoff and punish/reward totals). I show payoffs rounded to two decimals. Calculations: each player's base payoff = (20 - contribution) + (3.8 * total contributions / 6). Then adjustments: each unit sent costs the sender 3 coins; each reward unit received adds 2 coins to the recipient; each punishment unit received deducts 12 coins from the recipient.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated (6-round) public goods game with 10 players. Each round players choose whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it; contributed coins go into a pot multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all players. The group optimum is for everyone to contribute (big payoff), but individually there is an incentive to free-ride: if many others contribute, a defector keeps their 20 and still gets the shared return, earning more than a contributor. Typical dynamics: some players will try to coordinate contributions through sparse chat, cooperative types may contribute repeatedly, others will defect when they see defectors earn more, and overall contributions often decline over rounds (end-game effect). Uncertainty comes from not observing choices before deciding and from players' varying social preferences: some will continue contributing out of principle (or to try to induce reciprocity), while others will exploit contributions or switch to defection as the game progresses. Messages can partially coordinate behavior, but because defection often yields higher immediate payoff when there are contributors, sustaining full cooperation is fragile.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round players privately choose to contribute all (20) or none (0) of their endowment to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 6.2 and divided equally. Because contributions are all-or-nothing and multiplied, full cooperation (everyone contributes) maximizes group payoff, but an individual who defects earns more that round if others contribute. Punishment is available but costly (2 coins per punishment unit to sender, reduces 2 coins from the target). Messages can be sent at any time and punishers are observable after actions.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players will initially contribute, while a minority test free-riding. Contributors may use small punishments to signal that defection will be punished.
- Middle rounds: after a few punishments, conditional cooperators will switch to contributing; cooperation tends to stabilize if punishment deters free-riding.
- Final round: with no future to incentivize, some players will opportunistically defect; punishments become rarer (costly spite) but may still occur by those who prefer norm enforcement.
- Punishments will be sparse and targeted (1 unit) rather than widespread, and chat will be used sparingly to call out defectors or explain actions.

The transcript below follows these expectations: initial partial cooperation, selective punishment to enforce norms, convergence toward full cooperation, a handful of opportunistic defections in later rounds, and limited but visible punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 identifiable players, six rounds, and costly punishment. With contributions restricted to all-or-nothing (0 or 20), incentives are simple: contributing 20 reduces your private holding but increases the group pot; defecting (contributing 0) keeps your private coins while still reaping the shared return. The pot multiplier (6.2) is high enough that full cooperation is collectively profitable: every 20 contributed returns 20*6.2/10 = 12.4 to each player (so each contributor increases every player’s payoff by 12.4), but individually a contributor loses 20 in private funds and only gets 12.4 back — a net private loss from contributing if others do not also contribute. That creates the usual tension: cooperation is socially optimal but individually costly.

Punishment lets cooperators impose costs on defectors at a personal cost; because punishment is costly (2 per unit sent) and only reduces the target by 2, players will use it sparingly and typically only to deter repeated free-riding or to signal norms. Early rounds often show moderate cooperation that is enforced by a few punishments targeted at clear defectors. Over rounds players learn others’ tendencies: punished defectors often switch to contributing, persistent defectors attract repeated punishment, and final-round incentives encourage more free-riding (because there is no future punishment payoff), so cooperation can drop in round 6.

Given the player priors (some players are more inclined to cooperate and to use sanctions, some less), I predict initial mixed cooperation with a cluster of punishments targeted at early free-riders, gradual improvement in cooperation for most players, occasional stubborn defectors who get singled out, near-universal cooperation for at least one round, and a modest collapse of cooperation in the final round. Chat will be sparse and purposeful (requests to contribute, brief reprimands or thanks). Punishment will be used but not excessively — mostly single-unit punishments directed at repeat defectors.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with 10 players, 20-coin endowments each round, and a high multiplier (6.2) on the group pot. Each 20-coin contribution increases the group pot by 20, which after multiplication and equal split returns 12.4 coins to each player (per contributor). That means any single player's contribution reduces their private holding by 20 but increases every player's share by 12.4/1 contributor-worth—so an individual who contributes loses 7.6 coins relative to keeping their 20 if everyone else's behavior is fixed. In game-theoretic terms: contributing is socially efficient if many do it (everyone would be better off if all contributed), but individually dominated (each has an incentive to free-ride).

Typical unfolding and uncertainties:
- Round 1: Without prior history, a mix of idealists and conditional cooperators will contribute; others will defect. Early contributors (who expect reciprocity) may be exploited by free-riders.
- Middle rounds (2–4): Players observe round payoffs. Since defectors earn strictly more in mixed groups, many will reduce contributions after seeing they were exploited. A small core of persistent cooperators may remain; occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation (through messages or repeated contributions) are possible.
- Endgame (round 6): Standard backward-induction and end-game effects tend to push toward universal defection (all keep 20) in the final round. Sparse chat can influence a few conditional cooperators but rarely reverses the downward trend in large groups.

Personas as priors:
- RABBIT and SLOTH show high “purchase/yes” tendencies and are more likely to contribute initially and sometimes persist.
- GORILLA and DUCK show tendencies to avoid contributing (very low purchase-yes for GORILLA).
- PARROT, MOOSE, CHICK show medium–high framing sensitivity and may respond to appeals or try to coordinate.
- DOG, OWL, CHICKEN are mixed or more cautious; they may contribute occasionally but will often defect if exploited.

Given these tendencies, I predict an initial moderate level of cooperation that declines over rounds, with a small number of persistent contributors (especially RABBIT), a few brief coordination attempts via chat (PARROT, RABBIT, CHICK), and full defection in round 6.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated, anonymous public goods game with a binary contribution choice each round (contribute all 20 or withdraw all 20). The private incentive each round is to withdraw (defect): keeping 20 plus a share of the multiplied pot typically yields a higher immediate payout than contributing, so unconditional defection is individually attractive. However, the group payoff rises when more players contribute (the pot is multiplied by 2.6 and shared), and the reward stage lets players transfer payoffs to signal approval or punish (costly to the sender, beneficial to the recipient). Because players do not know how many rounds remain, small, targeted rewards can sustain cooperation by rewarding contributors and thereby changing expectations about future payouts. Expect a mix of initial free-riding and conditional cooperation: a handful of players (more cooperative personas) will contribute early; contributors will reward each other sparsely to build reciprocity; some defectors will switch to contribution after being excluded from rewards or after seeing rewards for others. Over rounds cooperation should gradually increase, with rewards concentrated on those who switch or reliably contribute. Chats will be sparse and used mainly for short commitments, thanks, or reproach.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a typical repeated public-goods game with an added voluntary reward stage. Each round, players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied (×3.8) and split equally. Individually, contributing reduces your private holdings but increases the group pot — the private return per coin contributed is 3.8/6 ≈ 0.63, so contributing is socially efficient but privately costly. After contributions are revealed, players may pay 3 coins per reward unit to give 2 coins to another player. Rewards therefore transfer gains to recipients but are costly to the sender; they are used to incentivize cooperators (or reciprocate helpful behavior).

Given the six player personas, I expect:
- A small core of relatively cooperative players (DOG, PARROT, CHICKEN) will start by contributing at moderate-to-high levels and use rewards sparingly to sustain cooperation.
- Some players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK) are less likely to buy into costly cooperation; SLOTH and GORILLA are more persistent low contributors, while DUCK is a weak conditional cooperator and may contribute a modest amount.
- Rewards will be sparse and targeted to high contributors; most rounds have only 1–3 reward edges. Because players don't know the horizon, cooperation tends to be stable early and may slowly decay if low contributors persist and are not effectively incentivized.
- Chat will be used rarely and instrumentally (requests, short explanations, thanks).

Uncertainties: specific numeric contributions are private and depend on beliefs about others' behavior. Rewarding is costly, so players only reward when they expect it will positively influence future behavior or to reciprocate prior rewards. Because players can identify who rewarded them, small reciprocal cycles can emerge (A rewards B, B rewards C, C rewards A), sustaining cooperation for a while.

I will simulate 26 rounds. I use a small set of realistic contribution profiles and two simple reward patterns (a three-way reciprocal loop in relatively cooperative rounds, and a two-edge rewarding pattern when cooperation is lower). Chat appears rarely and reflects plausible social nudges.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This public goods game is a repeated interaction with seven players. Each round everyone faces a binary choice: keep the 20 coins privately (defect), or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly, so an individual who contributes loses 20 privately but increases everyone’s payout by 2.6*20/7 ≈ 7.43 each — a private loss but a group benefit. Without enforcement, free-riding (keeping coins) dominates individually.

Because players can identify punishers/rewarders and may send messages, we expect some players to try to sustain cooperation by punishing persistent free-riders and occasionally rewarding cooperators. Punishment is costly (2 to punisher, -3 to target), so it will be used sparingly and typically only when defectors persist or are lone free-riders. Rewards are also used sparingly (2 cost, +2 target) to encourage cooperation. Early rounds will show testing behavior: a few cooperators versus several defectors, followed by selective punishment leading some defectors to switch. Over rounds, expect oscillations: occasional defections by risk-taking players (who see higher short-run payoffs), followed by targeted punishment from cooperators, and occasional mutual rewarding when most cooperate. Chat will be sparse and purposeful (announcements of intent, calls to punish or acknowledgments).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players decide whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or keep it (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players, so individual incentives favor defection (you keep 20 plus get a share of the pot) while group payoffs maximize if everyone contributes. Punishment lets players reduce others' payoffs at a cost to themselves; because punishment is costly and identifiable, it will be used sparingly and strategically — often by cooperators to deter defectors or by retaliators in response to perceived unfairness.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some mix of cooperation and defection as players probe others. Contributors may punish obvious defectors once or twice to signal norms.
- Middle rounds: punishments can stabilize higher cooperation, but only if punishers accept personal costs. If punishers see little effect on defectors' relative earnings, they may reduce punishment over time.
- Final round: strategic motive to punish (to sustain future cooperation) disappears; expect more defection. Punishments in the last round often reflect spite or fairness preferences rather than enforcement.

Given the ten player personas, expect a mix: a majority of players will contribute in the first few rounds (people with higher "purchase yes" and prosocial cues), a small set of persistent defectors (very low purchase-yes or low cooperativeness), and sparse, targeted punishment events primarily from contributors. Chat will be sparse and mostly about requests to cooperate or short protest messages.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with seven identified players who choose (each round) whether to leave the full 20 coins in the group pot (contribute 20) or withdraw them (contribute 0). The group pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly, so an individual who contributes reduces their private payoff that round but raises the public share for everyone. After payoffs from the public pot are distributed, players can send costly positive rewards: each reward unit costs the source 2 coins and gives the target 2 coins. Players do not know the total number of rounds, so they have an incentive to build a reputation early; they can also see the round summaries afterward, which reveal net payoffs and who rewarded whom. Because rewards are costly and purely positive, we should expect (1) relatively sparse rewarding, usually targeted at cooperators; (2) some early variation as players test who cooperates; (3) gradual emergence of reciprocal rewarding among cooperators; and (4) a few persistent defectors who briefly benefit from high private payoffs but may be socially isolated (i.e., rewarded less). Chat will be sparse and used mainly to signal intentions or thank others.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly peer sanctions (punishments) and costly peer incentives (rewards). Each player gets 20 coins and decides how many to contribute to the group. The group's sum is multiplied by 3.8 then split equally — so each coin contributed returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to the contributor via the public pot (less than 1), creating a private incentive to free-ride while creating a group-level incentive to contribute. Punishment is harsh (each unit costs the sender 3 and deducts 12 from the target) while rewards are cheaper to target (each unit costs the sender 3 and gives only +2 to the target). Because players cannot see contemporaneous choices, reputational signaling comes through the punishment/reward stage and chat messages; players also don't know how many rounds remain, so endgame effects are muted.

Likely unfolding (high-level dynamics):
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation (around 10–12) as players test each other.
- Occasional defectors appear; others punish sparingly (1 unit punishments) to enforce norms. Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce contributors.
- Punishments tend to be one-unit actions (because they're costly to sender but strongly punitive to recipient). Rewards also tend to be single units.
- Over many rounds the group tends to settle into moderate-high cooperation punctuated by occasional defections and targeted punishments. Chat is sparse and used for brief appeals or acknowledgements.
- Because players can see who punished/rewarded them, targeted punishments temporarily reduce future defections from those recipients.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round players choose whether to leave the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or to withdraw them for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so a sole defector gains relative advantage in the short run, but punishments (costly to send, more costly to the target) and rewards (costly to send, beneficial to the recipient) can shift incentives toward cooperation. Because players can identify who punished/rewarded them and players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect:

- Early uncertainty: some players may defect to test whether they can exploit others without retaliation.
- Enforcement: norm-followers (high framing/anchor sensitivity avatars like CHICKEN, MOOSE) will punish defectors sparingly, which will often induce switching to cooperation in subsequent rounds.
- Rewards remain occasional and modest — used to reinforce cooperators but less common than punishment.
- Over rounds, cooperation should increase and stabilize, with occasional defections met by targeted punishments. Chat messages will be sparse and typically appeal to norms or question defections.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and must choose 0 or 20 to put into a common pot. Every coin contributed is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally across all 10 players. Individually, contributing is dominated (you lose 20 but your own contribution returns only 12.4 to you), so the incentive is to free-ride. However, group payoffs are much higher when many contribute, and some players (conditional cooperators, socially motivated types, or those persuaded by messages) may start by contributing. Expect initial mixed cooperation (some respond to appeals), then a decline as players see defectors earning more within rounds. A few players may keep contributing out of consistency or norm-following, but by the final rounds most will anticipate widespread defection and withhold contributions. Chat will be sparse — occasional calls to cooperate or short explanations for switching.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with seven players, binary contribution choices each round (either contribute the whole 20 to the shared pot or withdraw 20 for private use), and an after-stage for costly punishment and costly reward. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally; so cooperation increases the group pie but individual incentives to withdraw (defect) remain attractive unless social enforcement (punishment, reputation via messages, or targeted rewards) raises the personal cost of defection or rewards cooperation.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds will test norms. Some players with prosocial priors (SLOTH, PARROT, MOOSE) tend to cooperate; players with lower cooperative priors (DUCK, possibly DOG) are more likely to defect or to “test” the group.
- When someone defects, a small set of cooperators will punish sparingly (to signal norm enforcement) rather than punish heavily. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it will be used selectively.
- Rewards are used infrequently but strategically to reinforce cooperation and to "pay back" players who switch from defection to cooperation.
- Messages will be sparse and purposeful (requests to cooperate, brief admonishments or thanks) because players see explicit identification for punish/ reward and can adjust strategies in subsequent rounds.
- Because no one knows the total number of rounds, cooperation tends to be sustained if punishments are consistent but will be intermittently tested by occasional defections.

Below is a round-by-round transcript, showing the predicted contributions, sparse chat, punishment/reward actions (only non-zero edges), and the summary information that each player would see.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly positive rewards. Players start with 20 coins per round and choose contributions (0–20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 3.8, and split equally; so each player gains from others' contributions but pays a private cost. Because the multiplier per player (3.8/6 ≈ 0.6333) is less than 1, the private incentive is to contribute zero, but group payoff is maximized at full contribution. The reward stage lets players spend 3 coins to give 2 coins to another — a way to reinforce cooperation (or reward high contributors) without direct punishment.

Given the personas:
- GORILLA tends to prefer cooperation and may try to lead by contributing high amounts and occasionally reward cooperators.
- DUCK and CHICKEN are moderately cooperative and sensitive to framing; they may respond to rewards and reciprocate.
- DOG, PARROT, and SLOTH vary: SLOTH is slow-to-act and lower contributing early but may increase if others reward; PARROT and DOG are moderate contributors and may use occasional rewards.
- Rewards will be sparse and strategic: used to encourage steady cooperators (small 1–2 unit gifts). Early rounds will see experimentation; if rewards reinforce contributions, contributions will gradually rise toward full cooperation by later rounds.

Now the simulated transcript from round 1 through round 26 follows. Each round begins with a short explanation of likely behavior, then contributions, any chat, the reward actions (if any), and the summary information shown to players (net payoff, reward spent, reward received, and identities for received rewards). Values are shown to two decimal places.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with a binary contribution choice each round (contribute the full 20 or withdraw it). The private temptation is strong: one player's 20 contributed returns only 2.6*20/7 ≈ 7.43 to themselves, so contributors typically lose relative to withdrawers unless they receive rewards from others. Rewards are costly to the giver (2 coins per unit) and give an equal benefit to the recipient (2 coins), so rewards don't change group-level payoffs but can reallocate payoffs and sustain cooperation by compensating contributors.

Given the player personas, expect a mix of behaviors:
- Some players (MOOSE, DOG, PARROT) show traits that make them somewhat more likely to try cooperating or to persist when they believe it's a social norm.
- Others (SLOTH, CHICKEN, DUCK) tend to be more passive or inclined to withdraw/private-payoff focus.
- GORILLA and PARROT are plausible conditional cooperators — they may occasionally contribute or reward to support cooperation.

Typical dynamics:
- Early rounds: a few players try contributing; defectors earn more, which signals that cooperation is costly.
- Middle rounds: cooperation becomes sporadic — a few players continue to test or sustain cooperation while most withdraw. Rewarding is sparse and targeted (often contributors rewarding contributors), used to encourage persistence.
- Rewards are used sparingly because they cost the sender; they appear mainly from players who are willing to compensate cooperators (to uphold a fledgling norm) or reciprocate past help.
- Messages are infrequent and used to praise, encourage, or call out behavior; they rarely change the immediate binary contribution choice in a single round but can shape expectations.

Below I predict a plausible 8-round transcript using these tendencies: a handful of initial contributions, then partial collapse to mostly withdrawal with intermittent cooperative attempts and sparse rewarding.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot-style public goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round everyone privately chooses how much of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns only 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to each player — less than the private cost of 1. Rational self-interest predicts zero contributions, but social preferences, reciprocity, and signaling (via rewards and chat) can sustain positive contributions early on. Rewards are costly (3 coins per reward unit) but give a smaller benefit (2 coins per unit) to the target; this makes rewards a credible signal of other-regarding motive rather than a direct efficiency-improving device. Because players do not know the horizon, early rounds often show relatively higher cooperation, sustained by reciprocal rewards and encouragement in chat; if cooperation is not broadly reciprocated, contributions typically decline over time. Free-riders (players who repeatedly give little) may get ignored rather than formally punished (there is no direct punishment), and cooperators will reward one another sparingly. Expect: initial moderate-to-high contributions from social types (CHICKEN, DUCK, SLOTH), low contributions from more self-oriented types (PARROT, GORILLA), sparse reward exchanges focused on mutual cooperators early on, and a gradual erosion of cooperation toward lower contributions if rewards and reciprocity don't offset the private incentives.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds, and only two contribution choices each round: 0 or 20. The social dilemma is clear: contributing (20) increases the group pot, which is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally (so each coin contributed returns 0.62 to every player). A single contributor alone loses relative to free riders (they pay 20 but get only 0.62*20 = 12.4 back), while if many people coordinate on contributing, everyone gets much larger payoffs. Because choices are simultaneous within a round, early uncertainty and strategic mistrust can produce under-provision (some free-riding). Communication is allowed and sparse messages can shift beliefs: persuasive or conditional promises (e.g., "I'll contribute if you all do") often increase cooperation among players who are sensitive to framing or social anchors. End-game effects (final round) tend to reduce contributions because there is no future reciprocity.

Given the 10 player personas, expect:
- Some players (those with higher anchor/framing sensitivity) will respond to short coordination messages and are likely to be conditional cooperators.
- A subset will free-ride early to test others' behavior and adjust.
- Chat will be sparse and mostly used to promise or to call out free-riding.
- Over the six rounds we anticipate an early mixed pattern, a mid-game attempt to coordinate (with increased contributions if the coordination message is persuasive), and a drop in the final round due to the end-game effect.

Now the play-by-play transcript.

Each round every player privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally, so each contributed coin returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to its contributor. Without social enforcement, the dominant (selfish) strategy is to contribute 0. However, social preferences (conditional cooperation), communication, and the availability of costly punishment and rewarding can sustain higher contributions.

Expectations about likely dynamics given the personas and the punishment/reward technology:
- Early rounds: many players will try a moderate contribution (around half the endowment) out of conditional cooperation or fairness motives (DUCK, GORILLA, SLOTH, DOG), while CHICKEN (low purchase/cheap tendency) and PARROT (framing-sensitive, low search) may start lower.
- Communication is sparse but targeted: short requests to cooperate, occasional reminders, or short expressions of approval/disapproval.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly. Because punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) but highly damaging to the target (−12 per unit), players will avoid frequent or indiscriminate punishment. Expect a few punitive actions early where low contributors are singled out, and a handful of small rewards (1 unit) used to reinforce cooperative behavior.
- If a low contributor is punished and identified, they will often respond by increasing contributions in subsequent rounds; conditional cooperators will mirror the group average. Over many rounds, contributions may stabilize at a moderate level (around 8–14) if enforcement is present; without sustained enforcement, there is a slow downward drift.
- Because players do not know how many rounds remain, behavior does not show a final-round collapse until late; towards the end, some decline is possible if sanctions are withdrawn.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>
This is a repeated 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round players choose either to contribute their whole 20 coins to a shared pot or keep the 20 privately. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players. Because the public return per contributed coin (6.2/10 = 0.62) is less than 1, each player individually gets less back from contributing than they forgo; so the dominant (selfish) incentive is to keep the 20 and free-ride. However, if many players contribute, everyone’s round payoff increases compared to everyone defecting. Uncertainties: players do not see choices before deciding, but they do see round payoffs afterward (their own and peers'), so they can infer group behavior from payoffs and adjust strategy in later rounds. Chat is allowed and can serve as coordination or signaling; early rounds often see some cooperation that erodes as free-riding is observed. Given the player personas and typical dynamics, I predict a reasonably cooperative start (several players contribute), then a steady decline in contributions as the advantage of free-riding becomes salient; occasional short messages attempt to restore cooperation but have limited effect. Final round likely sees low contributions due to end-game incentives.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round players privately decide whether to contribute all 20 coins or keep them (0 or 20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 6.2, then split equally among all 10 players. So each contributor helps raise a common pot that benefits everyone, but any individual who withholds their 20 keeps it and still collects an equal share of the multiplied pot. That creates a strong short-term incentive to free-ride: non-contributors always receive 20 more than contributors in the same round (because contributors give up their 20 but both get the same share of the public pot).

Typical dynamics: early rounds may include some cooperative pledges or conditional cooperation (people who value group benefit or reciprocation). Because free-riding yields higher individual payoff when others contribute, you'll often see cooperation fall after learning others' behavior. Messages can encourage coordination, but they are cheap and unverifiable; when payoffs are shown only to participants, players who care about payoff maximization tend to reduce contributions over time, especially near the end (final-round defection). Persona cues suggest some players are more likely to cooperate (higher purchase-yes rates like MOOSE, OWL), while others have lower cooperation priors (PARROT, DOG, SLOTH). Expect a mix: a handful of contributors early, conditional cooperators who respond to peers' choices, and gradual decline toward lower contributions by the final round.
