game_explanation:
This is a repeated online public-goods game with optional punishment and reward after contributions are revealed. With six players, the social multiplier (3.8) makes contributions socially valuable: each coin contributed returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 to each player, so contributing benefits the group more than keeping the coin privately. However, privately each coin contributed reduces the contributor's private holding by 1, so selfish incentives push toward low contributions while group incentives push toward higher contributions.

Typical dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds will test cooperation — some players will contribute moderately, others will undercontribute. Because players cannot see choices before deciding, social norms and enforcement develop gradually.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly. Punishment is costly for the punisher (3 coins per unit) and very costly for the punished (12 coins per unit), so punishments are usually used only when someone persistently free-rides. Rewards are also costly to give (3 coins) but beneficial to the target (+2 coins) and thus are used occasionally to reinforce cooperative behavior.
- Players with prior tendencies toward cooperation or conditional cooperation (e.g., PARROT, DOG, SLOTH, CHICKEN) will generally contribute more; those with lower cooperation priors (GORILLA, DUCK at times) will sometimes contribute less and may be targeted with punishment until they moderate.
- Chat is sparse but can be used to signal intentions, ask for more contributions, or thank others; expect a few brief messages when norms shift.
- Over many rounds, we expect cooperation to stabilize at moderate-to-high contributions (around 10–15) as enforcement (punish/reward) and reciprocity take effect; occasional deviations will be met with targeted punishment or reward.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with seven players, binary per-round contribution choices (contribute 20 or withdraw 20), redistribution (total contributed ×2.6 split equally), and an afterstage allowing sparse costly punishment (2 cost to sender, -3 to target) and costly reward (2 cost to sender, +2 to target). Players do not know how many rounds remain, so we expect gradual norm formation: early rounds will show uncertainty and some free-riding because defecting immediately yields higher payoff. But the visibility of targeted punishment/reward and the ability to identify who acted gives cooperators tools to enforce contributions. Therefore likely dynamics: a mixed start with a small cooperating minority; targeted punishments of clear free-riders; conditional cooperators respond by switching to contribute; cooperation rises mid-game to near-full levels; occasional one-off defections trigger light, targeted punishment; sparse rewarding appears to reinforce switches to cooperation. Chats are sparse and used for brief calls for cooperation, thanks, or short threats.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits a clear private incentive to defect (withdraw the full 20) against a group incentive to contribute (leave the 20 in the shared pot). With 7 players, a single contributor’s private return from the public pot is small (2.6 × 20 divided by 7 ≈ 7.43), so anyone who contributes loses about 12.57 relative to withdrawing. Without additional mechanisms, defection is the dominant one-shot strategy.

Two forces can support cooperation here: repeated interaction (players learn others’ tendencies and can reward good behavior) and direct rewards (which shift payoffs among players and signal approval). Rewards here are pure redistribution (2 coins cost to giver → +2 coins to recipient) and can be used sparingly to reinforce contributors. Because players don’t know how many rounds remain, we expect a stable core of conditional cooperators to emerge (they contribute when they see mutual support), while some players continue to free-ride. Chat is likely to be sparse and tactical (pledges, thanks, short appeals). game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game pits private incentives (keep 20) against group payoff (contribute 20 to a pot that is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally). Because the multiplier per person (6.2 / 10 = 0.62) yields less return to a single contributor than keeping their 20 privately, the one-shot incentive is to free-ride. But repeated interaction, visible punishments, and the ability to identify who punished whom create scope for cooperation: some players will contribute to push group payoffs up, and a subset will use costly punishment to deter free-riding. Early rounds are uncertain (players test others); punished defectors tend to switch to contributing in subsequent rounds. Punishment is typically used sparingly — enough to signal norms but not so much that punishers lose heavily. In the last round, the incentive to defect is strongest (no future rounds), so some unraveling of cooperation is likely. Chat is usually limited and used to request cooperation or to threaten/justify punishment.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round every player chooses either to keep all 20 coins (contribute 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. Because the private payoff from keeping 20 plus the equal share usually dominates an individual contributor's private return for that round, the one-shot incentive is to withdraw (defect). However, across repeated rounds, players may sustain cooperation by using punishment (costly to administer, costly to the target) and reward (costly to source, beneficial to target), and by sending messages. Players do not know how many rounds remain, which favors conditional cooperation sustained by reputation, reciprocal rewards, and targeted punishments.

Expectations and likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed behavior. Some intrinsically cooperative or reputation-sensitive players contribute; others test free-riding. Cheap-talk ("I'll punish free-riders") will appear occasionally; such threats are modestly credible because punishment is observable and identifies source-target links.
- Punishment and reward: used sparingly and targeted. Punishment is used mainly against clear defectors to deter future free-riding; reward is used occasionally to reinforce contributors. Because punishments are costly to give (2 coins per unit) and costly to receive (3 coins per unit removed), players will usually administer a unit or two rather than heavy multilateral punishment.
- Over rounds: cooperation tends to rise from targeted sanctions and rewards. Occasional defections are likely (tests/exploitations), followed by a small number of punishments that bring the group back toward cooperation. Chats are sparse and instrumental (requests, warnings, short acknowledgements).

Now the play-by-play transcript from round 1 through round 8 follows.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with six players. Each round every player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split evenly. Because 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 < 1, each coin you contribute returns less to you personally than keeping it, so the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride. However, the group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes more, so social tension and coordination motives appear.

Two additional features matter here. First, after seeing contributions and payoffs players may send costly, directed rewards: every reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the receiver 2 coins. Rewards are net costly to the group but can change incentives by providing direct bonuses to cooperators (and are the only punitive/reward mechanism available here). Second, players do not know how many rounds will be played, which typically sustains cooperation longer than a known finite horizon but still produces gradual drift toward lower contributions over many rounds.

Given the six personas:
- SLOTH is dispositionally cooperative and likely to contribute relatively high amounts and use rewards sparingly to reinforce cooperation.
- PARROT is attentive and information-seeking; likely to propose or follow cooperative norms and reward cooperators.
- CHICKEN is framing-sensitive and may be responsive to communication and reciprocity—likely to settle on moderate cooperation if others do.
- GORILLA, DUCK, and DOG lean toward lower contributions and less engagement; they will experiment and sometimes free-ride, although occasional conformity or reciprocation can occur.

I predict an initial phase of moderate contributions with sparse pro-social rewarding to signal and reinforce cooperation (rounds ~1–8), a middle phase of relative stability (rounds ~9–16) where some players sustain cooperation while a few drift down, and a late-phase gradual decline (rounds ~17–26) as some low contributors exploit the group and rewards remain infrequent. Chat will be sparse and used mainly to encourage cooperation or register disappointment; rewards will be sparse and targeted to high contributors, mainly sent by SLOTH and PARROT, with occasional reciprocation by CHICKEN.

However, the presence of costly punishment and reward allows cooperators to discipline free-riders (punishment: costly and subtractive) and to reinforce cooperation (reward: costly but beneficial to recipient). Because punishment and reward are visible and attributable, we expect conditional strategies: some players will try to sustain cooperation by contributing and selectively punishing persistent defectors; some defectors may switch after repeated punishment, while stubborn defectors (or antisocial players) may persist and provoke occasional retaliation. Communication is allowed and will be sparse — short promises, warnings, or complaints. Punishment and reward actions will be relatively rare and targeted rather than wholesale, since both are costly.

Overall likely dynamics over eight rounds:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions (some test cooperation), a few targeted punishments/rewards to signal norms.
- Middle rounds: cooperators converge on contributing; persistent defectors receive targeted punishment; occasional rewards to encouragers.
- Later rounds: some defectors switch after sustained costs, enabling near-universal cooperation; a few last retaliations or rewards may occur.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, 6-round public goods game with a strict binary choice each round (contribute 0 or 20). The central tension: contributing costs 20 privately but benefits everyone because the shared pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split across all 10 players. Free-riding (contributing 0) gives a higher immediate private payoff that round, but others can respond by punishing defectors at personal cost. Punishment is costly (2 coins per unit to the punisher; 2 coins deducted per unit from the punished), so punishers must balance deterrence vs. expense. Messages let players coordinate or signal intent but are non-binding.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: moderate to high cooperation, with a few testers/free-riders. Cooperators may use modest punishment to discourage free-riding.
- Mid game: after a few punishments, many defectors switch to contributing to avoid repeated sanctions; cooperation tends to stabilize if punishment is effective but not too costly.
- Last round: backward-induction pressure reduces deterrence value of punishment, so some players may defect (no future rounds to enforce), and punitive responses tend to be sparser or more expressive than strategic.
- Uncertainties: individual tendencies (some players are more prone to experiment or defect) and small-sample punishment decisions can produce noisy transitions. Chat can foster cooperation but is not determinative.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game places the same 20 coins in a shared pot each round, and each player decides whether to withdraw all 20 for private use (contribute 0) or leave them in the pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among the seven players, so the more people who contribute, the larger the group payoff — but an individual who withholds their 20 gets a private benefit that typically exceeds the share they would get from contributing unless many others also contribute. After redistribution, players can spend coins to give positive rewards to others: each reward unit costs the giver 2 coins and gives the receiver 2 coins. Rewards are visible (who gave what) and sparse in most real games.

Key likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often start with some cooperation by conditional cooperators and high “purchase-yes” personas (they tend to be more willing to contribute). Here, GORILLA, DUCK, MOOSE and SLOTH are relatively likely to contribute early; CHICKEN, DOG, and PARROT are more likely to withhold initially.
- Because defectors (withdrawing 20) immediately keep their 20 plus still get the redistributed share, defecting yields higher round income unless a large majority contributes. This creates an incentive to free-ride.
- Positive rewards can sustain cooperation if cooperators selectively reward other cooperators and newcomers. However, rewards are costly and cannot directly punish defectors (only reward good behavior), so rewards are usually sparse and used to reinforce cooperation among a subset of players.
- Uncertainty about the total number of rounds encourages tentative cooperation early on; when free-riding is observed and not deterred, cooperation tends to decline over middle rounds. Occasional attempts to re-start cooperation with promises or rewards can appear late, but full recovery is unlikely without sustained reciprocal rewarding.

I simulate these tendencies: early moderate cooperation with selective rewarding among cooperators; a gradual erosion as free-riding pays off; sparse, targeted rewards; intermittent chat messages used to state intent, praise, or express frustration.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with seven players. Each round each player either keeps 20 privately (contributes 0) or leaves the full 20 in the shared pot (contributes 20). The total contributed is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly, so individual incentives favor keeping the 20 (private payoff) while the group payoff is maximized if many contribute. Players cannot see others' current-round choices when they decide, but they do see contributions and who rewarded whom after the round. Rewards are costly to send (2 coins per unit) and give an equal benefit (2 coins per unit) to the target; rewards are therefore a way to signal appreciation or build reciprocity but do not change total group earnings.

Likely unfolding and incentives:
- Early rounds are uncertain: some players will try to lead by contributing; others will free-ride because keeping 20 and sharing the multiplied pot often yields a higher immediate payoff.
- Rewarding is sparse because it costs as much as it benefits the target; players typically reward cooperators selectively to encourage them but not enough to fully offset the personal cost of cooperation.
- Over rounds we should expect partial cooperation from a subset of players (stubborn cooperators or those who reciprocate rewards) while others persistently withdraw. This typically produces a slow erosion of cooperation unless a stable cluster of mutual reward/reciprocity forms.
- Chat is rare and typically used to explain motives, thank rewarders, or complain about free-riding.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game with costly punishment and costly reward typically unfolds with an early period of mixed contributions, occasional targeted sanctions against clear free-riders, and gradual establishment of a cooperative norm if sanctions and/or rewards successfully discourage persistent defections. Because each contributed coin returns only ~0.63 to the contributor, self-interest favors zero contributions; social incentives (multiplier 3.8 split across 6 players) favor full contributions. Punishment is a strong deterrent (source pays 3 per unit, target loses 12 per unit), so players who are willing to incur costs to enforce norms (or who prefer fairness) can raise contributions, but overuse of punishment is costly to punishers. Rewards are less punitive (source 3 per unit, target receives +2), so they can reinforce positive behavior without as much cost-effectiveness for the group.

Given the provided personas:

game_explanation:
This online public goods game pits private incentives (keep 20 coins) against group incentives (contribute to a pot that is multiplied by 3.8 and split). Early on, players who are dispositionally cooperative (DOG, DUCK) and those sensitive to anchors or social cues (DUCK, GORILLA) are likely to contribute more; conditional cooperators (SLOTH, CHICKEN) will match others. Rewards are costly (3 coins per reward unit) but transfer social value (2 coins to the recipient), so players tend to reward to sustain cooperation or to signal approval to consistent contributors. Because players don’t know the length of the game, cooperation often holds for a while through reciprocity and rewards, then drifts downward as some players free-ride or reduce contributions. Rewards are typically sparse — used selectively to reinforce cooperation — and as contributions decline, reward frequency falls. By late rounds, expectations and contributions commonly collapse toward zero.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This seven-player public goods game makes cooperation simple but strategically costly: each player either leaves the full 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so any single player's best-response (ignoring social preferences) is to withdraw 20 and free-ride — defectors keep the private 20 and still benefit from the public good. However, because players can reward others after seeing contributions, social incentives can sustain cooperation: small, targeted rewards transfer wealth to cooperators and signal approval. Uncertainties include whether defectors will be shamed or ignored, whether rewards will be stable and reciprocal, and whether a few persistent free-riders (especially those with low cooperative priors) will erode mutual cooperation. Expect a mix: a core of cooperators who reward each other modestly, a few persistent defectors who exploit the public pot, occasional experiments (someone trying to shift norms), and sparse chat aimed at coordination or reputation signaling.

This is a repeated public‑goods game with 6 players who each receive 20 coins per round and simultaneously decide how many coins (0–20) to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players can spend private coins to punish (cost 3 per unit; deducts 12 per unit from the target) or to reward (cost 3 per unit; gives 2 per unit to the target). Players do not know how many rounds remain, so their behavior typically follows conditional cooperation: people increase contributions if peers contributed more previously or if they were rewarded, and they reduce contributions after being exploited. Punishment is costly and usually used sparingly to respond to clear free‑riding; rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce cooperation. Chat messages are infrequent and usually short coordination or signaling (requests to cooperate, apology, thanks).

Given the provided personas:
- SLOTH tends to be slow/reactive and anchor‑sensitive; likely starts moderate and adjusts slowly.
- GORILLA tends toward lower cooperation at first but will respond to sanctions.
- DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG tend to cooperate more often (higher contribution tendencies) and may be the ones to punish or reward to maintain cooperation.
- PARROT is middling and responsive to framing; may fluctuate.

I expect early rounds to show mixed contributions, a few targeted punishments of obvious low contributors, some small rewards for cooperators, and overall convergence toward moderate cooperation (many rounds with contributions in the 10–14 range). Punishments will be rare and typically single‑unit actions; rewards will also be occasional single units. Chats will be short and purposeful (requests, short justifications, thanks).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round one-shot-style public goods game repeated for a known finite horizon. Each round every player privately chooses to contribute 20 or 0 from a 20-coin endowment. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split evenly among all 10 players, so each contributed 20 yields 12.4 back to each player. Individual incentives push toward free-riding (you lose 20 but directly get only 12.4 of that), while group efficiency favors everyone contributing (everyone contributing yields a large group payoff). Typical dynamics: some cooperation may appear early (pledges or unconditional cooperators), then decline as free-riders get higher immediate payoffs; occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation can appear mid-game, and many players defect in the final round because there are no future consequences. Messages can change expectations briefly, so expect sparse, purposeful chat (promises, short appeals, or accusations) and a gradual decline in contributions with possible brief recoveries.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds, and binary contributions (0 or 20 each round). The shared pot multiplies total contributions by 6.2 and splits equally, so whenever k players contribute, each player gets 12.4·k coins from the pot. Contributing therefore reduces a player's private holding by 20 but increases everyone (including themselves) through the pot; defecting (contributing 0) always gives a direct private advantage of 20 relative to a cooperator in the same round, but cooperation can be sustained if players punish defectors at some cost. Punishment is costly (2 coins per unit to the punisher and 2 coins deducted from the target) and observable in the summary (targets are told who punished them), so punishment can serve as a deterrent or a costly expression of norm enforcement.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show a mixture of contributions and free-riding. Some players declare intentions or signal cooperation via chat; others remain silent.
- Cooperators tend to punish obvious free riders, but punishment is used sparingly because it reduces the punisher's own payoff.
- Players who are punished are likely to switch to cooperating in subsequent rounds to avoid future losses; some stubborn players keep defecting until repeatedly punished.
- As rounds progress, cooperation tends to rise because punishments reduce the attractiveness of being a persistent defector. However, in the final round (round 6) backward-induction effects often make defecting more attractive because future retaliation cannot be credibly threatened; thus a small unraveling of cooperation is common in the last round.
- Chat remains sparse and typically used to call out defectors, propose norms, or protest punishment.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a standard public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each, and a pot multiplier of 6.2. Individually, contributing 20 is dominated: a unilateral contribution reduces private holding by 20 but raises the per-player return by 12.4 for everyone, so a contributor gets less than a free-rider when others' contributions are fixed. Socially, however, full contribution maximizes group payoff (if all 10 contribute, each gets 124). Expectation: a modest initial level of contributions from more prosocial or high "purchase-yes" personas (MOOSE, DOG, PARROT, maybe DUCK) plus sparse promises in chat; then gradual decline as contributors see they earn less than free-riders. Messaging can sustain a small committed core (MOOSE appears most likely to persist), occasional revivals are possible if peers reciprocate, and end-game defection is likely (fewer or only the most prosocial contribute in the last round). Chat will be sparse and used mainly for pledges, quick reactions, or a plea to cooperate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players. Each round everyone can either leave the 20-coin pot intact (contribute 20) or withdraw it privately (contribute 0). The sum of contributions is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. After payoffs are computed, players may spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs 2 to the sender and gives 2 to the receiver). Players do not know how many rounds will be played, so they face uncertainty about future reciprocity.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: experimentation. Some players who lean cooperative (high search/strategic priors like GORILLA, PARROT, DOG) start by contributing to test reciprocity; others with more self-oriented priors (SLOTH, CHICKEN) may withdraw and free-ride.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperators. Because rewards are costly, expect 0–2 small positive reward acts per round, typically targeted at contributors.
- Over rounds, conditional cooperators (PARROT, DUCK) and strategic types (GORILLA, DOG) tend to sustain contributions if they receive positive feedback; some freeriders may switch late but some (CHICKEN) may persist in free-riding.
- Toward the end, if players suspect an endgame, cooperation may fall off; because players don't know the horizon, this decline is gradual rather than immediate.

Below I simulate a plausible 8-round transcript, showing round-level reasoning, sparse chat, contributions (0 or 20 each), the small set of reward actions, and the round summaries players see (net payoff, amount used for rewarding, amount received).

game_explanation:
This is a standard public-goods dilemma: each coin you keep yields 1 to you; each coin you contribute yields 3.8 total to the group, split among all 6 players (so each contributor gets back about 0.633 of every coin they put in). Alone, the marginal private return (≈0.63) is less than 1, so pure selfish incentives favor contributing 0. In practice, people often vary: some start cooperative, some free-ride, and conditional cooperators respond to others.

Because there's a costly reward stage after redistribution, players can use rewards to promote cooperation: spending 3 coins to give someone 2 coins is costly but can serve as positive reinforcement. Expect a few altruistic/rewarding players (DOG, CHICKEN, PARROT, and DOG especially by persona) to try to seed cooperation early by rewarding contributors; free-riders (GORILLA, possibly DUCK) will enjoy short-term high payoffs unless rewarded players can sustain cooperation through repeated positive feedback. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so early establishing of a cooperative norm via sparse but visible rewards and occasional chat is likely. Chats will be few and functional (praise, promises, or short signals). Rewards will be used sparingly and targeted at those who contribute noticeably; not everyone will reward every round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 6 players, private endowments of 20 each, and a per-coin social multiplier of 3.8 (so each coin contributed returns ~0.633 to each player). Individually, contributing costs you 1 and gives you less than 1 back, so purely selfish incentives push toward 0 contributions. Group-optimal behavior is full contribution, but that requires trust and enforcement. Because players don't know the number of rounds, cooperation can persist if sanctions (punishment) or incentives (rewards) are used credibly. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 per unit) but inflicts a large penalty on the target (12 per unit), so players will punish sparingly and usually only for clear free-riding. Rewards cost 3 and give 2 to the recipient, so they are a milder, more prosocial incentive.

Expectations for dynamics here: early rounds will show moderate contributions from those predisposed to cooperate and lower contributions from more selfish players. Occasional targeted punishment of clear under-contributors can stabilize mid-range cooperation; rewards may be used occasionally to encourage high contributors. Chat will be sparse and typically used to call out free-riders, encourage cooperation, or respond to punishment. Punishments will be rare and usually single-unit; rewards will be even rarer.

Because each player keeps private holdings and does not know the horizon, there is no forced end-game collapse; instead contributions will reflect a mix of conditional cooperation (matching others seen in summaries), occasional free-riding, and a small number of costly reward choices used to praise cooperators. Rewards here are expensive (3 coins cost per 1 reward unit) and modestly beneficial to recipients (2 coins per unit), so players will use them sparingly — mainly to signal approval of consistent contributors or to try to sustain cooperation after a drop. Personas suggest CHICKEN and PARROT are the most likely to use normative prompts and to reward cooperators; SLOTH and DUCK are moderately cooperative/conditional; DOG and GORILLA are more variable, with GORILLA more likely to free-ride and DOG to stick to anchors. Chat will be rare and purposeful (thank-yous, short requests, or brief strategy notes).

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round binary public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round every player chooses either to contribute their full 20 coins or keep 20; the pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally. The individual incentive is to free-ride (not contribute) because the private cost of contributing (20) exceeds the private return from raising the pot (12.4 to each player). Group-optimal behavior is full contribution. Because punishment is possible but itself costly (2 coins spent per 1 punishment unit, and each unit deducts 2 from the target), we expect the following dynamics:

- Early rounds: some players will try contributing; others will free-ride. Free-riders often earn more in the short run, so contributors may feel exploited.
- Punishment will be used sparingly and targeted at obvious free-riders to enforce norms, but because punishing is costly and there are only 6 rounds, punishments will be limited. Contributors who punish will incur direct costs each round they punish.
- Over rounds: contributions may fall when contributors see they are exploited, then possibly rebound if players coordinate (via chat) and commit to punishing serial defectors. Near the final round, backward induction and lack of future interactions typically reduce incentives to punish and cooperate, so contributions tend to drop.
- Chats will be sparse but may be used to call out defectors or to try briefly to coordinate punitive enforcement. Punishments will be visible to targets (who see who punished them), which supports targeted retaliation/policy.

Given the provided personas and these incentives, we predict mixed cooperation early, some targeted punishment, a temporary recovery mid-game when players punish repeat defectors, and a decline in cooperation in the final round. The transcript below follows those expectations.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This public-goods game sets up a classic tension: each player can either keep their 20 coins (contribute 0) or put them into the group pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and shared equally, so the group maximizes payoff when everyone contributes, but each individual gains more privately by free-riding (keeping 20) unless punishment or social incentives deter them.

Key likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed behavior. Some players (conditional cooperators and those more sensitive to norms) will contribute; others will test free-riding. Because contributions are binary, defections are highly visible and attractive.
- Punishment will be used sparingly but meaningfully: cooperators who lose out will punish obvious free-riders to deter future defection. Punishment is costly (2 coins per unit sent) so it's used selectively.
- Punishment tends to increase cooperation in subsequent rounds: punished players often switch to contributing. When cooperation becomes common, punishment drops. In the final round some players may attempt last-round free-riding; punishment sometimes persists even in round 6 but is usually less frequent.
- Messages are sparse and usually short: some pre-commitments, some calls to punish or to cooperate, occasional expressions of gratitude or frustration.

I predict an initial mixed contribution pattern with a few punished defectors, rising cooperation through rounds 2–3, stability in the middle, a small defection mid-to-late game that gets punished, and a few last-round defections with limited punitive responses.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits private incentives (keeping coins) against a social benefit from contributing to a shared pot. Each coin a player contributes returns 3.8/6 ≈ 0.633 coins to each player, so a contributor loses more privately than they gain directly — there is a one-shot incentive to free-ride. Because the game repeats and players can both send messages and reward others (at private cost 3 to give +2 to a target), we expect conditional cooperation: some players who value coordination or who notice others contributing will contribute relatively high amounts and occasionally use sparse rewards to reinforce cooperation. Others with weaker social preferences or higher framing sensitivity will tend to under-contribute or respond to perceived unfairness by cutting back.

Uncertainties that shape behavior here:
- Players do not know how many rounds remain, which typically sustains modest cooperation longer than if the end were known.
- Rewards are costly and somewhat inefficient (3 → 2), so they will be used sparingly — mainly to signal appreciation or to sustain reciprocity among reliable cooperators.
- Chat is available but usually used infrequently; short appeals ("let's keep contributing") or short complaints are most likely.

Overall forecast for the 26 rounds:
- Two players (GORILLA and CHICKEN) are the most consistent cooperators and will repeatedly contribute at high levels and reciprocate each other with small reward units.
- SLOTH and DUCK will be moderate, responsive to others (sometimes increasing when others do).
- DOG and PARROT will contribute relatively little across the experiment, occasionally nudged upward by messages or rewards but mostly free-riding.
- Rewarding behavior will be sparse and targeted, mostly 1-unit transfers between cooperating players; many rounds will have no rewards.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players with differing tendencies into repeated interaction without a known end. Because each coin contributed yields only 0.633 coins back to the contributor (3.8 total multiplier split among six), the selfish best response each round is to contribute 0. But social return is higher if everyone contributes, so conditional cooperation and social enforcement matter.

With messaging and costly punish/reward available, I expect an initial mix of contributions: some players test cooperation, some free-ride. When free-riding is visible, cooperators are likely to send a few small punishments to signal norm enforcement; cooperators will also occasionally send rewards to reinforce good behavior. Punishments are typically sparse and small (1–2 units) because they are costly to send. Messages are also sparse but used to set norms early and later to signal approval or request compliance. Over rounds, repeated small sanctions and positive reinforcement should move the group toward a higher, more stable cooperation level, with occasional lapses and targeted responses. Retaliation is possible but generally limited in frequency.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with seven players. Each round starts with 20 coins in the shared pot; a player either leaves all 20 in the pot (contributes 20) or withdraws them (contributes 0). The sum of contributed coins is multiplied by 2.6 and divided evenly among all seven players. After seeing contributions and returns, players can spend 2 coins per reward unit to give 2 coins to any other player. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so there's no guaranteed endgame; that tends to encourage some sustained cooperation but also allows opportunistic defection because defecting (withdrawing) gives an immediate private payoff advantage unless many others contribute. Rewarding is the only way to directly change others' payoffs post-distribution; because rewards are costly, we expect them to be used sparingly and mainly to (a) reward consistent cooperators or (b) reciprocate earlier rewards. Chats are usually short — pledges, short protests, thanks — and may influence a few players but won’t fully prevent free-riding. Given the personas: DUCK is the most reliably pro-social and likely to contribute consistently; SLOTH and GORILLA are moderately prone to cooperate sometimes; PARROT is likely to defect; CHICKEN, DOG, and MOOSE are mixed-to-selfish but may experiment. Expect DUCK to be the steady contributor across rounds, occasional cooperation from SLOTH and GORILLA, sparse rewards targeted at visible cooperators, and gradual overall defection pressure.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with binary contribution choices (contribute 20 = cooperate, withdraw 20 = defect). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so defectors keep their private 20 and still receive a share of the public return — giving them a tempting short-run advantage. However, players can punish (costly to the punisher but harsher on the target) or reward (costly to the giver and beneficial to the recipient). Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect:

- Early rounds: some hopeful cooperation as players test norms, with a small set of players experimenting by defecting.
- When defectors consistently earn more, cooperators sometimes punish sparingly to deter exploitation. Punishment is costly, so it tends to be used only when perceived as necessary.
- Rewards appear occasionally to reinforce cooperation (cheap signaling).
- Defectors may oscillate between defection and cooperation depending on how strongly they are punished.
- Over several rounds the group often converges toward a cooperative norm if punishers coordinate; alternately, some persistent defectors can keep cooperation fragile.
- Chat will be sparse and used to coordinate, threaten, or thank.

All contributions are shown in the given avatar order: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 20 coins each round, and a group multiplier that yields an individual return per contributed coin of 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 — less than 1. That means every coin you personally give to the public pot lowers your private holding by 1 but returns only ~0.623 to you; the individually rational (selfish) choice is to contribute 0. In contrast, total group welfare rises with contributions. Players cannot chat and do not see others' choices before deciding, but each player's round payoff (which is a function of their contribution and the group total) lets them infer the group's total contribution after the round.

Given those incentives and the provided personas, a typical unfolding is: early rounds start with moderate contributions (some initial optimism, heuristics, or social preferences). After seeing payoffs that reflect others' choices, many participants gradually reduce contributions toward free-riding; without direct communication this tends to be a steady decline. Some players with stronger cooperative tendencies or high “purchase yes” / low anchor-sensitivity in the priors (e.g., MOOSE, COW, CROCODILE) are likely to remain partial contributors longer; high-anchor players may stick to a contribution level for several rounds before adjusting. Because players can infer the group sum from their own payoff, they will adjust contributions in response, accelerating the decay toward low contributions by mid-to-late rounds. I therefore predict a decline from mid/high initial contributions to mostly low or zero contributions by the final rounds, with a small minority continuing to contribute modestly.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game with costly punishment typically unfolds as a coordination problem: players decide between keeping private payoff (free-riding) or contributing to a pot that benefits everyone. The pot multiplier (6.6) divided by 10 means each coin contributed returns 0.66 to each player — less than the private value of 1 — so pure monetary incentives favor free-riding. Social norms and punishment are the mechanisms that sustain higher contributions: when cooperators see low contributions, some will pay the cost to punish defectors to reduce their future defections. Punishment is costly (2 coins per unit to the punisher) but can be effective because it subtracts 5 coins per unit from the punished player.

Given the player priors, I expect an initial mix of modest-to-high contributions with a few low contributors. Other players will use punishment sparingly to discipline repeat defectors; that discipline will raise average contributions over the middle rounds. Chat will be sparse and used mainly to propose norms ("let's do 10 each"), call out defectors, or thank punishers. Occasional lapses by a low-contributing player (or fatigue) will trigger focused punishment, keeping group-level cooperation high but not perfectly stable. Because players do not know the number of rounds, there should be no coordinated endgame collapse; contributions may dip occasionally but generally converge toward high levels by mid-game.

game_explanation:
This is a standard multi-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to place into a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and then split equally across all active players; equivalently each coin contributed returns 0.66 coins to each player (so every contributed coin yields 0.66 to your own payoff and 0.66 to each other player). Because a coin you keep instead of contributing gives you 1 private coin but only 0.66 in private return if contributed, the individual incentive is to free-ride (keep coins), while the group as a whole benefits when people contribute.

Given uncertainty (players do not know the total number of rounds) we expect relatively higher contributions early on (possible “hope” for cooperation and no endgame rush), then a gradual decline as players test and respond to outcomes. Messaging can be used to signal intentions or coordinate, but messages are cheap and non-binding; some players will honestly pledge contributions, others may mislead or remain silent. Players only see their own net payoff at summary, not others’ contributions directly, but because the group payout depends on total contributions they can infer something about aggregate behavior from their own payoff. Personas suggest some players (e.g., OWL, PARROT, MOOSE) tend toward low contributions; others (SLOTH, DUCK, DOG) are more likely to contribute moderately. Expect sparse, purposeful chat (pledges, short complaints, occasional calls for more cooperation), and an overall trending decline in contributions across the 19 rounds.

This public goods game pits private incentives (keep 20 coins) against a group benefit: contributions to the pot are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all active players. Punishment is available and is costly to the punisher (3 coins per punishment unit) but quite costly to the target (7 coins lost per unit). Because players see contributions before punishing, but cannot observe others' future round choices, behavior will be shaped by a mix of conditional cooperation, occasional sanctions, and growing endgame incentives to free-ride.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players start moderately high (conditional cooperators and conformists). A few low contributors (especially those with low "purchase" tendency in their profiles) will be visible and occasionally targeted with small, symbolic punishment (1 unit) to signal that free-riding is noticed.
- Midgame: if punishment is effective, many low contributors will raise contributions toward the group norm. Punishment remains rare and targeted — enough to sustain cooperation but not so frequent as to be prohibitively costly for punishers.
- Late rounds: the incentive to defect grows as the horizon approaches (final rounds), so average contributions drift down. Punishment also becomes scarcer because punishers weigh the cost against dwindling future benefits; a few persistent defectors may still get occasional sanctions but overall punishing declines.
- Uncertainties: we don't know exact preferences for each player; some players labeled as high anchor-pull or high framing sensitivity might be more consistent/conformist (e.g., GORILLA), while low purchase-rate players (e.g., SNAKE, MOOSE, CROCODILE, COW) are more likely to contribute less. Punishment choices are strategic and costly, so expect sparse, targeted enforcement rather than heavy retaliation networks.

- Heterogeneity: given the provided personas, expect a mix—some consistent contributors (e.g., PARROT, COW, CROCODILE, CHICK) and some persistent low contributors (e.g., DUCK, RABBIT), with many in-between adjusting through the rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-player repeated public goods game with costly punishment. Each round players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, so each coin contributed yields 0.66 back to the contributor — a private loss but a social benefit. Because the marginal private return is below 1, the dominant temptation is to free-ride (contribute 0) while hoping others contribute. However, the ability to punish (2 coins cost to punish per unit, 5 coins deducted from the punished player per unit) lets players impose costly sanctions on low contributors. That creates an enforcement mechanism: if some punish, cooperation can be sustained near high contribution levels despite the material incentive to free-ride.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: players "test the waters" with a mix of moderate contributions and a few low contributions/defections. Some targeted punishments appear to signal norms and deter free-riding.
- Middle rounds: with punishment used sparingly but credibly, contributions converge to a higher level (most players contributing well above the selfish optimum). Punishment becomes less frequent, used mainly when a player defects noticeably.
- Late rounds: occasional deviations still occur (experimentation, spite, or attempted exploitation). Those are usually met with targeted punishments that keep average contributions relatively high. Because players do not know the end, we do not see a guaranteed final-round collapse; instead behavior often remains fairly consistent.

I will simulate 19 rounds. Chats are sparse and purposeful (calls for cooperation, brief reprimands, short acknowledgments). Punishments are used but not excessively; most punishment units are small (1-3), concentrated on clear low contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round, players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and shared equally. Punishment is available after contributions are revealed: sending a punishment unit costs the sender 3 coins and removes 7 coins from the target. The game lasts 16 rounds and players can see who punished them in the round summary (but not other players’ full details).

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds tend to show a mix of cooperation and caution. Some players (by persona) are predisposed toward high contributions and enforcement of norms (they will contribute a lot and occasionally punish low contributors). Others are more self-interested, conservative, or slow to engage, contributing little at first.
- Because punishment is costly, it will be used sparingly — mainly as a signal to deter persistent free-riding by low contributors. Punishment tends to be directed at the same outliers repeatedly until they adjust.
- There is uncertainty about others’ strategies. Early punishments can establish a cooperation norm; if punishments are effective, low contributors raise contributions in later rounds. If punishment is sporadic or too costly, some players will drift toward lower contributions.
- Toward the end of a known finite-horizon game, classic “endgame” incentives push contributions down: players expect less retaliation in final rounds and may free-ride more. Punishment also declines late because it’s less efficient to punish without future rounds to change behavior.

I’ll simulate a plausible unfolding given the player priors: a handful of high contributors (DUCK, CHICK, COW, CHICKEN, SNAKE, RABBIT) uphold cooperation and punish occasionally; several cautious or low-contributing players (SLOTH, MOOSE, CROCODILE, sometimes GORILLA or DOG) start low or drift down and receive targeted, sparse punishment; punishment frequency is higher in early-to-mid game to enforce norms and falls off toward the end.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players, each endowed with 20 coins per round. The group pot multiplies total contributions by 8.1 and splits evenly; that gives a private return of about 0.623 coins per coin contributed (8.1/13). Because that private return is below 1, contributing is individually costly but can generate large collective gains if many cooperate. Typical dynamics in such games: some players start with moderate-to-high contributions (conditional cooperators, high anchor-pull types), others start low or free-ride (low anchor-pull, low purchase-rate types). Over rounds, contributions commonly decline as free-riding spreads or as conditional cooperators reduce contributions after receiving lower-than-expected payoffs. Near the final rounds, backward-induction incentives push toward lower contributions; however, some high-anchor or pro-social types sometimes sustain positive contributions for a while. Uncertainty comes from noisy feedback: players only see their own net payoff each round, not peers' contributions, so inferences about others are indirect and sometimes ambiguous — this can slow down or accelerate declines depending on whether players interpret their payoff as evidence of cooperation or defection.

Predicted pattern here: initial moderate cooperation from anchor-sensitive players (e.g., MOOSE, SLOTH, DUCK) and mid/low contributions from more self-oriented players (e.g., GORILLA, RABBIT). Over rounds contributions decline as many players reduce to low or zero contributions; occasional small rebounds occur when some conditional cooperators try to raise group earnings, but by the last rounds nearly everyone free-rides.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game (16 rounds) sets up the classic tension: each player privately decides how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied (×8.1) and split equally, so individual contributions help the group but are costly to the contributor (the private return per coin is less than 1). In a finite 16-round game, players typically start with some cooperative contributions (social preferences, conformity, or uncertainty about others), but contributions often drift down toward free-riding as the horizon approaches. The available punishment (costly to send, damaging to targets) can sustain higher cooperation early- and mid-game—especially when cooperators target persistent low contributors—but punishment itself is costly and used sparingly in practice. Expect a moderate-to-high cooperation level early on from the more pro-social players (CROCODILE, CHICKEN, DUCK, COW, SNAKE), sporadic low contributions from a few defectors (CHICK, SLOTH, GORILLA), occasional targeted punishments by cooperators to discipline free riders (usually single-unit punishments), a period of relatively stable cooperation while punishment enforces norms, and a gradual unwinding toward lower contributions in the final rounds as endgame incentives reduce the benefit of costly punishment.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

Mid-game begins to show slight variability; a few players reduce contributions for strategic reasons or fatigue. Cooperators sometimes remind low contributors with a small punishment, but overall enforcement is economical.

The group starts inching toward lower contributions as the end approaches. Players who were marginally cooperative cut back; punishment becomes less frequent because senders weigh costs against waning long-term benefits.
Mid-to-late game decline continues. Some players respond to lower expected future gains by contributing less. A few occasional punishments persist, targeted at those who remain very low.
Approaching the final third of the game, cooperation softens further. Punishment is used only rarely, since costly disciplining buys little future benefit in a finite horizon.
As final rounds near, players discount the value of maintaining norms; some residual punishments appear as quick reminders, but overall the downward trend continues.

Endgame effects are clearly visible: several players cut contributions substantially. Cooperators risk punishing but often avoid the immediate cost when the future benefit is limited; occasional one-off punishments may still occur.
With only two rounds after this, most players prioritize private payoffs. Punishment is rare to non-existent because it yields little future improvement in others' behavior.
Very close to the end: contributions fall further as players free-ride. A small number of targeted punishments may appear from players who still hope to deter drastic free-riding, but these are rare.

Final round: standard prediction is near-universal free-riding because there's no future to enforce cooperation. Punishment is very unlikely since it only reduces immediate payoff with no future leverage.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-player repeated public-goods game with private endowments of 20 coins each round. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 6.6 and the total is split equally across the 10 players, so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to every player (including the contributor). Individually, contributing one coin costs you 1 but gives you 0.66 back, so the private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) while the social optimum is for everyone to contribute their full 20. Because players do not see others' contemporaneous choices and only observe their own net payoff each round, there is uncertainty about group behavior. Cheap talk (group chat) can be used to coordinate or signal intent, but messages are non-binding.

Given the player personas, I predict an initial phase with moderate-to-high contributions from several players (especially high-anchor, inclined cooperators like RABBIT and GORILLA), accompanied by occasional pledges in chat. PARROT, who tends to avoid purchasing and is less cooperative, will contribute very little from the start. Players who are search- or strategy-prone (DOG, OWL, CHICK) will try to sustain cooperation early but will reduce contributions when expectations about others' cooperation fall. Over repeated rounds — particularly because players cannot see peers' contributions and only get privacy-limited feedback — we should see cooperation decay as free-riding becomes apparent or suspected. Occasional messages will attempt to shore up contributions, but by mid-to-late game most players will scale back toward zero. A handful of conditional cooperators may keep contributing small amounts for a while, but by the end the group converges toward low or zero contributions.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 10-player repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 coins each round. Every coin a player contributes is multiplied by 6.6 and then split evenly among all 10 players, so each coin a player contributes returns 0.66 to themselves (and 0.66 to every other player). Individually, contributing is costly (private marginal return < 1) while the group as a whole is best off when everyone contributes more. Because players do not see others' choices before deciding and only see their own round payoff at the summary, information about others' behavior is sparse; messages can be used to signal intentions but can be unreliable.

Given the player priors:
- DUCK and CHICKEN are most likely to start with relatively high contributions and to send occasional cooperative messages.
- PARROT and OWL are moderately cooperative and may support proposals.
- MOOSE and some who are moderately sensitive to anchors may initially follow suggested contributions, but can reduce if momentum fades.
- SLOTH, GORILLA, DOG, and RABBIT lean toward lower contributions, with SLOTH and GORILLA especially likely to free-ride and remain quiet.
- Over repeated anonymous rounds with only private-payoff feedback, a common pattern is initial moderate cooperation by a subset, followed by a gradual erosion of contributions as free-riding increases, unless strong, credible coordination messages sustain cooperation.

I predict an early phase with several players contributing substantial shares (driven by DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT, MOOSE), occasional messages trying to sustain cooperation, and then a gradual decline toward minimal contributions as incentives to free-ride dominate and feedback is limited.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20 out of a 20-coin endowment. The group's contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all 13 players. Thus a given player's private incentive is to contribute nothing (keep 20) while still benefiting from others' contributions; the social optimum is for everyone to contribute the full 20. Players do not observe others' contributions directly, but they do observe their own net payoff each round, which allows them to infer the aggregate contribution level (because net payoff = 20 − own contribution + share of the public pot).

Predicted dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: players will start with heterogeneous, somewhat optimistic contributions (some conditional cooperators and some strong cooperators, plus several low contributors/free riders). Because players infer average contributions from their own payoff, those who free-ride and see a high payoff will be reinforced; contributors who see lower-than-expected payoffs will tend to reduce contributions.
- Mid game: contributions will drift downward as free-riding is rewarded and uncertainty about others' cooperativeness persists. Some stable cooperators (highly prosocial personas) will continue contributing more than average, but the group total will decline.
- Endgame (final rounds): classic end-game effects produce further declines: players expecting little future reciprocity or punishment reduce contributions further. A few players with stronger cooperative priors may sustain modest contributions, but overall contributions are lower than at the start.
- Uncertainties: because players only see their own payoff, inferences about group behavior vary by individual (those who contributed little but gained a lot will likely free-ride more; those who contributed and earned little will reduce). Also individual differences (anchor sensitivity, purchase-rate tendencies, search tendencies) create heterogeneity in how quickly each player reduces contributions.

Below is a simulated, round-by-round transcript (round explanations, predicted contributions, and the per-player net payoffs shown to each player after the round). Contributions are listed in the exact avatar order given.

Players update from their own payoff. Those who gave little but received high payoffs (e.g., CROCODILE, GORILLA, COW, CHICKEN) infer others contributed and are likely to continue low contributions. Conditional cooperators reduce somewhat if their payoff was not high relative to contribution.
Trend toward lower contributions continues; a few steady cooperators (DOG, MOOSE, OWL) still contribute relatively more, but many participants cut back after seeing modest returns.

As the middle game approaches, conditional cooperators respond to their inferred group contributions by trimming contributions further. Some stable contributors maintain moderate levels; free riders remain low.
Mid game momentum toward lower total contributions persists. Those who continue contributing fairly strongly (DOG, MOOSE, OWL) keep the public pot from collapsing entirely.

Players who saw good payoffs while contributing little continue to free-ride; some previous contributors reduce further. Steady cooperators begin to feel the cost but still contribute nonzero amounts.
Decline continues; the public return is noticeably lower, reinforcing reduction by conditional cooperators. A handful of participants maintain modest contributions.
Lower totals continue; some players reduce to near-zero contributions. Those who still contribute keep the pot from collapsing completely.
As contributions fall further, public returns are smaller and many players shift to minimal contributions. Some steady cooperators also step down gradually.
Late-middle game: low contributions continue; the average public benefit is small, so most reduce to very small amounts. A few try tiny contributions to test whether others will reciprocate.
Players nearing the end of the horizon reduce expectations of future reciprocity; many drop to very low contributions. Some who strongly prefer cooperation still give small amounts.
With few rounds left, many players see little benefit to contributing; steady cooperators may still give modest amounts but totals stay low.

Final round: strong end-game effect. With no future rounds to influence, many players contribute zero or very little. Some who care intrinsically still give small amounts, but the group total is low.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, each getting 20 coins per round. Each coin contributed returns 0.66 coins to the contributor (6.6 total multiplier split among 10), so the private incentive is to contribute 0 while the group optimum is full contribution. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is no definite endgame to drive a final-round collapse; instead we expect a mix of conditional cooperation and occasional free-riding. Punishment is available but costly (2 coins per punishment unit, 5 coins deducted from the target per unit). That makes punishment an effective deterrent but one players will use sparingly — typically to discipline repeated or egregious free-riders.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty and experimentation. Some players contribute moderately; a few test low contributions.
- If low contributors persist, a few players who value cooperation or have signaled willingness to enforce it (more punitive or high "purchase" tendencies in the priors) will issue small punishments to discipline them.
- Punishment use will be targeted and small (mostly 1-unit sanctions). Once punished, many players raise contributions toward the group norm.
- Overall contributions trend upward after early enforcement and stabilize at a fairly high level (but rarely everyone contributes 20). Punishment remains sparse and mostly directed at repeat low contributors.
- Chat messages are rare and typically admonishments, short coordination appeals, or acknowledgements of punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and costly bilateral punishment. Each coin contributed yields 8.1 total to the group, so each contributor gains about 0.623 per coin from the public pot while paying 1 coin privately — cooperation is socially valuable but individually costly. Punishment lets players reduce another's payoff (−7 per punishment unit) at a substantial private cost (−3 per unit), so punishment is effective but itself costly. Players do not see contributions before choosing, and they only see their own round payoff and who punished them afterward, which creates uncertainty about others' choices and motives.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players start moderately cooperative (following internal norms or anchors); a few attempt to free-ride. Because punishment is available and identifiable, a small set of norm-enforcing players (those who value the public good or are anchor-sensitive) will punish low contributors sparingly to deter exploitation. Punishment tends to be limited to 1–2 units per incident because of its cost.
- Middle rounds: after some enforcement, contributions rise or stabilize at moderate-to-high levels for many players. Persistent or opportunistic free-riders may be targeted again, but punishments remain rare.
- Endgame (last few rounds): finite-horizon reasoning and recognition that punishment has limited future deterrence drive contributions down. Players reduce contributions and punish less (punishing in the final round is especially unlikely because it only imposes costs with no future benefit).
- Across the game: punishment is used selectively, mostly to protect cooperative contributors against repeated or blatant free-riding. Overall punishment incidence is low relative to the number of rounds and players.

As the finite horizon nears, contributions fall more noticeably. Players start conserving endowment for themselves; because punishment is costly and less useful as a deterrent late, sanctions are left mostly unused.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round one-shot-style public goods game with 13 anonymous players each round receiving 20 coins and choosing an integer contribution (0–20). The group pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so each contributed coin returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to each player. Because a player loses 1 coin for every coin they contribute while receiving only ≈0.623 back per contributed coin, the individual (selfish) incentive is to contribute 0; the group (social) optimum is for everyone to contribute 20. Players only see their own round net payoff (not others’ contributions or payoffs), so they must infer others’ behavior from their own payoff and adjust beliefs across rounds.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often show moderate contributions from conditional cooperators or those initially inclined to cooperate; a few selfish players free-ride from the start.
- Because players only see their own net payoff, high personal payoff signals (ambiguously) that others likely contributed, which encourages free-riding in subsequent rounds; low personal payoff suggests others contributed little, which may encourage stepping up for some conditional cooperators.
- Over repeated rounds, typical behavior is a decline in average contributions (learning and end-game effects), with a sharper drop toward the final rounds as players anticipate endgame defection.
- Individual differences from the provided personas will shape heterogeneity: some players (e.g., SLOTH, CHICKEN) start relatively cooperative and adapt; others (e.g., DUCK, OWL, SNAKE) contribute little throughout. Some (PARROT) may anchor to a low stable value, occasionally nudging up, while a few (MOOSE, GORILLA, CROCODILE) take intermediate, slowly declining paths.

Below I simulate a plausible sequence (round-by-round) consistent with these incentives and the player personas. Each round shows (1) a short explanation for expected updating and incentives and (2) the contribution vector in the exact player order, followed by the net payoff each player would see that round (their private round payoff only).

As the sequence of moderately high personal payoffs continues for some, free-riding becomes more attractive; conditional cooperators begin to scale back further. Some low-contributors remain at zero; a few small positive contributions persist from players trying to encourage cooperation.

Signs of declining group contributions accumulate. Some players who briefly experimented with giving small amounts now reduce further. A few hold moderate contributions out of habit or fairness preference, but the average continues to drop.

Players continue to glean information only from their own payoffs. Many see that keeping coins yields decent payoffs and reduce or stop contributing; a small set of consistent cooperators still give modest amounts.

Mid-game; many players converge toward low contributions as expected returns from contributing remain lower than holding. Persistent low givers maintain token amounts; hard-line free-riders remain at zero.
Contributions continue to fall; those who value fairness give small symbolic amounts (1–3), while many give zero. Players increasingly treat the game as one where free-riding is typical.

By now most players have low expectations of others’ giving. Very small contributions persist for a few (symbolic or experimental), but the average is low. Some players who prefer cooperation might still give modestly.

Almost everyone expects final defection and acts accordingly; contributions are essentially zero, with perhaps one player making a last symbolic contribution.
Final round: standard backward-induction/endgame effect leads virtually everyone to defect (contribute 0); there is no future to incentivize cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution 0–20 to a shared pot; the sum is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. Because returns to the group are higher than the private endowment, cooperating (contributing) can raise everyone's payoff — but the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute less) if others contribute. Players do not see others' contributions directly but can infer the group's total from their own round payoff; they can also communicate via chat. Typical dynamics: an initial period of moderate cooperation (some pledges, conditional cooperation) followed by declines as players detect lower total contributions and respond by cutting their own contributions. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, behavior often stabilizes (rather than full last-round collapse), with conditional cooperators trying to sustain cooperation and some persistent free-riders keeping averages down. Messages (when present) tend to be sparse and used either to pledge cooperation or to signal dissatisfaction when payoffs fall.

Players do not see others' contributions directly but can infer the group's total from their own round payoff; they can also communicate via chat. Typical dynamics: an initial period of moderate cooperation (some pledges, conditional cooperation) followed by declines as players detect lower total contributions and respond by cutting their own contributions. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, behavior often stabilizes (rather than full last-round collapse), with conditional cooperators trying to sustain cooperation and some persistent free-riders keeping averages down. Messages (when present) tend to be sparse and used either to pledge cooperation or to signal dissatisfaction when payoffs fall.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players who each receive 20 coins every round. Each coin contributed to the public pot is multiplied by 6.6 and then split equally among all 10 players, so each contributed coin returns 0.66 to its contributor through the public pot but costs 1 privately. That creates a classic tension: individual incentives favor free-riding (keep the coin) while group payoff is maximized by contributing. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they may treat early rounds as if the interaction will continue, which encourages conditional cooperation and occasional enforcement through costly punishment.

Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) and much costlier to the punished (5 coins per unit), and players can see who punished them. This encourages selective, targeted punishment to enforce norms (punish low contributors) but also opens the door to retaliatory or overzealous punishment. Real groups typically use punishment sparingly: a few players who care about cooperation will target clear free-riders to keep contributions from collapsing, while others exploit the public good or reduce contributions over time. Messages are used rarely but can be consequential (calling out defection, promising to punish next round, or complaining about being punished).

Given the player personas, I expect a group that starts moderately cooperative (many players contributing mid-range amounts), with a few low contributors. Early targeted punishments will appear against clear under-contributors; that will produce short-term compliance from some. Over rounds contributions will tend to drift downward overall as free-riding spreads and punishment is costly and used sparingly. A few players (those with higher pro-social priors like CHICK, DUCK, RABBIT) will keep contributing more and will punish obvious shirkers repeatedly; a few (GORILLA, OWL, DOG) will be recurrent low contributors and occasional targets. Chats will be infrequent and focused (calls to punish, explanations or short complaints).

game_explanation:
This 13-player repeated public goods game pits private incentives (each coin kept yields a sure private benefit of 1) against a group return (each coin in the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across all players). Individually, contributing costs 1 while returning about 0.623 to the contributor (8.1/13), so free-riding is privately dominant, but group payoffs rise with contributions. Because players do not see others' choices before deciding but do see their own net payoff after each round, they can infer the group's average contribution from their round payoff (given they know their own contribution). Repetition and the expectation of reciprocal contributions often support positive contributions early on; however, the known finite horizon (16 rounds) and observed declines in inferred others' generosity typically produce a gradual decay in contributions and a steeper drop toward the end (an “end-game” effect).

Player personas give additional priors: some players (e.g., RABBIT, PARROT, SLOTH, DUCK) have higher purchase/cooperation tendencies or are more willing to search for strategies and so are more likely to start with sizable contributions and reduce them slowly if they infer low reciprocation. Others (GORILLA, MOOSE, CROCODILE) have low purchase rates and are likely to free-ride almost always. High “anchor pull” players (RABBIT, SNAKE, OWL) tend to stick close to prior actions, producing inertia in their contributions. Combined, I predict moderate initial cooperation that decays over time as players infer mediocre group returns and, as the end approaches, contributions collapse toward zero.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public-goods game with an MPCR = 6.6/10 = 0.66 per coin returned to each player. Because 0.66 < 1, the one-shot selfish incentive is to contribute 0; however, group payoffs are maximized when everyone contributes more. In practice we expect an initial mix of conditional cooperators (who give moderately hoping others reciprocate) and free-riders (who give little or nothing). Because players only see their own round payoff (not others' contributions) and rounds length is unknown, cheap talk (messages) can sometimes sustain cooperation early but is nonbinding; once players infer (via low personal payoffs or quiet responses) that others are free-riding, contributions usually decay toward zero. Some players with strong pro-social priors (here, MOOSE, DUCK, CHICK, OWL, SLOTH to various degrees) will keep trying small contributions longer; low-cooperative types (PARROT, RABBIT) will mainly free-ride. Expect sparse, purpose-driven chat (pledges, complaints), initially supporting cooperation and later expressions of resignation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, private endowments of 20 coins each round, and the opportunity to punish after seeing contributions. The social return to each contributed coin (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to each player) is positive for the group but negative for the individual: contributing is collectively efficient but individually costly. That creates an incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) unless players expect others to cooperate or use punishment to enforce norms.

Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation as many players test the waters; free-riders get exposed and may be targeted by punishment. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) and expensive for the target (7 coins per unit deducted), so real players punish sparingly — enough to signal and deter repeated defection but not continuously. Because players learn from private summary information and can identify who punished them, small cycles of retaliation and targeted enforcement can appear. Toward the final rounds, backward-induction and endgame effects typically reduce cooperation: punishment becomes less effective (and less attractive) when the future is short, so contributions often decline near the end.

I therefore predict moderate initial contributions from many players (8–16), low contributions from more selfish types (often 0–4), occasional targeted punishments against repeat low contributors, some temporary enforcement raising cooperation mid-game, and a gradual decline in contributions as the horizon approaches. Punishment events will be infrequent and small (mostly single units), concentrated on persistent low contributors rather than broad, repeated punitive attacks.
