game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits 13 players (each with 20 coins per round) against the temptation to free-ride. The private marginal return on giving one coin (8.1/13 ≈ 0.62) is less than 1, so the selfish short-run incentive is to contribute 0. However, with repeated interaction and the ability to punish (costly to sender, harmful to target), expect early rounds to show some cooperation from those predisposed to prosocial choices and active punishers targeting obvious free-riders. Punishment is costly and identifiable, so it will be used sparingly to discipline persistent low contributors; punished players may raise contributions or retaliate in a few cases. Over time, contributions commonly drift downward as the horizon shortens (end-game effect), and punishment becomes less frequent near the end because the deterrence value falls. Given the personalities: SLOTH, CHICKEN, OWL are relatively likely to contribute and to punish low contributors; PARROT, CROCODILE, DOG, CHICK and some others are more likely to under-contribute early and be targets. I therefore predict moderate-to-high contributions early, targeted low-frequency punishment aimed at persistent free-riders, occasional short retaliations, and a gradual decline in contributions (and less punishment) as rounds progress toward round 16.

game_explanation:
This public goods game will likely show a mix of experimentation, conditional cooperation, and occasional targeted punishment to enforce norms. Early rounds usually feature diverse contributions as players test each other's tendencies. Some players (the high cooperators) will consistently contribute a lot; a few (the freeriders) will contribute little or none, inviting selective punishment from conditional cooperators. Punishment is costly to send, so it will be used sparingly and mostly to discourage repeated low contributions. Sparse chat will serve as light coordination and signaling (requests to contribute, brief acknowledgements, or calls for punishment). Over time contributions should converge toward moderate-to-high levels for most players, with intermittent defections and targeted punishments keeping overall cooperation relatively stable.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated 16-round public goods game with 13 players, a high multiplier (8.1) but the payoff per player from one coin contributed is less than 1 (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623). That creates the classic tension: full-group efficiency favors high contributions, but each individual has a material incentive to free-ride on others' contributions. Because players only see their own net payoff (not others' contributions), they can infer group behavior only indirectly from their payoff changes. Early rounds typically start with a mix of moderate contributions from those predisposed to cooperate and lower contributions from more selfish or insensitive players. When players observe large round payoffs, selfish incentives can produce a drift toward lower contributions in subsequent rounds; when payoffs fall, a minority of more cooperative types may sustain contributions, but without explicit communication the group rarely re-establishes full cooperation. Expect heterogeneity across personas: a few (e.g., SNAKE, MOOSE) tend to contribute more initially and remain relatively more cooperative; many (PARROT, DOG, CHICKEN, CROCODILE) start low and stay low; some (SLOTH, GORILLA, CHICK) show moderate/mixed contributions and adjust based on perceived group payoff. Over 16 rounds I predict an initial moderate aggregate contribution followed by a gradual decline toward low contributions, with occasional small rebounds from the more cooperative players.

With no prior feedback, expect a mixed opening with a fairly high total because a subset is willing to provision the public good.

Players observe only their own net payoff from round 1. High payoffs for many will reveal substantial aggregate contributions, incentivizing some to free-ride. Expect a drop from those sensitive to selfish incentives (CHICKEN, PARROT, DOG) and moderate trimming from others; cooperators (SNAKE, MOOSE) reduce little.

After seeing lower (but still above-endowment) payoffs, players who are more selfish continue lowering contributions. Framing-sensitive or norm-aware players (SLOTH, GORILLA) moderate downward rather than disappearing. SNAKE keeps contributing comparatively more.

With only private payoff feedback, learning is noisy. Some players test slightly higher or stable contributions (SLOTH, MOOSE) hoping for better group outcomes; others continue to cut back as free-riding looks attractive.

As individual net payoffs fall compared to early rounds, many interpret the trend as others reducing contributions and respond by reducing theirs further. Cooperators taper contributions but do not vanish entirely.

Patterns of decline continue. Players with low prior willingness to buy or contribute remain at or near zero. Moderates (GORILLA, MOOSE, SLOTH, CHICK) hover at small positive contributions to test whether group production rebounds.

By now many players have observed diminishing round payoffs and infer lower aggregate contributions; most respond by further cutting their own contribution. A small core (SNAKE, a bit of MOOSE) still contributes noticeably.

Contributions stabilize at a lower level. Some players who are framing- or anchor-sensitive (SLOTH, CROCODILE to some extent) might try tiny adjustments, but absent clear public signals, the trend is broadly flat or slightly down.

Free-riding becomes the dominant pattern: many contribute zero or near-zero. The more prosocial SNAKE continues to contribute a meaningful positive amount, while a few others keep token contributions.

With multiple rounds of low personal payoffs, the incentive to cut contributions further is strong. A handful (MOOSE, SNAKE) still provide small support for the public pot; most have converged to minimal giving.

Sustained low group investment makes further cooperation implausible without communication. Some modest contributors reduce to near-zero but a small residual remains among the more cooperative personas.

Endgame effects start to appear: players who were holding small positive contributions may lower them slightly to capture a last-round personal advantage, but full free-riding is not universal because a few keep contributing nominal amounts out of preference or habit.

As the horizon shortens, strategic selfishness rises: many forego contributions to maximize their private final-period payoff. A small set of players (notably SNAKE and MOOSE) still place a small value on contributing.
Late-game moves further reduce contributions. Even some moderate contributors trim down further. Expect very small total group contributions.
With one round left after this, nearly everyone shifts to securing private payoffs. Small residual giving remains from habitual or intrinsically cooperative players.
Final round: no future rounds to incentivize reciprocity, so most contribute zero. A very small number give a token amount out of intrinsic preference or habit, but aggregate contribution is minimal.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public‑goods game with costly punishment. Every round players decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied (6.6×) and split equally, so there is a clear social return to contributing but an individual incentive to free‑ride. Because players don't know how many rounds remain, standard dynamics are likely: early rounds see moderate cooperation as players test one another; occasional low contributions attract targeted punishment from norm‑enforcers; punishment is costly to both sender and receiver and is used sparingly to discourage persistent free‑riding. Communication is allowed, so short messages (“please contribute”, “why did you punish me?”) will appear occasionally and can shift contributions a bit — particularly for players who are framing‑ or anchor‑sensitive.

Uncertainties: who will enforce the norm (some players have a stronger tendency to punish), whether punished players will retaliate, and whether cooperation will drift downward from fatigue. Expect most players to contribute around the group mean (≈10) on many rounds; a few lower contributions (0–6 or similar) will be punished occasionally by one or two players (typically 1–2 punishment units). Punishment events remain sparse because they cost the punisher 2 coins per unit and only remove 5 coins per unit from the target.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with ten players, 20 coins each round, and a group multiplier of 6.6. Each coin contributed yields 6.6/10 = 0.66 back to every player, so the private marginal return on any single coin is 0.66 (less than 1). Individually, the dominant action each round is to contribute 0 (free-ride), but the group-maximizing outcome is full contribution. Because players do not observe others' contributions and only see their own net payoff after each round, information about peers is noisy and indirect; players can only infer others' behavior from their own payoff and any voluntary messages.

Given the provided personas, I predict an initial phase of moderate-to-high contributions from more cooperative players (DOG, RABBIT, MOOSE, SLOTH, PARROT), some early free-riding by more selfish or cautious players (DUCK, CHICK, CHICKEN), and a gradual drift downward as free-riding is rewarded. Occasional messages—sparse and mainly appeals to reciprocity or short payoff reports—will produce short-lived recoveries when touted by respected cooperators (DOG, RABBIT). Because players never know the total number of rounds, there will be no explicit endgame collapse, but contributions will fluctuate and trend lower, with a few cooperative rebounds mid-game when players attempt to restart cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round each player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from an endowment of 20. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 8.1, and split equally among all 13 players. Players only see their own net payoff each round (the transcript below reports those payoffs for the reader). Because actions are private each round, players must infer others' behavior from how their own payoff changes over time. Typical dynamics in this setup: early rounds often show moderate cooperation from some players who have pro-social tendencies; free-riders (who contribute little) reap high payoffs and often stick to low contributions; conditional cooperators reduce contributions once they observe lower returns; and toward the end (final rounds) behavior can either collapse toward free-riding (no future punishment) or show last-round bursts of generosity from a few. Given the player personas, I predict a moderate initial average contribution driven by a subset of pro-social players (DUCK, CHICK, CROCODILE, CHICKEN, PARROT) while several others (GORILLA, OWL, COW, and some low-buy-rate personas) contribute little. Over rounds the average contribution gradually declines as the private incentive to free-ride becomes salient; near the final round a small uptick is possible from a few socially-motivated players but many will still free-ride.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, fixed roles, and costly peer punishment. Each coin a player contributes benefits the group (multiplied by 8.1 then split among 13), but returns less than 1 coin to the contributor, so the one-shot selfish incentive is to contribute 0. Punishment allows cooperators to sanction low contributors, partially offsetting the free-rider incentive by imposing a large cost on targets (7 coins deducted per punishment unit) at a moderate cost to punishers (3 coins per unit). Because the game has a known finite horizon (16 rounds), we expect contributions to start at moderate levels (some social preference/reciprocity and enforcement) and then trend downward as the end approaches. Early on, a few players who are inclined to cooperate (Parrot, Snake, Duck, Moose) will contribute highly and occasionally punish obvious free-riders (very low contributors). Punishment will be used sparingly — typically one or two units directed at persistent low contributors — because punishment is costly. When players are punished and can see who punished them, they usually increase contributions to avoid future sanctions; occasional retaliatory punishments are possible but should remain rare. In later rounds contributions decline overall and punishment becomes less frequent, culminating in very low contributions (often zero) in the final round due to backward induction.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This public goods game gives each player 20 coins per round; contributions are socially efficient (everyone benefits if people contribute) but individually costly (each coin returned only 0.66 to the contributor). With punishment available (each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 and reduces the target by 5), we should expect an initial mix of contributions, some early low contributions by inherently more "selfish" or inattentive players, and a handful of targeted sanctions by more norm-enforcing or high-framing-sensitivity players. Punishment will be used sparingly because it's costly, but when used it will mainly target clear undercontributors (low contributions relative to the group). Chat will be limited and pragmatic: short appeals to contribute, a few warnings, and occasional acknowledgments of punishment. Over time, punishment and messaging should increase average contributions as players converge on a cooperative norm, though occasional lapses (or final-round temptations) may provoke targeted sanctions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their personal 20-coin endowment. The group pot multiplies total contributions by 8.1 and splits that amount equally across the 13 players, so the public return to each player is 8.1*(sum of contributions)/13. Players only see their own round net payoff at the summary stage (so they can infer the public-share value from their own choice and round payoff), but they never see peers' individual contributions or payoffs directly.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- The social-welfare maximizing move is to contribute everything (20), since the multiplier >1 and benefits are large; however, free-riding is privately attractive: regardless of others, each player keeps 1 coin for themselves for each coin they keep rather than contribute (but they still receive a fraction of the public return).
- Early rounds typically show heterogeneous behavior: some players start moderately high (conditional cooperators or prosocial types), while others start low (selfish or anchor-driven types). Because players only see their own payoff, they can deduce the group's total contribution from that payoff and will adjust in later rounds — so conditional cooperation and learning can produce an initial decline in contributions if some players free-ride.
- Over repeated rounds participants often converge toward lower contributions as they respond to inferred free-riding, but some players (with strong pro-social priors) may keep contributing a little or even push up contributions near the end (final-round deviations are possible because there are no future consequences).
- Persona priors suggest: CHICK and RABBIT are more likely to give relatively high amounts; PARROT may respond to framing and give moderately early; GORILLA and CROCODILE (high anchor pull) may stick to low anchors or small fixed contributions; DOG and CHICKEN are likely low; SLOTH and MOOSE likely moderate but trending down; OWL may be consistent with a strong anchor (could be low or match an early anchor); others like DUCK, SNAKE, COW moderate-to-low.

Below I simulate a plausible play path across 16 rounds: early moderate contributions by some, a gradual decline as inferred free-riding accumulates, occasional small rebounds from cooperative types, and some endgame fluctuations.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits short-run self-interest (keeping your 20 coins) against a collective benefit (every coin put in the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and then shared, giving each player 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62 back per contributed coin). Individually the marginal return per coin < 1, so pure selfish logic predicts zero contributions; but groups commonly sustain intermediate-to-high cooperation through social preferences and by using costly punishment to deter free-riding. Because punishment is visible to targets (they learn who punished them) and costly to send (3 coins per punishment unit, −7 to the target), players will use it sparingly — typically a few low-level sanctions aimed at persistent low contributors rather than mass retaliation.

Expectations and likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds usually show moderate cooperation as players test each other. First-round punishment is often low or absent (no history yet), but occasionally some players immediately sanction obvious low contributions.
- If a few players free-ride repeatedly, others will occasionally punish to raise contributions; these sanctions are small (1–2 units) because punishment is costly.
- Punishment can raise average contributions in middle rounds, but toward the end (last few rounds) cooperation tends to unravel (endgame effect): contributions decline and punishment effort often drops because deterrence value falls.
- There is uncertainty about who will be willing to punish consistently: some avatars (based on provided personas) lean more toward enforcement; others are more inclined to defect or respond to framing. Overall, expect a mid-game peak in cooperation sustained by intermittent punishment, followed by a gradual decline in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot-style public goods game where players do not know how many rounds remain. Each round everyone gets 20 coins, contributes some number (0–20) to a shared pot, the pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, and then players may pay to punish others. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) and much harsher to the target (5 coins per unit). Because players don't know the horizon, early rounds are often used to probe others' norms; low contributions tend to be punished by cooperators who want to enforce contribution norms. Conversely, punishers pay a cost to raise future contributions or to retaliate, so punishment remains sparse and targeted in most groups.

Given the provided personas, I expect:
- A mix of cooperators (DUCK, DOG, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK) and conditional/coordinating types (CHICKEN, MOOSE, PARROT) to contribute moderate-to-high amounts most rounds.
- A few players with lazy/low-contribution tendencies (SLOTH, GORILLA at times) will sometimes under-contribute early and get targeted with limited punishment.
- Punishment will be used sparingly to signal disapproval and enforce norms; chat messages will be brief and mostly used to threaten or to apologize/acknowledge punishment.
- Over the 19 rounds we should see some early enforcement (punishments aimed at obvious free-riders), followed by general convergence toward more stable contributions with occasional one-off defections and short, targeted punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a standard finite-horizon-style public goods game except players do not know when it will end. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20. The group pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among the ten active players, so each coin contributed returns 0.66 to each contributor via the public good but costs 1 privately. That makes free-riding (contributing 0) the individually dominant action in any single round, while group-optimal outcomes require substantial positive contributions. Because players do not know the end point, we often expect an initial period of moderate cooperation (some players try to establish norms or anchor contributions), followed by gradual decay in contributions as incentives to free-ride dominate and noisy evidence accumulates. Sparse messages can sometimes stabilize cooperation if persuasive and trusted players commit early; conversely, visible low contributions promote further decline. Given the provided personas, a few players with higher prosocial/“purchase” tendencies (GORILLA, CHICKEN, DOG, CHICK, possibly DUCK/OWL) are likely to contribute more early on, while low-purchase-rate players (PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT) and more positional “slothful” or anchoring profiles may contribute little. Expect overall contributions to decline across rounds, with occasional attempts to restart cooperation via messages.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, a strong group multiplier (6.6) but a private return of 0.66 per coin contributed. Individually, contributing is costly (you lose 0.34 per coin) so the one-shot Nash is to contribute 0. Over repeated interaction, cooperation can appear if players coordinate or reciprocate, but here players do not see others' contributions directly — they only observe their own net payoff each round. Because each player can infer the group's total contribution from their own payoff (they know their contribution), information about aggregate behavior will gradually accumulate, enabling conditional responses (e.g., "I gave 10, got X, others must have given a lot, so I can free-ride" or "I gave 10, got little, so others must have given little — I'll reduce"). Expect the following likely dynamics:

- Early rounds: Some players with cooperative tendencies or who respond to proposed anchors (high anchor-pull personas) will try moderate-to-high contributions and/or suggest coordinated levels in chat. Others who are more self-interested or low purchase-yes will contribute 0 from the start.
- Rapid learning & exploitation: Those who contribute little but infer a high group total will receive large payoffs and thus have incentives to keep free-riding. Contributors who see they are worse off will cut contributions in subsequent rounds.
- Collapse toward low contributions: As contributors are exploited, many will lower or stop contributing. Occasional proposals or anchor-driven attempts to rebuild cooperation (from high-anchor or high-framing players) can create small temporary spikes, but sustained cooperation is unlikely without reliable enforcement and because players only see private payoffs (not individual contributions).
- Sparse chat: Real subjects rarely chat often; expect a few purposeful messages — proposals for a contribution level, brief complaints or "I'll try again" statements, occasional boasts from free-riders.
- Uncertainty about length: Because players do not know how many rounds remain, there is no predictable final-round collapse, though some players might attempt renewed coordination late in the sequence based on norms or anchor influence.

Following these expectations, the transcript below simulates rounds 1–19 in order. Each round begins with an explanation, then any short chat, the contributions in the fixed avatar order, and then the summary stage with sparse chat.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game (16 rounds) where each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across the 13 players, so a single coin contributed yields 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62 to each player — less than the 1 coin cost to the contributor. The dominant private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) while hoping others contribute; the group incentive is to contribute more. Because contributions are revealed each round and players can punish identified individuals (cost 3 per punishment unit to punisher and −7 per unit to the punished), punishment can sustain higher contributions by imposing a cost on low contributors, but punishment itself is costly and may provoke retaliation.

Key dynamics I expect:
- Early rounds: conditional cooperation — many players start with moderate-to-high contributions, while a few test by contributing little. Those who are harmed by low contributors may use small punishments to signal disapproval.
- Mid game: contributions tend to drift downward unless punishment is used occasionally to discipline persistent free-riders. Punishment is typically used sparingly (1–2 units), because it is costly.
- Retaliation and identification: because punished players learn who punished them, some retaliatory punishment can occur, producing tit-for-tat pairs, but these are usually small and intermittent.
- End game (last few rounds): forward-looking incentives disappear, so contributions tend to fall (many players free-ride). Punishment may also decline (because it no longer affects future behavior) or it may be used as spite in a few cases.

Uncertainties: exact contributions and who punishes whom are stochastic — different players have different priors and tendencies. Some players (e.g., RABBIT, MOOSE, CHICK) are more likely to contribute generously; others (SNAKE, CROCODILE, SLOTH in our simulation) are more likely to under-contribute. Punishment is predicted to be occasional, targeted, and small in magnitude.

<PLAYERS> COW, SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20; the group pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally, so each coin a player contributes costs them 1 but returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to their own payoff. The social optimum is full contribution, but the private marginal return is below 1, so a purely selfish best-response (given beliefs about others) is to contribute 0. Because players do not observe others' contributions and only see their own net payoff each round, social feedback is limited: players can infer something about total contributions from their own payoff (since they know their own contribution), but they cannot directly observe peers' actions. That makes coordination harder and tends to produce declining contributions over time as people experiment and move toward safer, selfish choices — especially once they infer that average contributions are low.

Heterogeneity in initial behavior is expected because player priors and heuristics differ. Some players (more prosocial/experimenting types) may start with higher contributions, others with low or zero. Over rounds we expect an overall downward drift in contributions, with occasional small experiments upward; the last round typically sees no strategic reciprocity incentives, so contributions usually fall further (an endgame reduction). Because summaries show only each player's own net payoff, responses will be somewhat myopic and noisy rather than sharply conditional on group averages.

Learning and risk aversion push many contributions lower; some players drop to zero while others keep small positive contributions to test stability.
As evidence accumulates that many peers contribute little, average contributions fall further; some players maintain small contributions out of habit or fairness preference.
Many players now contribute very little or zero; average contributions and per-player returns decline accordingly. A few still give small amounts.

In the penultimate round contributions are typically very low; some players make a last small experiment but the dominant tendency is to conserve.
Final-round incentives remove future reciprocation concerns; many players contribute zero. A couple of players contribute 1 out of habit or slight prosocial preference, but average is very low.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a standard linear public goods game with a multiplier (6.6) that yields a per-coin group return of 0.66 to each contributor — less than the private cost of 1. So the one-shot dominant strategy is to contribute 0. However, with repeated play (and uncertainty about the number of rounds) conditional cooperation, social preferences, and messaging can sustain positive contributions early on. Expect a typical pattern: initially many players try modest-to-high contributions (especially those with higher prosocial priors), some low contributors hold back from the start, then the group gradually learns that others are free-riding and contributions drift downward. Sparse messages will appear: early cooperative prompts, occasional calls to punish or reward, and later attempts to rekindle cooperation. Because players only see their own net payoff each round (not others' contributions), coordination is harder and decay toward low contributions is likely but not monotonic — there will be occasional temporary upticks when cooperative players appeal to the group.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20; contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally. The marginal per-capita return (8.1 / 13 ≈ 0.62) is less than 1, so privately every coin contributed reduces that player's own payoff in that round. The equilibrium in a one-shot game is full free-riding (0 contributions). In repeated play, however, some players with cooperative or conditional tendencies may contribute early or respond to others, producing higher early contributions that typically decay over time as free-riding pays off and players anticipate end-game defection.

Uncertainties: players don't observe others' contributions before deciding and only see their own net payoff each round, which reduces available public feedback and can slow or obscure reciprocity. Given the supplied personas, I expect a modestly cooperative start (some participants like OWL, CHICKEN, PARROT, DOG are relatively more likely to contribute), followed by a gradual decline as free-riding spreads and the finite horizon (round 16) causes an end-game collapse to zero. Occasional small rebounds may occur when conditional cooperators try to raise contributions, but the overall trend is downward.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with optional costly punishment each round. With ten players and a multiplier of 6.6 split evenly, every coin put into the pot returns 0.66 coins to each player — so contributing benefits the group but every individual gets less back (0.66) than they paid (1), creating the classic social dilemma. Early rounds typically show heterogeneous behavior: some players experiment or free-ride (contribute little), others contribute more. When contributions are high, cooperators often use punishment selectively to discipline persistent low contributors; punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher and can provoke retaliation. Over many anonymous rounds (players do not know the total number of rounds), cooperation often rises if punishment is present and credible; if defection becomes common or punishment is perceived as costly or futile, contribution levels can drift down. Players who are repeatedly targeted may either increase contributions, retaliate, or withdraw (contribute less). Chat is usually sparse and used to request contributions, call out low contributors, or signal intent to punish. Below I simulate a plausible 19-round run using the given avatars and the personas as priors for motivations.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) from their 20-coin endowment. The total contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among all players; each player's round payoff is (20 − their contribution) + (8.1 * total contributions / 13). Because the per-coin private return is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 < 1, a purely selfish, myopic best response is to contribute 0 every round. However, players differ in tendencies (some are more likely to contribute or follow anchors), and players can infer group-level contributions from their private round payoff (they know their own contribution and can back out the shared pot). That means learning is possible: early cooperative moves can be punished by free-riding once individuals see that contributing reduces their private payoff, and behavior will likely drift toward low contributions over time. I therefore predict:
- Early rounds: mixed/moderate contributions (some high contributors driven by cooperation/anchors).
- Middle rounds: decline as players deduce the marginal private return < 1 and reduce contributions.
- Late rounds: near-zero contributions by most, with a few occasional small or symbolic contributions from the most cooperative/anchored players.

Below I show a round-by-round transcript. At the start of each round I explain the expectation for that round. After contributions I show the private summary each player would see (their own net payoff for that round). Peers' payoffs are not shown to players (but are listed here as each player's private view).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with costly peer punishment. Each round players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally. Because the multiplier (8.1) divided by the number of players (13) yields a return per contributed coin that is less than 1 for the individual, the selfish incentive is to contribute 0; the group-efficient move is to contribute more. With punishment available (costly to the punisher and costly to the punished), we should expect modest initial cooperation, occasional low contributors, and selective punishment aimed at those perceived as free-riding. Punishment is typically sparse because it is costly; it is used enough to discipline repeated low contributors but not so much that everyone wastes large amounts on retaliatory cycles. Over time contributions usually drift downward unless punishment stabilizes them; identification of who punished whom allows targeted deterrence (and occasional retaliation), while the finite horizon (16 rounds) encourages some decline toward the end. I'll simulate plausible, conservative choices and sparse, targeted punishment over the 16 rounds.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public‑goods game with 13 players, 20 coins each round. Each coin you keep yields 1 to you now; each coin you contribute increases the group pot which is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among the 13 players, so the per‑person return to the group from one contributed coin is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623. Because 0.623 < 1, each player individually gains by keeping coins, while the group as a whole would be better off if most players contributed. The punishment stage allows players to reduce others' payoffs at a private cost (3 coins per punishment unit) and impose a larger harm on the target (7 coins per unit). That makes punishment a potentially effective deterrent against low contributions, but it is costly for punishers and therefore used sparingly in typical experiments.

Given the personas and these incentives, I expect:
- Early rounds: many players start with moderate to fairly high contributions (near the social norm) while a few low‑contribution types (PARROT, DOG, MOOSE, sometimes SNAKE or CROCODILE) contribute less.
- Punishment will be used occasionally to discipline clear free‑riders (especially zeros or very low contributions). Punishers tend to be cooperative, norm‑sensitive players (e.g., SLOTH, OWL, RABBIT, CHICKEN).
- Because punishment is costly, it will be limited to a few units and usually only when a player's contribution is conspicuously low.
- Over time contributions may drift downwards, especially near the final rounds (end‑game effect). Punishment helps sustain cooperation in the middle rounds, but in the last round many players will reduce contributions and punishment largely disappears (no future rounds to influence).
- There is uncertainty: some players (high anchor or framing sensitivity) may react strongly to single punishments; some low cooperators may gradually increase after being punished, some may not. I simulate plausible gradual adjustments and occasional enforcement actions across 16 rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a standard finite-action public goods game with simultaneous contributions each round. Each player keeps 20 private coins and decides how many to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. Individually, every coin contributed returns 0.66 to each player (less than 1), so unilateral defection (giving 0) strictly dominates giving any positive amount. But because the multiplier is large, mutual cooperation would create much higher group payoffs. Key likely dynamics:

- Early rounds: some players will try to establish a cooperative norm (messages proposing contributions). Players with stronger pro-social priors or high "purchase-yes" cues (MOOSE, OWL, PARROT, DUCK) are likely to give relatively more at first. Some players with low stated willingness to buy or low anchor sensitivity (GORILLA, RABBIT) are likely to give less.
- Information: players do not see others' choices before deciding, but they do see their own round payoff at summary. From that payoff and their own contribution, analytically-minded players can back out total group contributions; some will do so and adjust. Those with high search/analysis tendencies (GORILLA, DOG, RABBIT, CHICK, CHICK) are more likely to infer totals and react.
- Communication: sparse, purposeful chat can sustain cooperation early (promises, calls for fairness). But because contributions are private, mistrust can grow if individual payoffs are lower than expected; free-riding tends to spread, producing gradual decay of contributions across rounds.
- Horizon uncertainty (players do not know how many rounds remain) tends to sustain cooperation relative to a known finite horizon, but only up to a point: repeated small defections and the temptation to free-ride will usually lead to a slow decline in contributions.
- End result prediction: initial moderate-to-high cooperation, a few explicit defectors, then declining contributions across rounds with occasional appeals to cooperate. A few steadfast cooperators (e.g., MOOSE, PARROT, OWL) will continue contributing more than others even late in the game.

Punishment lets players sanction others at personal cost (2 per punishment unit) that reduces targets by 5 per unit; this can sustain cooperation but is itself costly and typically used sparingly. Because players do not see others' contributions before deciding, social enforcement and messaging become important: vocal punishers can deter repeated free-riding, but punishing is expensive so most players punish only occasional or extreme deviations. Some players here (MOOSE, DUCK, OWL) lean toward steady cooperation; others (GORILLA, initially SLOTH) are more likely to free-ride. CHICKEN appears dispositionally enforcement-oriented and is likely to punish low contributors repeatedly; MOOSE will often support cooperation and punish obvious free-riders. Over repeated rounds (without knowing the horizon), we expect early moderate contributions, targeted punishments against persistent zeros, occasional retaliation, then a stable pattern: most contribute mid-to-high amounts while one or two persistent low contributors remain lightly punished rather than fully converted.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game gives each player 20 coins per round and a binary choice: contribute all (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 3.7, and split equally among the six players; after that stage players can pay to punish specific peers (each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and subtracts 5 coins from the punished player). Players also may send chat messages at any time. Crucially, players do not learn the total number of rounds while playing, so there is ongoing uncertainty about whether "end-game" behavior will appear.

Likely dynamics and incentives
- Incentive to free-ride: If other players contribute, an individual who contributes 0 keeps their 20 and still receives an equal share of the multiplied pot, so short-run payoff often favors free-riding unless punishment is sufficiently strong or coordinated.
- Punishment as norm enforcement: Because punishment is visible and punishers are identifiable, contributors who are willing to pay a small cost to punish can deter repeated free-riding. But punishment is costly to the punisher, and single punishments (a 5-coin hit) often do not fully offset the 20-coin gain from not contributing—so enforcement must be frequent or coordinated to be fully effective.
- Heterogeneous tendencies: Based on the provided personas, expect some players to be more stable cooperators (e.g., CHICKEN, PARROT, DUCK), some to be occasional defectors (GORILLA, DOG), and SLOTH to be slower to change but responsive to framing/punishment. That suggests early rounds with mixed contributions, selective punishment to signal norms, and movement toward more cooperation mid-game as punishments shape behavior; occasional tests or lapses will trigger sparse punishments. Chat will be sparse and typically used to signal expectations, apologize, or request cooperation.

Uncertainties
- How often players are willing to pay the cost to punish. If punishers are sparse, free-riding can remain profitable and stable.
- Whether any player retaliates (punish back) after being punished; retaliation would raise enforcement costs and could destabilize cooperation.
- Without round-count knowledge, no guaranteed last-round collapse—players may maintain cooperation as long as enforcement and reputational signals persist.

Now the simulated transcript (rounds 1–12).

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with voluntary positive rewards. Each round players choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so individual incentives favor free-riding but group payoffs rise with contributions. After contributions are revealed, players can spend 4 coins per reward unit to give 3 coins to another player (net loss of 1 coin economy-wide per unit). Messages are allowed and can shape expectations but contributions are chosen without seeing peers' current-round decisions.

Likely dynamics: some players with prosocial tendencies (or high framing sensitivity) will contribute substantial amounts and reward other contributors modestly to encourage cooperation. Players with low search/anchor tendencies and low pro-social cues (here GORILLA) are more likely to free-ride. Because rewards are costly to the giver and slightly inefficient overall, they tend to be used sparingly and mainly to reinforce high contributors rather than to punish free-riders. Knowing the game ends after 2 rounds, players may attempt to sustain cooperation in round 2 by rewarding reciprocators and withholding rewards from repeat free-riders.

Each round, players choose how many of their 20 private coins to contribute to a public pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly, so each contributed coin returns only about 0.367 to the contributor (2.2/6). That makes full free-riding (contribute 0) individually tempting, while group-efficient behavior requires substantial contributions. Because the game includes costly positive rewards (each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 3), players can reward high contributors to encourage cooperation, but rewards are expensive and typically sparse. Players see contributions when rewards are decided, so rewards are likely to target visibly cooperative players; free-riders who contribute 0 will typically receive little or no reward. With only two rounds, players mix short-run incentive to free-ride (especially late) with social preferences and reciprocity: those who contributed in round 1 and received rewards are likely to keep contributing in round 2, while free-riders who earned high payoffs may continue to contribute little. Because players cannot identify who rewarded them in the summary, reward anonymity reduces personal reciprocity but still allows targeted reward decisions based on observed contributions.

game_explanation:
This 6-player public goods game pits private withdrawals (what you keep) against contributions to a shared pot that is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. Incentives: keeping more yields a larger guaranteed private payoff that round; contributing more raises the shared return for everyone but is individually costly unless others also contribute. With repeated interaction over a known finite horizon (19 rounds), we expect early rounds to be exploratory — some players test cooperation while others free-ride. Players with cooperative priors (here: DUCK, PARROT, SLOTH to a degree) are likely to contribute relatively high early; those with low search/purchase signals (CHICKEN, DOG) tend to contribute little. GORILLA is middling and responsive to social signals.

The reward stage lets cooperators reward each other (positive reinforcement) at a private cost, which can sustain cooperation if used selectively. Because rewards are sparse and costly, expect only a few small positive rewards each round, primarily directed at visible high contributors. Chat will be sparse and strategic: occasional praise, short threats, or announcements. Over successive rounds, a gradual drift toward lower contributions is common unless rewards create sustained reciprocity; near the end (final rounds), incentives to free-ride strengthen and contributions often fall.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with 19 rounds, 6 players. Each round players choose how much of a 20-coin endowment to keep (withdraw) versus contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After payoffs from the pot, players can spend coins to punish (cost 2 per unit; target -3 per unit) or reward (cost 2 per unit; target +2 per unit) others. Players cannot see others' choices before deciding, and they cannot identify who punished/rewarded them in the summary stage.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds often show moderate cooperation as players test each other. Messages and small rewards help sustain cooperation; small punishments target clear free-riders.
- Because contributions are private before each round, players face uncertainty about others' moves; they use past behavior and cheap signals (chat, small punishes/rewards) to build expectations.
- Punishments and rewards are sparse in real games (they're costly to send). Expect occasional one-unit punishments aimed at repeated free-riders and occasional one-unit rewards for consistent contributors.
- Over many rounds, absent very strong enforcement, cooperation tends to decline (the "endgame" effect) as players anticipate fewer future repercussions.
- Personas imply some players are more likely to coordinate/chat (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT) and some tend to contribute less or buy less cooperation (SLOTH, DOG). CHICKEN may be sensitive to framing and sometimes enforces norms (moderate punishments).

I will simulate a plausible path across 19 rounds: moderate-to-high cooperation early with a few punishments aimed at low contributors (mostly DOG and sometimes SLOTH), occasional reciprocal small rewards among cooperators, and a gradual decline in contributions toward the end.

game_explanation: This is a two-round public goods game with costly rewards. Typical dynamics: in round 1 players have no direct history so choices reflect priors (some contribute moderately, some more, some less). Because contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and shared across six players, the social optimum is to contribute everything but the individual incentive is to free-ride; rewards provide a way to privately sanction or reinforce contributors. Chat can help build cooperation but is usually sparse. Players who expect reciprocity or who were rewarded in round 1 are likely to maintain or raise contributions in round 2; players who see weak returns or who pay reward costs may adjust downward or reward again selectively. Because players cannot identify who rewarded them, the signaling value of being rewarded is limited to motivating pro-social behavior rather than personal reciprocation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public-goods game with cheap talk. Individually, contributing 20 coins yields a personal loss (you give up 20 but get back only 20*(3.7/6) ≈ 12.33), so the one-shot dominant action is to free-ride (contribute 0). With communication and repeated interaction, conditional cooperation and coordination are possible: pro-social or trustful players (who send or heed promises) will attempt to sustain contributions, while exploitative players who consistently defect will be punished by others reducing their contributions in later rounds. Because players do not know the horizon, there is no deterministic end-game collapse — instead we expect oscillations: early rounds often have higher contributions if some players signal intent to cooperate; if a player defects and gains relative advantage, others may retaliate, lowering contributions in subsequent rounds. Given the provided personas (some strongly inclined to follow anchors or mimic, one consistently pro-social), I predict initial high cooperation from most, repeated exploitation by SLOTH (the persistent free-rider), brief punishment phases where some players withhold contributions, and intermittent attempts (usually by DOG and sometimes GORILLA/DUCK) to rebuild cooperation. Chat will be sparse and strategic (promises, short reprimands, occasional coordination attempts).

game_explanation:
This two-round public goods game creates a tension between private incentives (keep your 1 coin) and group benefits (each contributed coin returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.3667 to each player). Purely selfish play would be to contribute 0, but social preferences, focal anchors, and the possibility of punishment/reward can push people toward positive contributions. Punishment is costly to the sender (4 per unit) but very costly to the target (−10 per unit), so even a few punishments can strongly deter free-riding. Rewards are moderately generous to recipients (+3) while costing the sender 4, so they are used sparingly to reinforce cooperation.

Given the provided personas: some players (SLOTH, PARROT, DOG) are more likely to contribute moderately; others (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) have stronger priors toward low contributions. Players who contributed in round 1 and paid punishment costs may try to sustain cooperation in round 2; punished free-riders may increase contributions in round 2 to avoid further penalties. Because identities of punishers/rewarders are hidden, there is uncertainty about who will be targeted, which tends to moderate aggressive punishment but can encourage conditional increases in contributions after being sanctioned.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with punishment and reward. Each coin you contribute benefits the group (multiplied by 2.2 and split among 6), but your private return per coin is about 0.367 — so individually you keep more by not contributing, yet the group does better when people contribute. Because players cannot see others' decisions before choosing, there is uncertainty about how cooperative the group will be. Punishment (costly to the punisher, very costly to the target) and rewards (costly to the giver, beneficial to the receiver) allow conditional cooperation: people can sanction free-riders and reward contributors, which can sustain higher contributions. With only 2 rounds, incentives to free-ride strengthen in the final round (backward induction), but the presence of immediate retaliation/reward and reputational signaling through messages can still shift behavior in round 1 and cause conditional responses in round 2. Punishments and rewards are typically used sparingly; chat tends to be short and strategic.

This is a two-round public goods game with punishment and reward. Each coin you contribute benefits the group (multiplied by 2.2 and split among 6), but your private return per coin is about 0.367 — so individually you keep more by not contributing, yet the group does better when people contribute. Because players cannot see others' decisions before choosing, there is uncertainty about how cooperative the group will be. Punishment (costly to the punisher, very costly to the target) and rewards (costly to the giver, beneficial to the receiver) allow conditional cooperation: people can sanction free-riders and reward contributors, which can sustain higher contributions. With only 2 rounds, incentives to free-ride strengthen in the final round (backward induction), but the presence of immediate retaliation/reward and reputational signaling through messages can still shift behavior in round 1 and cause conditional responses in round 2. Punishments and rewards are typically used sparingly; chat tends to be short and strategic.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each round each player effectively chooses a contribution (0–20) from an initial 20 coins; the group pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions (but not who rewarded whom), players can spend costly reward units (each unit costs them 2 coins and gives the target 2 coins) to signal approval or encourage cooperation. Players also can send free-form chat messages at any time.

Incentives and likely dynamics:
- The marginal return from contributing a coin is 1.8/6 = 0.3 to yourself, less than the private value 1, so individually the dominant short-run incentive is to withdraw (contribute 0). However, group payoffs are higher when many contribute.
- Because the horizon is finite and known (19 rounds), we expect a decline in average contributions over time (endgame effect), unless sustained by social preferences or targeted reward signaling.
- Rewards are neutral for total welfare (cost to sender equals benefit to receiver), but they serve as social signals: cooperators often reward other cooperators to reinforce behavior; reciprocators may respond in later rounds.
- Given the player personas: PARROT and DOG are the most likely to sustain higher contributions and to send rewards; SLOTH and DUCK are conditional/heuristic responders and may start moderate then adapt; GORILLA and CHICKEN are more likely to free-ride or contribute little.
- Chat is usually sparse. Expect occasional short messages praising cooperation or calling out low contributions; reward actions will be sparse and targeted (a few 1–2 unit transfers to those perceived as cooperative).

Uncertainties:
- Players only see their own net payoff and reward activity in summaries, not who rewarded whom, so social learning is noisy.
- Some players may experiment early; others may be stubbornly selfish or cooperative, leading to heterogeneous patterns.

Given these priors, the play will likely show moderate initial cooperation with a few targeted rewards (mostly from PARROT and DOG), gradual decline in contributions as free-riding spreads, occasional stabilization attempts via rewards, and a general downward trend toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game (19 rounds) with 6 players. Each round players choose how much to contribute (0–20); the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly, then players can spend coins to punish (cost 2 to source, −3 to target) or reward (cost 2 to source, +2 to target). Key dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds: testing and signaling. Some players try cooperation; others experiment with free-riding. Because contributions are publicly visible before punishment/reward, norm-enforcers can sanction low contributors or reward high ones.
- Midgame: conditional cooperation emerges. Players who see rewards/punishments will typically adjust contributions; norm-enforcers (here SLOTH and CHICKEN) tend to sustain higher contributions and use punishment/reward sparsely to shape behavior.
- Late rounds (endgame): incentives to punish decline because there is no future gain; contributions tend to drift down. Some last-minute rewards can appear from players who maintain norms despite endgame.
- Uncertainty: which players will punish or reward is not revealed to targets; thus punishment/reward influences behavior via aggregate consequences rather than targeted reciprocity. Punishment is costly so it will be used only against repeated low contributions; rewards appear occasionally to reinforce cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated binary public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round players choose either to contribute their whole endowment (20) or nothing (0). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the six players, so an individual contributor receives less back from their own contribution than they forgo (20 invested yields about 12.33–37 back depending on how many contribute), creating a strong short-run incentive to free-ride. Punishment lets cooperators impose costs on free-riders (punisher pays 2 per unit; target loses 5 per unit), which can sustain cooperation across rounds despite the individual temptation to defect. Because players do not know when the game ends, we expect no backward-induction collapse; instead we expect:

- Early rounds: some mix of contribution and free-riding as players "test" each other.
- Punishment: cooperators will punish clear free-riders but do so sparingly (typically 1 unit), enough to make defecting less attractive. Punishment will tend to target repeat defectors.
- Mid-to-late rounds: cooperation tends to increase after punishment is applied, with occasional deviations (temptation or retaliation) followed by targeted punishment and restored cooperation.
- Chat will be sparse but used to call out or explain punishment or to signal intent.

Player tendencies (personas) make SLOTH, DUCK, and DOG likely to be reliable cooperators and punishers; GORILLA and PARROT are more likely to test or free-ride early; CHICKEN is moderately likely to experiment with defection but will respond to punishment.

Players who are punished tend to increase contributions in round 2; because the game ends after round 2, some backward-induction incentives reduce punishment in the final round, but players may still reward cooperators. Messaging is usually sparse and used to justify behavior or to signal intentions.

game_explanation:
This is a finite-but-unknown-horizon repeated public goods game with six players who each face the classic tension: contributing 20 to the group increases total group payoff (3.7 multiplier) but yields less than 1 coin of private return per coin contributed (3.7/6 ≈ 0.617). Individually, free-riding (keeping 20) is the dominant one-shot action; group welfare is maximized when everyone contributes. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they can try to build trust by sending messages and by reciprocating others' past contributions, but any successful cooperation is fragile: defectors earn noticeably more in rounds where others contribute, which creates an incentive for exploitation and then collapse.

Given the provided personas and typical dynamics, I predict the game will show initial cooperative attempts (some players signal willingness to contribute), early exploitation by a subset who consistently keep their 20, a fall in contributions as cooperators adapt, a few attempts to re-start cooperation via messages and small coalitions, and then cycles of brief cooperation and collapse. Chat will be sparse and purposeful — short promises, accusation/defense, and occasional coordination attempts. Payoffs at each summary show individual round earnings and peer payoffs; these observable differences drive behavioral adjustments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each round everyone starts with the same 20 coins in the shared pot and chooses privately how many to withdraw (equivalently, how much to contribute, an integer 0–20). The total contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally among all six players; each contributed coin returns 0.3 coins to each player (1.8/6 = 0.3). Individually, contributing is costly since one coin you keep privately is worth 1 to you while it returns only 0.3 to you via the group pot. So the classic individual incentive is to withdraw everything (contribute 0). But because players can reward others after seeing contributions, small reward transfers (each unit costs 2 coins to the sender and gives +2 coins to the recipient) can create incentives to support contributors.

Given the player priors, I expect heterogeneous behavior: a few players will favor higher contributions and try to sustain cooperation with sparse rewards (SLOTH, DOG, sometimes DUCK/PARROT), while some other players will contribute little (GORILLA, CHICKEN) and rarely reward. Rewards will be infrequent and targeted (mostly 1-unit gestures), often reciprocal between the same pair of pro-socials. Over rounds contributions slowly drift downward overall (typical in finite-horizon PGG), but the occasional targeted rewards keep a small cluster of contributors somewhat stable. Chat will be sparse — short commitments or thanks rather than full deliberation.

game_explanation:
This is a typical repeated public goods game with 6 players, MPCR = 1.8/6 = 0.30. Each coin contributed benefits the group but reduces the contributor's private payoff, so the dominant individual incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). Over a 19-round finite horizon we therefore expect an initial phase where some players experiment with moderate cooperation, occasional normative enforcement (punishment) and selective rewards to sustain contribution, followed by a gradual decline as the endgame approaches. Punishment is costly and used sparingly — typically by conditional cooperators (those who care about reciprocity or fairness). Rewards are also used but less frequently because they cost the giver. Some players (e.g., SLOTH) have priors that favor low engagement and will often contribute little; others (GORILLA, CHICKEN, DOG) are more likely to try sustaining cooperation early on. Messages will be sparse and usually aimed at encouraging contributions or calling out low effort. Because punishers cannot be identified by targets at summary stage, social signalling via chat and occasional public punishment are the primary enforcement tools.

game_explanation:
This is a six-person, repeated binary public-goods game with costly third-party punishment. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their full 20 coins to the group pot or keep all 20. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so the more contributors there are the larger each person’s share. Defectors who keep their 20 get an immediate private advantage (they retain 20 plus the equal share), so there is constant temptation to free-ride. Because players do not see others’ choices before deciding and they do not know the total number of rounds, we expect behavior to show some conditional cooperation: many will contribute when others are perceived to be cooperating, and switch to not contributing when they detect persistent free-riding.

Punishment allows contributors to impose costs on defectors (each punishment unit costs 2 to the sender and reduces the target by 5). In real games punishment is used sparingly and mainly as a deterrent — typically a few 1-unit punishments targeted at repeat defectors are enough to sustain relatively high cooperation. Some low-engagement players (here DOG and PARROT) will tend to free-ride early; after being punished they may move toward contributing. Some players (SLOTH, GORILLA, CHICKEN, DUCK) are more likely to be conditional cooperators and to punish when free-riding persists. Chats are infrequent and used to request cooperation or register displeasure.

Given these tendencies, the likely unfolding: early rounds show majority contributions with one or two defectors (DOG, PARROT). Contributors punish those defecting (single-unit punishments commonly). Punishment successfully nudges some defectors to cooperate in later rounds. There will be occasional lapses (especially DOG), producing targeted punishments. Overall contributions remain fairly high across the 12 rounds, with sparse, focused punishment and short chat exchanges.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous binary public-goods game with 6 players (each round each player either contributes 0 or 20). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly; so the per-player share when k people contribute is (74k)/6 ≈ 12.33k. Contributors for small k often earn less than non-contributors, so the dominant private incentive is to free-ride; but group-level payoffs are highest when many contribute. Because players do not observe choices before deciding and do not know the total number of rounds, cooperation tends to be fragile: some players will try to lead by promising or signaling contributions (and may occasionally do so), while others will test or exploit those signals. Expect an initial willingness from a few to contribute, followed by erosion as defectors earn higher immediate payoffs; occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation appear as short-lived clusters of contributions. Chat is available and will be used sparingly — mostly to state intentions, encourage others, or express disappointment after being exploited.

game_explanation:
This is a standard linear public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone privately chooses either to contribute their whole endowment (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally; because each player keeps any un-contributed coins, there is a private incentive to free-ride (contribute 0) while the group collectively benefits if people contribute. Key likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds often see some cooperative gestures (a few players contribute) if people hope others will reciprocate or if someone proposes coordination.
- Because contributors earn less than free-riders in the same round, observing payoffs that reward free-riding tends to reduce cooperation in later rounds unless communication or social preferences sustain it.
- Messages can revive cooperation temporarily (especially from socially-minded players), but without strong enforcement or repeated mutual reciprocity contributions tend to decline over time.
- Players do not know how many rounds remain, which can support repeated cooperation more than a known finite game, but repeated low payoffs for contributors usually discourage continued contributions.
Given the provided personas:
- SLOTH and CHICKEN are likely to default to free-riding or stay quiet; they respond little to prompts.
- DUCK and PARROT are relatively more willing to contribute and to try to coordinate.
- DOG is strongly influenced by anchors/messages and will often follow a visible suggestion to cooperate (or defect) depending on the group message.
- GORILLA is framing-sensitive and may try to coordinate early but will defect if cooperation looks unrewarding.
Expect an initial moderate level of cooperation, then a decline as contributors see they are earning less than free-riders, with occasional revival attempts via chat.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with punishment. Each round players choose to contribute their entire endowment (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally; punishment is costly to the sender (2 per unit) but removes 5 per unit from the target. Players do not know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: cooperators (high cooperative priors: DUCK, PARROT, maybe CHICKEN and DOG) will try contributing; low-cooperation priors (SLOTH) are likely to free-ride initially. Players who expect cooperation will punish occasional defectors to enforce norms. Punishment is used sparsely (1 unit usually) because it is costly.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is effective, defectors switch to contributing; cooperation stabilizes and punishment declines. If some players test the group (occasional defection), they are usually punished once and then return to cooperating.
- Late rounds: because players do not know the end, the same enforcement dynamic typically continues. Some occasional defections may reappear and attract punishment, though punishers may punish less often over time.
- Chat is sparse and used for short appeals or apologies.

We simulate 12 rounds with these tendencies: DUCK and PARROT are consistent cooperators and occasional punishers; GORILLA is moderately cooperative and will punish when defectors persist; CHICKEN is conditionally cooperative and may punish or defect once in a while; DOG tends to cooperate but may slip once; SLOTH is the most likely to test free-riding repeatedly and gets punished for it.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 anonymous players. Each round everyone chooses how many of the 20 coins to withdraw privately (so contribution = 20 - withdrawn). The group pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally (each player receives 0.3 times total contributions). After redistribution players may pay to punish (cost 2 per unit to source, -3 per unit to target) or reward (cost 2 per unit to source, +2 per unit to target). Players see only their own round summary (net payoff, what they spent on punish/reward, how much they lost to punishment, how much they received in reward). The game lasts 19 rounds and IDs of punishers/rewarders are not revealed.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds usually show moderate cooperation as players test norms. Some players will try high contributions to encourage reciprocity; others will test free-riding.
- Punishment and reward are used sparingly because they are costly to send. Punishment is typically used as a corrective in response to low contributions; reward is used to reinforce high contributors.
- Because actions are anonymous and players anticipate future rounds, occasional punishments or rewards can stabilize cooperation midgame. Toward the end (especially the final round) incentives to defect grow (no future rounds to influence) and contributions often fall, with less punishment because it’s less profitable to pay costs in the final round.
- Given the mixed personas, expect a few defectors occasionally (lower contributions) and occasional targeted punishments from norm-enforcing players (e.g., CHICKEN, PARROT), with intermittent rewards for consistent cooperators. Chat will be sparse and mostly used to cajole or complain.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated one-shot-style public goods game with six players who each choose 0 or 20 each round. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so any single player's marginal return from contributing (if others' behavior unchanged) is 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167 of the contributed amount — less than 1 — which creates a strong individual incentive to free-ride while group welfare is maximized when many contribute. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, we expect tempered endgame behavior: early rounds may see some attempts at cooperation (especially from players with higher pro-social/“purchase-yes” tendencies) followed by exploitation by consistent free-riders and then partial collapse to defection. Sparse chat lets cooperators try to coordinate; conditional cooperators (who see others contributing) may reciprocate, but repeated exploitation (contributors getting lower net payoffs than free-riders) will tend to drive down contributions over time. Expect cycles of small cooperation attempts followed by relapse to all-defect; payoffs will therefore show contributors earning less than free-riders in cooperative rounds, which motivates the later defections.

game_explanation:
This is a standard public-goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and an out-of-game costly punishment stage. The core tension: contributing (20) increases the group pot (each contribution adds 74 to the common pool, which is split equally), so contributors help everyone but personally forgo the private 20. Free-riding (contributing 0) keeps the private 20 and still grants the free rider a share of others' contributions. Because players can punish after seeing contributions and punishments are identifiable, cooperators can impose costs on defectors (punisher pays 2 per punishment unit; punished player loses 5 per unit). Messaging allows coordination and norm enforcement.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds will show mixed play as players test each other: some will contribute, some will free-ride to exploit early generosity.
- Punishment will be used sparingly but selectively against repeat free-riders. Because punishments are identifiable, they create reputational consequences (and risk retaliation), so punishments tend to be small (1 unit) and targeted.
- Over several rounds we should see cooperation stabilize as most players respond to punishment by contributing; occasional lapses (experimentation or spite) produce a few additional punishments.
- Chat will be used infrequently: short calls for cooperation, complaints after being punished, or brief thanks when cooperation stabilizes.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with costly punishment and costly rewards. Each player keeps any coins they don't contribute, but every coin contributed is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among the six players, so each contributed coin gives less than one coin back to the contributor personally (2.2/6 ≈ 0.367). That creates a tension: the group is better off if many contribute, but individual incentives favor contributing nothing.

Typical dynamics:
- Round 1: many players start with heterogeneous contributions (some cooperative, some free-ride). Punishment and reward are used sparingly; punishments target clear free-riders while rewards reinforce cooperators. Messages may try to set norms but are often vague.
- Round 2: players adjust: punished free-riders often increase contributions; punishers sustain or slightly reduce contributions; some retaliation can occur but is costly and thus limited. Overall contributions often rise modestly in round 2 after sanctions in round 1, but costly punishment can lower punishers' own payoffs.

Uncertainties:
- Chat promises are cheap; some stated intentions may not match actual contributions.
- Punishment/reward is anonymous; players may under- or over-react to perceived unfairness.
- Individual tendencies (personas) bias choices: some are predisposed to cooperate or to default to low effort; aggressive retaliation is possible but expensive so should remain limited.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits private incentives (each coin privately kept yields 1 to the keeper) against a collective return (contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly). Individually, contributing is costly: each coin contributed returns only 0.3 to each player, so contributing reduces a player's private payoff unless others also contribute. Over 19 rounds, we should expect a mix of strategies: some players (conditional cooperators) will contribute substantial amounts early and respond to others' behavior; some will free-ride opportunistically. Because players can punish (costly to the punisher; harsh to the target) and reward (costly but beneficial to the target), social enforcement can sustain higher contributions than one-shot Nash play would predict. However, punishment and reward are used sparingly in real experiments because they are costly and ambiguous (targets do not see who acted). That leads to targeted punishment of low contributors (to deter defection) and occasional rewards to signal appreciation. Communication, when present, is short — simple appeals or acknowledgements — and can influence conditional cooperators. Uncertainty about who punished or rewarded means players infer group norms indirectly from aggregate outcomes, so reputationally motivated enforcement is limited; punishment tends to be low-intensity but persistent by a few players.

Given the six-player mix and persona priors, I predict an initial modest-to-high contribution level by several players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT), a lower contribution by CHICKEN (early defector) and a middling one from DOG. Punishment will be used intermittently and narrowly (usually 1 unit) against repeat low contributors; rewards will be occasional to reinforce high contributors. Over rounds, defection will be deterred somewhat and average contributions should drift towards moderate cooperation, but occasional lapses and retaliatory downward adjustments will occur.
