game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with a modest multiplication factor (2.2) shared across six players. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot; contributions are multiplied and split equally. After payoffs are computed, players can spend costly reward units (cost 4 to giver, +3 to recipient) to incentivize others. Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Social vs. selfish tradeoff: contributing increases the group payoff but reduces your private holding that round. The multiply-and-split parameter (2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 per coin contributed) makes individual contributions privately costly but collectively beneficial.
- First round uncertainty: with only two rounds and no prior history, contributions are heterogeneous — some players (more pro-social or rule-following) tend to contribute moderate-to-high amounts; others free-ride. Messages (cheap talk) can nudge choices but are not fully credible.
- Reward dynamics: rewards are used sparingly. Players are likeliest to reward visible high contributors to encourage cooperation (reciprocity/positive reinforcement). Because rewards are costly, expect only a few 1-unit rewards rather than a dense reward network.
- End-game (round 2): knowing the game ends after round 2 tends to reduce contributions in the last round; however, players who were rewarded earlier or who care about fairness may keep contributing. Rewards in round 2 are still sparse and primarily reciprocal between cooperating players.
- Uncertainty: because players cannot identify who rewarded them and only see their own reward totals, strategic signaling through rewards is noisy; players may reward to encourage cooperation even if that reduces their own final payoff.

Given the six provided personas (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT), I predict mixed contributions in round 1 (some moderate/high, some low), a few targeted rewards to signal appreciation for contributors, and slightly reduced contributions in round 2 with a few reciprocal rewards among cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players and a marginal per-player return of 0.3 on each contributed coin (1.8/6). The one-shot incentive is to withdraw everything (contribute 0) because each coin you keep privately yields 1 to you, while contributing returns only 0.3 to you directly; however, contributions increase the group's pot and can be socially rewarded afterwards. Because the game lasts 19 rounds and rewards are costly (2 coins cost to give a 2-coin benefit), expect the usual pattern:

- Early rounds: some players who are pro-social or anchor-influenced start with moderately high contributions. They may send brief messages to encourage cooperation.
- Middle rounds: contributions may drift down as free-riding appears. Pro-social players may use occasional rewards to reinforce high contributors, but rewards are scarce because they are costly.
- Endgame (last several rounds): forward-looking players reduce contributions further (unraveling), and reward use declines sharply. Some players may attempt a late revival with messages and a few reciprocal rewards, but it's often limited.

Uncertainties: exact magnitudes depend on how persistent pro-social players are and how much reward they find worth the cost. Because rewards are anonymous in the summary, signaling via chat is more important for coordination than direct attribution of rewards. SLOTH and CHICKEN are more likely to contribute little and remain quiet; GORILLA, DOG, PARROT tend to be more cooperative and use rewards sometimes; DUCK is anchor-sensitive and may follow visible norms if a number is suggested.

This public goods game pits private incentives (keep coins) against a group return (the shared pot multiplies by 1.8 and is split equally). The social return per contributed coin to each player is 0.3, so individually people are tempted to withdraw everything. However, because the game runs for 19 known rounds and players can send costly positive rewards to others, we expect some cooperation early on supported by rewards and social signals, then a gradual decay as endgame effects and strategic free-riding dominate. Rewards, being costly to send (2 coins per reward unit), will be used sparingly — mainly to praise high contributors or reciprocate past kindness — and typically only by players who either value group outcomes or want to influence others. Uncertainties include how credibly players use rewards to sustain cooperation and whether early generosity is exploited; personas suggest a mix of low-search/inertia types (SLOTH, PARROT), strong anchor-followers (GORILLA, CHICKEN), and high-search/strategic players (DUCK, DOG), which yields modest initial cooperation followed by slow erosion and sparse reward activity.

This is a short (2-round) public goods game with six players. Each round players choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Because each coin contributed returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to its giver, the one-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Still, cooperative behavior can appear: some players have pro-social priors and may contribute expecting reciprocity or social approval. In round 2 players will adjust slightly based on round 1 payoffs and reward feedback — cooperators who were rewarded are likely to keep contributing; those who contributed but weren't rewarded may reduce contributions slightly; free-riders may continue to contribute little.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and divided equally, so contributions benefit the group but reduce the individual's private holdings. After payoffs are distributed, players may spend costly actions to punish (cost 4 to sender, -10 to target per unit) or reward (cost 4 to sender, +3 to target per unit) others. Communication is allowed and can influence expectations, but contributions are private at decision time. Because punishment and reward both reduce total group wealth (punishment reduces it substantially; rewards reduce it slightly), these actions are mostly strategic signals to shape behavior rather than welfare-improving.

Likely dynamics: Some players with pro-social priors (high anchoring and framing sensitivity) will contribute relatively high amounts and reward others; conditional or reputation-sensitive players will raise contributions after being punished or rewarded. Players who are less prosocial or price-sensitive will contribute less initially and may be targeted by sparse punishment. Punishment is typically used sparingly and mostly against clear low contributors; rewards are also sparse and used to reinforce high contributors or improvement. With only two rounds, players often use the first round to signal or test others, and the second round to adjust: low contributors (if punished) often increase, and high contributors often maintain their contributions and may reward improvement.

Players who are less prosocial or price-sensitive will contribute less initially and may be targeted by sparse punishment. Punishment is typically used sparingly and mostly against clear low contributors; rewards are also sparse and used to reinforce high contributors or improvement. With only two rounds, players often use the first round to signal or test others, and the second round to adjust: low contributors (if punished) often increase, and high contributors often maintain their contributions and may reward improvement.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits the short-run private incentive to keep your 20 coins (and therefore benefit from the others' contributions) against the long-run group benefit from mutual contributions (the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and shared). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), expect a mix of behavior: some players who lean toward cooperation will contribute early and consistently, while a few will try to free-ride. When contributors see free-riding, they have a credible enforcement tool: costly punishment that reduces the target's payoff (5 coins per unit) at a cost to the punisher (2 coins per unit). That tends to produce a pattern where (1) initial uncertainty yields some free-riding, (2) punishment appears sparsely to discipline repeat defectors, (3) punished players often switch to contributing to avoid future losses, and (4) cooperation stabilizes but occasional lapses still provoke targeted punishment. Because players don't know how many rounds remain, they are more likely to sustain cooperation once it looks enforceable.

Given the player personas:
- GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT are more likely to contribute and to enforce cooperation.
- DOG is the most likely to try free-riding early but will respond to punishment.
- SLOTH is inconsistent and may defect sporadically; when punished might retaliate once.
- CHICKEN is low-engagement and may occasionally defect.

Expect sparse chat (short admonitions, promises to contribute) and relatively limited punishment events targeted at identified defectors rather than mass retaliation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 19-round public goods game with a twist: players choose how many of the 20 coins to withdraw (so contributions are 20 - withdrawal). The collected contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally; rewards are costly signals (each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins), and rewards are anonymous to recipients. Because the game length is common knowledge (19 rounds), incentives evolve over time: early rounds typically favor cooperation because future reciprocity is possible; later rounds normally see more free-riding as the end approaches. Rewards can support cooperation by rewarding contributors and punishing (via omission) free-riders, but because rewarders are anonymous, reputational signaling is noisier. Uncertainties include who is actually rewarding (players only see amounts received, not sources), how persistent players are in rewarding, and how strongly players respond to others' apparent generosity.

Using the supplied personas: PARROT tends to be reliably cooperative and willing to reward; DOG is moderately cooperative and rewards sometimes; SLOTH contributes fairly often and will occasionally reward; GORILLA tends to freeride more and rarely rewards; DUCK follows anchors / group cues and contributes moderately; CHICKEN is sensitive to framing and may vary but leans toward lower contributions in many rounds. Expect moderate cooperation early, intermittent rewards targeted to visible contributors (even though anonymous), some retaliation via withholding rewards rather than explicit punishment, and gradual decline in contributions toward the final rounds with sporadic attempts (especially by PARROT and DOG) to stabilize cooperation via rewards and short messages.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with six players. Each round players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly. Individually, contributing reduces a player's private payoff (the individual return per coin is less than 1), but if many contribute the group payoff increases. After contributions are revealed and payoffs redistributed, players can spend costly reward units (4 coins each) to give benefits (3 coins per unit) to others. Because rewards are costly to give but beneficial to recipients, they are typically used sparingly to reinforce cooperation (reward cooperators) or sometimes to reciprocate previous rewards. With only two rounds, first-round behavior is partly driven by prior disposition (some will start cooperative, others selfish), and second-round behavior is influenced by what people saw in round 1: observed contributions and who gave rewards.

Given the player personas:
- Some (DOG, DUCK) are likely to contribute relatively high; others (GORILLA) are more likely to free-ride; SLOTH and PARROT may choose moderate contributions; CHICKEN may be modest or slightly increase after seeing others.
- Rewards will be sparse and targeted at clear cooperators. A small number of one-unit rewards is plausible; reciprocation across rounds is also plausible. Chat will be minimal and used to signal intentions or thank others.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with costly punishments and rewards available after contributions. Typical dynamics:
- In round 1, players are uncertain about others' intentions. Some will contribute moderately (prosocial types), some will free-ride. Because punishment/reward is available, cooperators may be willing to use a small amount of costly punishment to sanction clear free-riders and a small amount of costly reward to encourage cooperators. Messaging (cheap talk) can nudge behavior but is not binding.
- Between rounds, players update beliefs. Free-riders who were punished may raise contributions to avoid further sanctions, or they may continue free-riding if they expect punishment to be rare. Because the game lasts only 2 rounds, some backward-induction pressure pushes players toward lower contributions in round 2, but intrinsic cooperators may still contribute. Punishment and reward remain possible in round 2 but are usually sparser (players punish/reward sparingly, especially in the final round).
- Summaries are private: each player sees their own round net payoff, how much they spent on punishment/reward, how much was deducted from them by punishment, and how much they received via reward; they do not see who targeted whom.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with privately held endowments of 20 coins each round and an all-or-nothing contribution choice (0 or 20). The shared pot (20 per contributor) is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the 6 players, so each contributor increases the per-player return by 12.333... coins. Group-optimum is everyone contributing (each would get 74), but individual incentives favor free-riding: if others contribute, one person can defect and earn more that round. Players do not know the total number of rounds, which tends to reduce long-run cooperation (no guaranteed last round to sustain conditional cooperation). Players cannot see others' contributions before deciding, but at the end of each round everyone sees every player's net payoff for that round, so contributions can be partly inferred from payoffs. Because messages are allowed, promises and short appeals can influence behavior but are limited by strategic incentives.

Given the provided personas:
- PARROT (very pro-cooperation) will repeatedly pledge to contribute and typically follow through.
- DOG and CHICKEN have moderate tendencies to cooperate; they may contribute early but can withdraw if they feel exploited.
- DUCK has a low cooperative tendency and is likely to free-ride.
- GORILLA has mixed tendencies: sensitive to framing and may try matching a pledge, but is also responsive to payoff signals.
- SLOTH shows high anchor susceptibility and very low active search: likely to default to free-riding and stay quiet.

Overall predicted dynamic: initial moderate cooperation driven by PARROT's pledges and a few reciprocal contributors (CHICKEN, DOG). Seeing that defectors often earn higher round payoffs, some contributors (especially CHICKEN and GORILLA) may switch to free-riding in later rounds. Cooperation will likely decline over rounds, possibly stabilizing at PARROT alone continuing to contribute.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with six players who each decide between contributing their entire 20-coin endowment or keeping it. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so group returns favor mutual contribution but individual incentives favor free-riding. Because players can punish at a cost (2 coins per punishment unit, each unit deducts 5 coins from the target), punishment is the primary enforcement mechanism: contributors may punish defectors to discourage free-riding, while defectors can sometimes retaliate. Messages allow limited coordination and social signaling; players don't know the game's length, which tends to sustain cooperation compared with known finite games.

Given the personas, I expect a mix of initial cooperation from more pro-social players (SLOTH, DOG, CHICKEN) and early defection from more self-interested players (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT). Punishment will be used sparingly to enforce cooperation; once a cooperative norm emerges, most players will contribute regularly, with occasional opportunistic defections that trigger targeted punishments. Retaliatory punishments may occur but should remain limited. Chats will be few and generally used to call out defectors or announce intentions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with 6 players, 20 coins each. Contributions are privately chosen each round; the group pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can punish (cost 4 to sender, -10 to target per unit) or reward (cost 4 to sender, +3 to target per unit). Players see only their own round summary (their net payoff, how much they spent on punish/reward, how much they had deducted by punishment, and how much they received via rewards), not peers' summaries or who sent punishments/rewards.

Likely dynamics:
- Round 1 typically shows heterogeneity: some players contribute moderately-high out of cooperative preference or signaling (e.g., CHICKEN, PARROT), some contribute less (SLOTH). Without reputation, contributions are mixed.
- After contributions are revealed, high contributors may punish low contributors to enforce norms; rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce high contributors. Because punishment is costly and visible, we expect only a few 1-unit punishments and occasional 1-unit rewards.
- The punishments in round 1 will encourage modestly higher contributions in round 2. Some punished players may retaliate in round 2 (despite anonymity of who punished them), but retaliation is typically limited because punishment is costly.
- Overall, expect contributions to rise slightly in round 2 and relatively sparse punishment/reward activity, with a few targeted sanctions and a couple of small rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, lasting 19 rounds. Each round each player keeps some private coins (withdrawal) and contributes the rest to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players may spend costly reward units (2 coins cost per unit) to give others a matching 2 coins per unit. Players see only their own round payoff and their own reward sending/receiving amounts, not who sent rewards to whom.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players will try moderately high contributions to signal cooperative intent. Because withdrawals are private, free riders who contribute little get high private payoffs early, which can tempt others to reduce contribution.
- Rewarding is costly, so players will reward sparingly — mostly to reinforce high contributors or "thank" cooperators; rewards will be used more to reward high contributors than to punish (no negative rewards).
- A persistent low contributor (a free rider) will tend to get ignored (no reward) and might prompt calls in chat to contribute more; but because rewards are costly, social exclusion through withholding rewards is the main lever.
- Midgame: attempts at coordination (short chat appeals) can temporarily raise contributions, but occasional declines reappear, especially near the final rounds due to endgame effects.
- Final rounds: contributions usually decline as the horizon approaches, with a few last-round thank-you rewards. Overall, cooperation will fluctuate but remain present among several players; the persistent low contributor will often get a higher net payoff from private withdrawals despite social pressure.

game_explanation:
This is a simultaneous public goods game with six players who each have 20 coins per round and can either contribute 0 or 20 to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so contributors pay the full 20 cost but may help the group; free riders keep their 20 and also receive an equal share of the multiplied pot. Early rounds typically see some cooperation (people try to coordinate) but because contributors are directly exploited by free riders (who get higher round payoffs), cooperation tends to decline unless players can credibly coordinate or punish. Because players do not know the number of rounds, there is continued uncertainty about whether to attempt cooperation again: some players will intermittently try to restart cooperation, others will settle into defection, and a few may remain conditional cooperators who contribute when they believe enough others will. Chats are sparse and mostly used to propose cooperation or to explain strategy after seeing payoffs; they shape expectations but cannot force contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a small-group binary public-goods game with costly punishment and an unknown horizon. Each round players choose either to keep their 20 coins or contribute all 20 to the group pot; contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the six players, then players may spend coins to punish identified peers (each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and reduces the target by 5 coins). Players see who punished them and, at the round summary, their own net payoff, how much they spent punishing others, and how much they lost from being punished — and the same information for all peers.

Likely dynamics
- Early rounds: uncertainty. Some players test cooperation; others free-ride to see responses. Visible punishments in early rounds should encourage more cooperation in subsequent rounds.
- Enforcement: a few norm-enforcing players (high sensitivity profiles) will punish defectors occasionally rather than always; punishments will be sparse but targeted. That tends to sustain a moderate-to-high cooperation level across rounds.
- Recurrence: the same two players (SLOTH and PARROT) have higher propensities to defect in our projection; other players will punish them intermittently. Escalation is possible if a player defects repeatedly, but excessive punishment is costly and thus will remain limited.
- Communication: chat is sparse and used mainly to request contributions or express disappointment/apology.
- Uncertainty about the horizon means cooperation is sustained longer than with a known last round, but occasional defections persist.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 2-round public goods game with optional costly rewarding. Each coin you keep yields more to yourself than a coin you contribute yields back to you individually (private return ≈ 0.367 per coin contributed), so the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). But because the pot is amplified and shared, group welfare rises with contributions. The reward stage lets players transfer payoff to others at a net group cost (each reward unit costs 4 to the source and gives 3 to the recipient), so rewarding is an altruistic/incentive act rather than a pure wealth-creating one.

Uncertainties and dynamics to expect:
- In round 1 players choose with no direct history; contributions will reflect prior prosocial tendencies, anchor sensitivity, and whether someone signals intentions in chat.
- Players who contribute substantially may be rewarded by others who want to encourage cooperation, even though rewarding is costly.
- Because the game lasts only two rounds, players may use rewards in round 1 to signal reciprocity or primarily in round 2 to reward/reciprocate observed behavior.
- Personas suggest CHICKEN is likely to contribute relatively high; GORILLA is likely to be low; SLOTH may follow a focal number (anchor-susceptible) rather than exert search effort; DOG, DUCK, PARROT are moderately cooperative. Expect sparse chat and sparse, targeted rewards (rewarding cooperators rather than widespread transfers).

Given these priors, I predict some positive contributions in round 1 with a few targeted rewards to high contributors; round 2 will adjust slightly — cooperators tend to sustain or modestly increase contributions, free-riders may continue to exploit. Rewarding stays sparse, focused on those who contributed above the group average.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round players decide privately how much of their 20 coins to keep (withdraw) and how much to leave in the shared pot (their contribution). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally; with 6 players each contributed coin returns 0.3 to every player. The individual incentive is to withdraw everything (keep 1 coin) because each contributed coin gives only 0.3 back to the contributor, but the group gain is maximized by full contribution. That tension typically yields moderate contributions early, then declines as free-riders appear. The reward stage (cost 2 per reward unit, gives 2 to the target) allows players to remunerate cooperators — but rewards are costly, so we expect them to be used sparingly to signal approval (or maintain cooperation). Messages will be infrequent and strategic (praise, short appeals, or explanations). Over 19 rounds I predict moderate-to-high early contributions that drift downward, with a few players (e.g., DOG, PARROT) repeatedly rewarded for relatively high contributions. Sparse rewards will sometimes stabilize contributions locally, producing small recoveries later, but the general trend is a decline with some endgame upticks as players anticipate final-round reciprocity.

game_explanation:
This is a multi-round public goods game with a fixed group of six players and 19 rounds. Each round players choose how much of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution), which is then multiplied and divided equally; after seeing outcomes they may send costly punishments or rewards to others. Expect initial rounds to be exploratory: some players propose a cooperative norm, others test by free-riding. When low contributions appear, a few players (those who value cooperation or retaliate against free-riders) are likely to punish or occasionally reward high contributors to encourage cooperation. Punishments are costly and relatively rare; rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce good behavior. Over time contributions often rise if sanctions are effective, but occasional defections can cause temporary drops; toward the final rounds some decline is likely as the endgame approaches. Chat messages are usually sparse and used to propose norms or complain after punishments.

game_explanation: This 2-round public-goods game balances private incentives (keeping endowment) against group benefit (multiplied pot shared equally). With a 2.2 multiplier and six players, each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to each player — the group benefit outweighs the private cost only when others also contribute. Because contributions are simultaneous, first-round uncertainty is high and players often split between modest cooperation and free-riding. The post-decision punishment/reward stage encourages enforcement: punishments are costly to send but harsh for targets (sender pays 4, target loses 10), while rewards are also costly (sender pays 4, target gains 3). These incentives typically lead to some punishment of low contributors in round 1 and increased cooperation in round 2, with selective rewards to reinforce high contributions. Chat is available but usually sparse; players will use it more to express approval or complaints than to signal exact actions.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with subsequent costly punishment and reward. Each player starts each round with 20 coins and decides privately how much (0–20) to contribute to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among the six players, so every coin you give returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to each player (including you). The private cost-benefit tradeoff: contributing lowers your private holding but increases everyone’s share. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players can spend coins to punish (cost 4 per unit, -10 to target per unit) or reward (cost 4 per unit, +3 to target per unit) others. Players may also send chat messages at any time; punishments/rewards are anonymous unless a player confesses in chat.

Likely dynamics across two rounds:
- Round 1: Some players will contribute a moderate amount (reciprocal/cooperative types), others may free-ride (contribute 0–low). Messages and initial reputational signaling (announcing intended contributions) may appear. Because punishments and rewards are available, at least one or two players who care about fairness will use them sparingly to discipline clear free-riders or to reinforce cooperative peers.
- Round 2 (final round): Without future rounds to build reputation, average contributions tend to fall. Punishment and reward use typically declines as well (people are less willing to pay to change others’ future behavior), though some spiteful punishments or last-round reciprocal rewards still occur. Expect sparser pro-social enforcement than in round 1.

Player priors: SLOTH and PARROT are somewhat attentive and likely to respond to framing; CHICKEN and DUCK lean cooperative; DOG is moderately cooperative; GORILLA has low process engagement and is likelier to free-ride. Use of punishment/reward will be sparse and focused on obvious low contributors.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game has 6 players and lasts 19 rounds. Each round, group-level returns equal 0.3 times the sum of contributions (because 1.8/6 = 0.3), so a fixed-sum group benefit encourages contributions but individual incentives push toward withdrawing (private use). Early on, a few low contributors (free-riders) will attract sparse punishment from more cooperative players; punishments are costly to the sender (2 coins per unit) and inflict a larger loss on the target (3 coins per unit), so they are used sparingly. Rewards (also costly) will appear occasionally to reinforce high contributors. Because players cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, social feedback is noisy; communications are sparse but can steer norms. Over 19 rounds I expect moderate cooperation with occasional lapses by a few players (notably PARROT), a handful of small punishments aimed at correcting those lapses, occasional rewards for steady contributors, and small adjustments in contributions over time (some recovery after punishment, a little end-game drifting).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 coins each round. Contributing 20 to the public pot benefits the group (pot multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally), but any individual who withholds the 20 gets both the private benefit (keeps 20) and the shared pot — so single-round incentives favor free-riding. In early rounds some players with cooperative priors (DUCK, PARROT, CHICKEN) are likely to try contributing, while strong free-riders (GORILLA, DOG) and more opportunistic or hesitant players (SLOTH) are likely to withhold. After a round where cooperators earn less than defectors, cooperation commonly decays; occasional attempts to restart cooperation (promises, appeals) can produce temporary pockets of contributions. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, there is no guaranteed endgame collapse from knowledge of the final round, but repeated exploitation tends to push most players to defect as the game proceeds. Chat will be sparse and used primarily to promise contributions or to respond when exploitation occurs.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a short (2-round) public goods game with costly rewards and optional chat. Key incentives and likely dynamics:
- Contributions: Each round players choose 0–20. The shared pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among all 6 players. Because the multiplier (2.2) yields a per-player return of 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 for each coin contributed, private marginal return is <1, so the individual incentive is to free-ride. However, group benefit rises with contributions, so cooperative players will contribute some positive amount, especially early.
- Time horizon: With only 2 rounds, standard backward-induction logic predicts lower contributions in round 2 (final round) because there's no future to reward or punish. Round 1 therefore typically shows higher contributions than round 2.
- Rewards: Rewarding is costly (giver pays 4 per unit; recipient gains 3 per unit). Rewards can sustain cooperation if players use them to reinforce cooperative behavior, but because rewards are costly and anonymous in summaries, they will be used sparingly. In the final round, rewards are less effective as incentives for future behavior and thus are often reduced.
- Messaging: Chat can be used to signal intentions (e.g., "I'll contribute X") or to coordinate. Players differ in susceptibility to anchors and framing; high anchor-susceptible players (GORILLA, DOG) may follow public commitments, while low-anchor players (DUCK, CHICKEN, PARROT) may be less swayed.
- Uncertainty: Players do not know others' private preferences and cannot see reward senders in the summary, so there is noise and ambiguity in interpreting motives. Expect modest cooperation in round 1, sparser use of rewards, and a fall in contributions in round 2.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, unknown horizon, binary contributions (0 or 20). Each round the group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so contributors help everyone but pay an immediate private cost. Punishment is costly to the sender (2 coins per unit) and harsher to the target (5 coins per unit). Because players do not know the number of rounds, we expect initial experimentation, then a tendency for a small set of players to enforce cooperation by occasional punishment. Enforcement will be targeted and fairly sparse: punishers will mostly target persistent free-riders rather than punish widely. Some players (pro-social types) are likely to contribute early and may bear punishment costs; defectors may switch after repeated penalties. Chat will be rare and used to signal intentions or protest. Over 12 rounds we therefore expect: a few early free-rides, occasional targeted punishments, then increasing cooperation as punishments curb persistent defectors, with a few slips and sparse punishment later.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, a marginal per-unit return of 1.8/6 = 0.30 (so each coin contributed returns only 0.30 to the individual), and a finite horizon of 19 rounds. Individually the dominant action is to withdraw all 20 coins (contribute 0) in every round, but social welfare is maximized when everyone contributes everything. With communication and a small costly-reward mechanism (2 coins to giver per 2 coins to receiver), we typically see a mixture: some players try to establish cooperative anchors early (moderate contributions like 8–15), others free-ride or experiment (low contributions). Rewards are used sparingly to praise consistent contributors or to sustain norms; because rewards are costly, they tend to be targeted and infrequent. Over time, absent strong, consistent reward enforcement, contributions usually drift downward and approach zero as the end of the game nears (backward induction and declining incentive to sustain costly rewards). Chats will be few and purposeful — suggestions, thanks, or occasional calls-out — and reward edges will be limited to a few rounds where players try to support cooperators.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with costly one-way rewards. Each coin you keep gives you 1.0 privately, but each coin contributed adds 2.2 to the group pot which is split across 6 players (so each contributed coin returns about 0.3667 to each player). That makes contribution socially efficient (total group gain) but privately dominated: every player has an incentive to free-ride. Because the game lasts only two rounds, backward induction can push toward low contributions, but uncertainty about others and the reward stage make the first round particularly noisy.

The reward stage lets players reinforce cooperative behavior (pay 4 to give another +3) but cannot punish directly. In practice, rewards tend to be sparse and targeted at noticeably high contributors as positive reinforcement. Players who received rewards in round 1 are likely to keep or raise contributions in round 2; those who were not rewarded (or paid to reward others) sometimes adjust downwards to avoid net loss.

Using the provided personas as priors: CHICKEN and PARROT are relatively likely to contribute more and to follow social cues; GORILLA tends toward lower engagement; DUCK and SLOTH are moderately cooperative but cautious; DOG is modest/cooperative but not extreme. I therefore predict moderate-first-round contributions with selective rewarding of the highest contributors; in round 2, rewarded cooperators increase or maintain contributions and get mild further rewards.

game_explanation:
This 19-round public goods game balances private incentives (keeping coins) against collective returns (contributions multiplied by 1.8 and shared). Because the per-person share equals 0.3 times the group total, each coin contributed returns 0.3 to each individual — 1.8 total to the pot but only 0.3 to any single player — so the immediate selfish incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). However, players can use costly rewards to transfer payoff to others (each reward unit costs 2 to the sender and gives 2 to the target). Rewards are the only sanctioning mechanism here: they encourage cooperation by reinforcing contributors, or signal reciprocity.

Prediction outline:
- Early rounds: modest-to-high contributions from generally cooperative types (DUCK, DOG, CHICKEN, PARROT) and moderate contributions from GORILLA and SLOTH. Small, targeted rewards emerge to reinforce high contributors; rewards are sparse because they cost 2 per unit.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperation patterns stabilize around a subset of contributors (DUCK frequently high). Rewards mostly flow toward reliably cooperative players; some players free-ride more (SLOTH drifts lower).
- Late rounds: gradual erosion of cooperation as endgame approaches; contributions decline but some targeted rewards persist among reciprocating pairs. Players reward sparingly in final rounds — often a last thank-you or reciprocal gesture.

Throughout, chat is rare and functional (short clarifying notes, thanks, or mild appeals). Rewards are used selectively, typically 1 unit at a time, and only when players want to reinforce a pattern — not to punish.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with binary contribution choices (0 or 20) and costly punishment. The shared pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so when many contribute there is a clear group benefit but each player individually has an incentive to free-ride (keep 20 privately while still collecting the public share). Because players do not know how many rounds remain, standard dynamics are: some players will cooperate early, a few will test by free-riding, and others will use punishment to enforce cooperation. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) but inflicts a larger cost on the target (5 coins per unit), so it can sustain cooperation if used sparingly. Expect initial noise (some free-riding), selective punishment from reciprocators/conditional cooperators (especially CHICKEN, DOG, and DUCK), and then more stable cooperation after free-riders learn they will be punished. Chat messages tend to be sparse and used to request cooperation or call out defectors.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with six players. Each round each player privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Individually, each contributed coin returns less than 1 coin to its contributor (2.2/6 ≈ 0.37), so the dominant self-interested choice is to contribute nothing. However, mutual cooperation (higher contributions by many players) raises everyone’s total payoffs. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (costly to punishers, harsh to targets) or reward (costly to rewarders, moderately beneficial to targets). Because punishment is expensive for the punisher (4 coins per unit) but removes 10 coins from the target per unit, players typically punish sparingly and only to deter clear free-riding; rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce high contributors.

Key uncertainties: players may strategically announce intentions in chat (which can affect expectations but not force compliance), and each actor faces a tradeoff between the private cost of sanctioning/rewarding and the indirect benefit through future behavior (only one later round here). Given the personas, I expect some strong cooperators (DOG, PARROT, DUCK) to contribute high, some cautious/low contributors (SLOTH, CHICKEN) to free-ride in round 1, and moderate adjustments in round 2 after seeing contributions and a small amount of targeted punishment for blatant free-riding.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a short (2-round) public goods game with a post-contribution reward stage. Key incentives:
- Contributing increases the group pot: the sum of contributions is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. If you contribute, you reduce your private holdings now but may gain back through the shared return.
- Because the multiplier (2.2) divided by 6 players gives a per-coin private return of 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367, each coin you contribute returns less than one coin to you personally, so the dominant selfish incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride).
- Rewards let players transfer value: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the receiver +3. Rewards are expensive to send, so they tend to be used sparingly to encourage cooperators or to reciprocate.
- Communication is allowed but not binding; players can send short messages that may influence expectations.
- Because the game is only 2 rounds, there's an “endgame” effect: limited scope for long-run reciprocity. Players often moderate cooperation in later rounds (round 2) depending on round 1 outcomes.
- Important uncertainty: players don't know others' types exactly; personas suggest a heterogeneous group (some likely low contributors, some moderate cooperators). Also players cannot identify who sent them rewards — they see only the amount received and their own sending.

Predicted overall dynamics:
- Expect mixed play: at least one clear free-rider (likely GORILLA) contributes 0 both rounds. Some players (CHICKEN, SLOTH, DOG) are more likely to contribute moderately and to reward cooperators they observe. DUCK and PARROT may be more variable/low contributors.
- Rewards will be sparse and targeted to visible cooperators (those who contribute more). Senders will use small numbers of reward units (1 or maybe 2) because of the high cost.
- Because players learn from round 1 and there are only two rounds, contributors may maintain or slightly lower contributions in round 2; reward patterns may persist but not dramatically escalate.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and costly punishment. Typical dynamics: some players will try to contribute to sustain a cooperative outcome because the pot multiplier (3.7) makes full contribution socially valuable; others may free-ride because defecting leaves them with their private 20 plus the public share. Because punishing is costly to both sides (2 coins per unit to the punisher, 5 coins deducted per unit from the target), punishment will be used sparingly — mainly as enforcement by a subset of players who care about cooperation (or about norms/reciprocity). Early punishment of visible defectors can stabilize contributions in later rounds; players who get punished are likely to switch to contributing, at least sometimes. Chat is generally sparse: short admonitions, apologies, or promises to change. Over 12 rounds I expect a majority-cooperation norm to emerge, with one or two intermittent free-riders (SLOTH is prone to occasional defection in the persona priors) and a small set of enforcers (CHICKEN and GORILLA, occasionally PARROT or DOG) delivering low-intensity punishments to deter free-riding.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game gives each player a binary choice each round: keep their 20-coin endowment (contribute 0) or put it into the group pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players, so contributing benefits the group but is individually costly unless enough others also contribute. Because free-riding (not contributing) yields a higher private payoff in the short run, norm enforcement (punishment) is the mechanism that can sustain cooperation: a punished free-rider loses 5 coins per punishment unit, while punishers pay 2 coins per unit. Punishment is costly, so players will typically punish sparingly and mainly target defection when they care about future cooperation or fairness.

Based on the player personas and typical PGG dynamics, I expect:
- Early rounds will show mostly cooperative contributions but a few defections (testing the group).
- Punishment will be used selectively against obvious free-riders; punishers tend to be those who care about cooperation or who already cooperated.
- After being punished, defectors generally switch to contributing in subsequent rounds.
- Over time cooperation stabilizes at a high level and punishment declines, with occasional single defections met by targeted punishments.
- Chats will be sparse and purposeful (requests to contribute, quick apologies or justifications, or short threats/commitments to punish).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with 6 players. Each round each player privately decides to contribute either 0 or their full 20 coins to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and divided equally among the six players, so the group-maximizing outcome is for everyone to contribute (everyone gets 74 coins when all six contribute), but individually a player always earns more by free-riding (keeping 20 and still receiving the shared return) given other players' contributions. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, early rounds tend to be used to probe others' willingness to cooperate; conditional cooperators (who match others) may start contributing but will reduce contributions when they see many free riders. Some players here are more likely to signal intentions or respond to signals (high anchor/communication users), others are more likely to ignore messages and freeride. Overall I expect moderate initial cooperation that erodes as freeriders reap higher immediate payoffs, then intermittent attempts to re-establish cooperation via short messages and matching by a subset of players. Chat will be sparse and strategic (promises, short complaints, brief coordination attempts).

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with 6 players, 20 coins endowment per round, all-or-nothing contributions (0 or 20), a group multiplier of 3.7 and an equal split of the pot. Individually, contributing is costly: a single contribution increases the total group payout but reduces the contributor's private holding more than the private return from the multiplied pot (private marginal return ≈ 12.33 < 20). So the selfish dominant strategy in any single-shot view is to contribute 0. Across repeated play, however, cooperative signals, promises and reciprocity can sustain contributions early on; typical dynamics show an initial flurry of contributions followed by a decline as free-riding is observed. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, some will keep experimenting with cooperation or try to re-establish norms; others will exploit cooperative gestures.

Given the six personas:
- DOG (very low purchase/cooperation tendency) will often free-ride.
- GORILLA, CHICKEN, PARROT lean toward skepticism/low contribution rates but may respond to framing or promises inconsistently.
- SLOTH has a strong anchor tendency and may stick to an early choice for a while.
- DUCK and (to a lesser extent) SLOTH/ PARROT are more likely to try coordinating or experimenting with contributions early on.

reflection:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with 6 players, 20 coins endowment per round, all-or-nothing contributions (0 or 20), a group multiplier of 3.7 and an equal split of the pot. Individually, contributing is costly: a single contribution increases the total group payout but reduces the contributor's private holding more than the private return from the multiplied pot (private marginal return ≈ 12.33 < 20). So the selfish dominant strategy in any single-shot view is to contribute 0. Across repeated play, however, cooperative signals, promises and reciprocity can sustain contributions early on; typical dynamics show an initial flurry of contributions followed by a decline as free-riding is observed. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, some will keep experimenting with cooperation or try to re-establish norms; others will exploit cooperative gestures.

Given the six personas:
- DOG (very low purchase/cooperation tendency) will often free-ride.
- GORILLA, CHICKEN, PARROT lean toward skepticism/low contribution rates but may respond to framing or promises inconsistently.
- SLOTH has a strong anchor tendency and may stick to an early choice for a while.
- DUCK and (to a lesser extent) SLOTH/ PARROT are more likely to try coordinating or experimenting with contributions early on.

Expect early rounds to show some cooperation or promises, then a general decline with intermittent attempts to rebuild cooperation via short, purposeful chat messages.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with a costly reward stage. Individually, each coin contributed returns only about 0.3667 to the contributor (2.2/6), so the selfish best response is to contribute 0. However some players have pro-social priors and may contribute substantial amounts in round 1; those contributors are then visible and can be rewarded. Rewards are costly to send (4 coins per reward unit) and provide 3 coins to the recipient, so rewards will be used sparingly and mainly to signal approval of high contributors rather than to increase efficiency. Because the game lasts only two rounds, backward induction predicts falling contributions in round 2 for purely selfish players, but social/reciprocal types (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT) may maintain or slightly adjust contributions based on round 1 feedback. Expect a few small reward transfers targeting the highest contributors in each round and short, purposeful chat messages (announcements or expressions of intent, and brief reactions after feedback).

game_explanation:
This is a finite (19-round) public goods game with 6 players. Each coin left in the shared pot gives only 0.3 coins back to each individual (1.8 total multiplier split among six), while any coin withdrawn privately yields 1 coin to its owner. That makes the one-shot incentive to free-ride (withdraw everything) strong, but repeated interaction and the ability to punish/reward can create and sustain cooperative contributions. Key dynamics to expect:

- Early rounds: uncertainty about others' behavior. Some players who are conditional cooperators (sensitive to norms and anchors) are likely to contribute moderately-high amounts as a signal; more selfish players will start low. Small amounts of punishment and reward may appear to communicate norms.
- Middle rounds: if conditional cooperators are visible through consistent contributions and a few costly punishments target free-riders, contributions typically rise as cooperative norms stabilize. Rewards are used sparingly to reinforce high contributors; punishments are used sparingly to deter persistent low contributors.
- Late rounds (endgame): backward-induction incentives cause contributions to drop as players anticipate little future benefit from upholding norms. The collapse usually begins in the final few rounds, and punishment drops off as it becomes less profitable to enforce norms in the last periods.
- Uncertainty and anonymity: players cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, and chat is sparse. That increases noise in how people interpret signals, so enforcement tends to be moderate and selective rather than extreme.

Given the provided personas, I expect CHICKEN, PARROT, and DUCK to tend toward higher contributions (conditional cooperation, anchor-following), SLOTH and GORILLA to be relatively more selfish initially, and DOG to be moderate/occasional enforcer. Punishment and reward actions will be rare and small (mostly single units) and used to nudge behavior. Contributions will climb over the early-to-mid rounds, stay high for a while, and then fall sharply in the final few rounds.

In round 2 contributions will drop (endgame), and punitive actions will be even rarer.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-round public goods game with 6 players, 20 coins each per round. Each coin left in the shared pot becomes a contribution; public returns are 1.8× total contributions split equally (so each contributed coin returns 0.30 to each player). Individually, keeping a coin always yields a higher private return (1) than the personal share of contributing (0.30), so the myopic incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). Social welfare is maximized when players contribute more. Punishment (cost 2 to sender, −3 to target per unit) and reward (cost 2, +2 to target per unit) let players try to enforce cooperation but are costly for the enforcer.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players start moderately cooperative, while at least one player (a likely defector) contributes little. Initial social signals (chat) and first-round payoffs will reveal deviations.
- Mid game: other players will use punishment sparingly to discipline clear defectors and use small rewards to reinforce cooperators. This tends to raise contributions from occasional low contributors (conditional cooperation).
- Late game: tendency to drift back toward self-interest appears in many experiments. If punishment has successfully disciplined defectors, contributions can stabilize at a moderate level; if punishment is too costly or inconsistent, cooperation may erode.
- Messaging is sparse but purposeful: a few calls for punishment, promises to contribute, and short acknowledgements.
- Punishment is used only occasionally (not every round) and focused on clear outliers. Rewards are used even more sparingly to acknowledge sustained cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a simultaneous binary public-goods game with six players: each round everyone privately chooses either to contribute all 20 coins to a shared pot or keep their 20. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six active players. Because contributors give up private holdings and the public return is shared, free-riding (contributing 0) is individually attractive: a free-rider receives the full 20 plus an equal share of the multiplied pot, while a contributor gives up 20 and only receives the equal share.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds often see some cooperation: promises and short messages can anchor behavior and some players (high anchor sensitivity) will follow explicit commitments.
- Free-riders earn more in mixed groups, so cooperation tends to decay over repeated anonymous rounds unless group members can credibly coordinate or value reciprocity.
- Players with high anchor/imitative tendencies (SLOTH, DOG) will respond strongly to explicit pledges to contribute; high search/deliberation types (GORILLA, PARROT) will calculate payoffs and may switch to free-riding once they see exploitation. Framing-sensitive players (DUCK, CHICKEN) react to persuasive messages or when they see others cooperating.
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there can be intermittent attempts to re-establish cooperation; typically these succeed only briefly before defection resumes.

Transcript (rounds 1–12). Avatar order for contributions is always: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game (6 players, 19 rounds) will likely show a common pattern: relatively high cooperation at the start, localized responses to obvious defections via selective rewarding or withholding rewards, gradual erosion of cooperation as the end approaches, and an endgame collapse to lower contributions. Incentives: each coin left in the pot returns 1.8/6 = 0.3 coins to each player, so a fully selfish immediate incentive is to keep coins privately (private return 1.0 vs public marginal per-player return 0.3). Rewards provide a second-stage social incentive: players can spend 2 coins (per reward unit) to give 2 coins to another player. Because rewards are anonymous, they are used sparingly to (a) reinforce cooperators, (b) signal disapproval by not rewarding defectors, and (c) occasionally reward turnaround behavior. Uncertainty: players don't observe others' chosen contributions before deciding and do not see who rewarded them, so chat messages and past patterns shape beliefs. Given the provided personas (mixed tendencies toward conditional cooperation, framing sensitivity, or inertia), expect an initial cooperative norm, a few small targeted rewards to reinforce contributors, episodic defections and short punishment/reward responses, and a decline toward the final rounds as backward induction and endgame effects reduce contributions and reward use.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, 19 rounds, and the option to reward others after seeing aggregated returns. Each round each player chooses how much to withdraw (private) and how much to contribute to the shared pot (contribution = 20 - withdraw); the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally (so each player's public return = 0.3 * total contributions). Rewards are costly: each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins (a transfer, not a multiplier). Players only see their own round payoff and their own reward in/out, not the actions of specific others.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Early rounds: some players will try to sustain contributions; reward use will be sparse but targeted to visible high-return behavior. Because the group split is symmetric, cooperative players benefit the group but individual short-term incentives favor larger withdrawals (free-riding).
- Mid rounds: conditional cooperators (who pay attention, like GORILLA and DUCK) will respond to others' apparent behavior and to reward costs. They may attempt to stabilize cooperation by giving occasional rewards, but they pay costs themselves. Free-riders (e.g., CHICKEN) will often withdraw more and earn larger private payoffs in the short run, which can undermine cooperation.
- Late rounds: because of the finite horizon, cooperation tends to decay (backwards induction and observed payoffs push toward lower contributions). Rewards become rarer because sending them costs the sender and yields little future benefit.
- Communication: chat will be sparse — a few short messages encouraging cooperation or noting payouts — and mostly used early to signal intent or late to vent/thank.

I simulate a plausible path consistent with these incentives: moderate cooperation early, gradual decline in contributions across rounds, a small number of targeted rewards concentrated early-to-mid, and largely zero contributions and few rewards at the end.

This is a 2-round public goods game with optional peer rewards. Each round, players decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players may spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs 4 to the giver and gives 3 to the receiver). Because the game is only two rounds long, backward induction pulls toward lower contributions in the final round. However, the reward stage creates incentives to cooperate in round 1: players who expect reciprocity or who value group payoff may contribute more early on and signal cooperation via rewards. The reward mechanic also allows conditional cooperators to reward those who contributed and ignore (or not punish) low contributors.

Uncertainties: we cannot perfectly predict individual motives — some players (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT) have priors consistent with higher cooperation; others (CHICKEN, DOG) lean toward lower engagement; SLOTH is mixed. Expect moderate cooperation in round 1 with selective rewarding of higher contributors. In round 2, overall contributions will likely fall, but some pro-social players may still contribute and use small rewards to reinforce cooperative behavior.

game_explanation:
This 14-round binary public-goods game pits private incentives (keep 20) against collective benefit (contribute 20 to a pot that is multiplied by 2.6 and shared equally by all players). With 13 players, each contributed 20 yields 20*2.6/13 ≈ 4 added to each player, so one person's contribution helps the group but is costly privately. Because contributions are observable only after decisions, players learn about others' patterns only from past summaries. Punishment and reward are available but costly to the giver (3 per unit) and powerful on targets (−11 per punishment unit, +4 per reward unit). Real groups typically show some initial cooperation that can be sustained by occasional rewards and sparse punishment; punishment is used sparingly because it is costly, and many players reduce contributions toward the end (end-game decline).

Using the given personas as priors, I expect:

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 named players, 26 rounds, and a binary contribution choice each round: either withdraw 20 (contribute 0) or withdraw 0 (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally; that creates a tension: any individual gains more privately by withdrawing (keeping 20) but the group is better off if many contribute. Because contributions are revealed each round, players can condition future behavior on observed cooperation. The reward stage lets players transfer small amounts at personal cost to reward or punish others; these can sustain cooperation if cooperators selectively reward other cooperators, or discourage free-riding if defectors are punished. Chat is allowed but typically sparse and used to coordinate, accuse, or compliment.

Given the personas, expect an initial mix of cooperation and defection—some avatars (CHICK, GORILLA, OWL, PARROT, SLOTH) are more likely to contribute initially, while others (CHICKEN, DUCK) are more likely to defect. Early rounds will test trust; a few targeted rewards to cooperators should appear, and occasional small punishments toward persistent defectors. Over time, conditional cooperators will reduce contributions if defection spreads; however, sparse positive rewards can revive cooperation transiently. Because reward transfers are anonymous to recipients (they can't identify who sent them in summaries), social signaling via chat and visible reward activity (observed in this transcript) matters for reputation building among players. Expect cooperation to decline moderately across the run, with intermittent recoveries when groups cluster rewards on cooperators.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 26 rounds. Each round players choose between contributing their full 20 coins to the group pot or keeping them privately (0 or 20 contribution). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly — so, individually, withholding is privately profitable in a single round, but group-wide contributions raise everyone's return. Because contributions are simultaneous and private, players face uncertainty about others' behavior. Over repeated rounds players can signal norms with chat, and use costly punishments and rewards to encourage cooperation. Punishment is more powerful (each unit costs 1 and subtracts 2 from the target) which can deter persistent defectors but at a cost to punishers. Rewards are cheaper for targets (1 to giver adds 1 to receiver) but don't deter defection as strongly.

Expectations for dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed cooperation as players test each other; some contribute, some free-ride.
- Mid-game: social enforcement (punishments and selective rewards) tends to stabilize cooperation if some players are willing to pay enforcement costs.
- Late rounds: an "end-game" temptation emerges; cooperation tends to erode unless norms are strongly enforced or individuals value reputation across rounds. Punishment tends to be sparse and targeted; rewards are occasional and used to recognize contributors.
- Chats will be occasional and strategic (calls for cooperation, warnings, short thanks/apologies), not continuous.

Now the play-by-play transcript from round 1 through round 26.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated one-shot-style public goods game (14 rounds) with 13 anonymous players each round. Each round players choose whether to contribute their whole endowment (20) or keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and shared equally. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish or reward others (punishing: 3 coins costs per unit for sender, deducts 11 coins from target per unit; rewarding: 3 coins cost, gives target +4 per unit). Because choices are binary (0 or 20) and interaction is anonymous, standard dynamics are likely:

- Early rounds: many try cooperation; some test or free-ride. Defectors obtain higher immediate payoffs absent punishment, so cooperation is fragile at first.
- Punishment/reward: cooperators will punish clear free-riders occasionally (sparsely) and sometimes reward consistent cooperators to sustain norms. Punishment is costly to the sender, so it's used sparingly and typically only when persistent or salient defection happens.
- Convergence: if punishment/reward is effective, most players converge to contributing 20 in middle rounds. If punishment is too infrequent or costly, we may see intermittent defections.
- Endgame: with the final round known, some players are likely to defect more (endgame free-riding) because future retaliation is impossible. Punishment in the last round is uncommon because it’s costly and has no deterrent benefit.

Uncertainties: exact identities of who will punish or reward are hard to predict; punishment is typically limited to a few players and distributed sparsely. Rewards are rarer than defections but used occasionally to reinforce contributors.

Because choices are binary (0 or 20) and interaction is anonymous, standard dynamics are likely:

- Early rounds: many try cooperation; some test or free-ride. Defectors obtain higher immediate payoffs absent punishment, so cooperation is fragile at first.
- Punishment/reward: cooperators will punish clear free-riders occasionally (sparsely) and sometimes reward consistent cooperators to sustain norms. Punishment is costly to the sender, so it's used sparingly and typically only when persistent or salient defection happens.
- Convergence: if punishment/reward is effective, most players converge to contributing 20 in middle rounds. If punishment is too infrequent or costly, we may see intermittent defections.
- Endgame: with the final round known, some players are likely to defect more (endgame free-riding) because future retaliation is impossible. Punishment in the last round is uncommon because it’s costly and has no deterrent benefit.

Uncertainties: exact identities of who will punish or reward are hard to predict; punishment is typically limited to a few players and distributed sparsely. Rewards are rarer than defections but used occasionally to reinforce contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, and a binary contribution choice each round (contribute all 20 coins to the public pot, or withdraw all 20 and contribute 0). The public pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally. Individually, withholding (free-riding) strictly raises a player's private return in any given round, but if many players contribute the group payoff is much higher. Because play is finite and players know the horizon, there is pressure toward increasing defection over time (end-game unraveling). However, the presence of costly, directed rewards after contributions introduces an enforcement mechanism: cooperators can reward other cooperators to sustain contributions, and defectors can be left unrewarded (or, less commonly, rewarded for strategic reasons). Communication (chat) is allowed and typically sparse; a few strategic messages early on can establish norms and lead to short-term cooperation.

Predicted dynamics:
- Early rounds: experimentation and norm-establishment. Some players (conditional cooperators or those with higher framing sensitivity) contribute to signal cooperation. A subset of players free-ride to test responses.
- Mid-game: reciprocity via targeted rewards helps sustain a core of cooperators. Rewards are used sparingly (small integers) because they cost the sender.
- Late-game: with a known finite horizon, cooperation drifts downwards. Some players defect to extract private gains; others may stubbornly keep contributing but are vulnerable to exploitation. In the final rounds, cooperation largely unravels.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits the private incentive to keep money (20 coins each round) against the group benefit of contributing to a common pot. Each 20-coin contribution becomes 52 coins in the pot (20 × 2.6) and is split equally across the 13 players — that is, a single contribution yields 4 coins back to each player, including the contributor. So a contributor loses 20 privately but receives only 4 back, meaning the individually rational action is to keep the 20, while the group-optimal action is for everyone to contribute.

The reward stage lets players spend 3 coins to give another player +4 coins (per reward unit). Rewarding is a costly, positive enforcement tool: it cannot directly punish, but it lets players compensate cooperators or show appreciation. Because rewards are anonymous to recipients, they are best used when players expect future reciprocity or when they want to shape the group's behavior. In practice, players often reward sparingly because each unit is costly.

Given the player personas and standard PGG dynamics, I expect:
- Some players will contribute reliably (high prosocial priors like SLOTH and COW).
- Some will free-ride consistently (lower cooperation priors like PARROT, CROCODILE).
- Others will be conditional or mixed, responding to past rewards and the observed contribution pattern.
- Rewards will be used intermittently to bolster cooperators (a few 1–2 unit transfers per round), producing clusters of cooperation that rise and fall across rounds. Toward the final round, backward-induction pressure tends to reduce contributions, though social preferences may keep some players contributing.

Now the full round-by-round transcript.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players and 26 known rounds. Each player each round either keeps the 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leaves them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 then split evenly. Pure self-interest points toward withdrawing the 20 every round (defection), but the group payoff is higher if people contribute. Because contributions and punish/reward actions are anonymous (players don’t see who targeted them), social enforcement works through population-level norms: a few costly punishers or selective rewarders can sustain cooperation, especially early- and mid-game. Communication and reputation signals (cheap talk in chat) can help coordinate contributions.

Uncertainties:
- Some players (personas) are more likely to experiment or to follow norms; others are less engaged. That creates noise early on.
- Punishment is costly to send; senders may avoid punishing late in the game, producing an end-game unraveling of cooperation.
- Rewards are cheaper in terms of social effectiveness (they don’t reduce opponents’ payoffs as much), but senders may use them to encourage cooperation.
Expected unfolding:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions and a few targeted punishments aimed at early defectors; chat attempts to coordinate.
- Middle rounds: cooperation rises as defectors are sanctioned and rewarders reinforce contributors.
- Late rounds: cooperation declines as players anticipate the end of the interaction and punishers cut back because punishment is costly and less profitable.

game_explanation:
This 14-round public goods game pits 13 anonymous players against the temptation to free-ride. Each round players choose either to keep their 20 coins or contribute all 20 to a common pot that is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. After redistribution they can spend coins to punish (cost 3 to sender, -11 to a target per punishment unit) or reward (cost 3 to sender, +4 to target per reward unit) others. Because contributions are all-or-nothing and identity is anonymous, early rounds typically see some experimentation: cooperators test whether others reciprocate, defectors exploit early gains, and a few players use costly (but powerful) punishment to enforce norms. Rewards are a lower-cost way to reinforce cooperation but are less damaging to defectors, while punishment can be an effective deterrent but is costly for the punisher.

Uncertainty about others’ choices and about how often punishment will be used means mixed behavior. I expect an initial cluster of contributors (players with somewhat more cooperative cues) and a minority of persistent free-riders. Punishment/reward will be used sparingly: occasional small punishments targeted at persistent defectors and intermittent one-unit rewards to encourage cooperators. Cooperation will rise with early enforcement, plateau, then slowly erode toward the final rounds as end-game incentives to free-ride grow. Below is a round-by-round transcript that embodies these typical dynamics while keeping punishments and rewards rare.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players have no history yet. A subset who tend to be more attentive or more pro-social (based on personas) test cooperation by contributing; others free-ride to see whether cooperation is rewarded. Punishment is unlikely in the first round, but a couple of small rewards may appear to reinforce early cooperators.

Seeing some cooperation and a few small rewards, more conditional cooperators join in. A small number of players who stayed out last round remain testing free-riding and may draw one or two light punishments meant to signal norms.

Punishment signals begin to change behavior: a couple of prior non-contributors switch to contributing; persistent defection is now visible for a few players and elicits isolated punishments. Small rewards remain occasional.

Some punished players respond by switching to contributing; others remain stubborn. Cooperators sometimes use a one-unit reward to reinforce a recent switch to cooperation. Punishments keep being used sparingly against the small group of repeat defectors.

Momentum for cooperation peaks: most players are contributing after rounds of positive reinforcement and occasional punishment. With near-universal contribution there's little incentive to punish; a couple of small rewards may appear but many players save resources.
Mid-game temptation and idiosyncratic lapses appear: a small number of players revert to free-riding seeking personal gain. Cooperators respond with limited punishment to deter return to defection.
Punished free-riders sometimes switch back to contributing; the group largely stabilizes back toward cooperation. A single small reward might be used to acknowledge a return to contributing.
Cooperation remains high; punishers continue to target the rare persistent defector with one-unit punishments. Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce consistent contributors.

As the midpoint passes, a few players experiment again with defection (endgame effects are still distant but uncertainty grows). Peers punish single-round defection to discourage copying the behavior.
Some previous one-off defectors return to contributing after receiving information that punishments were applied; the group composition of contributors stays high but fragile as the horizon shortens.

With four rounds left, end-game incentives begin to show. A couple of players who had cooperated for social reasons revert to defection to grab one-round gains; others respond with minimal punishment targeted at the few persistent or newly defector players.
End-game dynamics strengthen: a couple more players opportunistically defect. Peers still punish but less often because costly sanctions bring no future benefit when only two rounds remain.

With only one round left after this one, many players pursue short-run payoffs. Contribution falls noticeably. A few cooperators still punish defection out of norm enforcement or spite; a couple of small rewards persist for steady cooperators.

Final-round logic dominates: many players free-ride because there is no future to protect; a handful of consistent cooperators (and maybe one or two players motivated by intrinsic fairness) still contribute. Punishment is rarer because senders bear immediate cost with no future enforcement payoff, though one last retrospective punishment or small reward may appear.
