game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This 15-round public goods game pits private incentives (keep 20 coins) against a group return that benefits everyone if people contribute. Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20) and the group multiplier is 1.9 split across 10 players, a single contribution raises the pot by less than the private cost (contributor forgoes 20, each player receives only 1.9 in total), so the individually rational action each round is to contribute 0. However, if many contribute, everyone gets a higher shared return. Punishment is available: it is costly to the punisher (4 coins per punishment unit) but removes more from the target (8 coins per unit), and identification of punishers is known. That sets up typical dynamics:

- Early rounds are uncertain: some players signal cooperativeness by contributing, others free-ride.
- Cooperators are likely to punish clear, persistent free-riders to enforce contribution norms; punishments tend to be sparse and targeted.
- Punished free-riders often switch to contributing in subsequent rounds, producing a pattern of intermittent defection followed by corrective punishment.
- In later rounds (especially the final round), incentives to punish fall and more players may free-ride, producing a rise in defections in round 15.
- Chats are expected to be sparse: brief moralizing, calls for cooperation, and occasional complaints after being punished.

I predict an initial mixed pattern with a core of regular contributors (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT, RABBIT, CHICK), some early free-riders (SLOTH, CHICKEN, DOG, MOOSE, OWL), and targeted punishments that quickly reduce free-riding. Cooperation becomes common midgame, with occasional one-off defections that are punished. In the final round a few players exploit the last-move incentive.

game_explanation:
This is a classic repeated public goods game with 15 players, 20-coin endowments each round, and a group multiplication factor of 3.7. Individually, each coin contributed returns only about 0.247 coins to a particular player (3.7/15), so the dominant private incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Socially, everyone would be better off if many contribute. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, they tend to start reasonably cooperative (hoping for ongoing reciprocity), but contributions often drift down over time unless there is credible sanctioning or rewarding.

Expectations for how the game will unfold:
- Round 1: Many players give a moderate amount (around half the endowment or a bit less) — a baseline of conditional cooperation and optimism.
- Punishment and reward are possible enforcement tools but are costly to the sender. In real experiments these are used sparingly. When used, punishments are targeted at clear low contributors; rewards target high contributors as positive reinforcement.
- Over rounds, without sustained sanctioning or strong reciprocal rewards, average contributions tend to decline. Occasional punishments and small rewards can stabilize cooperation at an intermediate level.
- Player-specific tendencies (from personas) produce heterogeneity: a few stalwart cooperators (e.g., MONKEY, MOOSE) consistently give high amounts; some participants systematically give low amounts (e.g., CHICKEN, SNAKE at times). Sanctions are deployed against persistent low contributors, and small rewards recognize high contributors.

Punishment/reward remain low-frequency instruments to sustain cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round players choose either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to the group pot or to keep it. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all players; thus a contribution helps the group but invites free-riding (defectors keep their 20 and still receive the share). Because contributions are binary and visible only after choices, reputational signals must come from punishment and chat.

Punishment allows cooperators to impose a cost on defectors (each punishment unit costs 4 to the sender and deducts 8 from the target). That makes punishment effective but costly; we expect it to be used sparingly and typically by a small set of norm-enforcers rather than by everyone every round. Early rounds often feature a mix of cooperation and defection and some targeted punishment; punishment tends to increase cooperation in middle rounds. Toward the final rounds, however, the incentive to invest in costly punishment falls (no future rounds to influence), so cooperation typically decays and punishment decreases.

Given the player priors, I expect one or two players with pro-social tendencies (e.g., GORILLA, OWL, DOG, CHICK) to contribute consistently and to do most of the punishing. Some players (SLOTH, PARROT, DUCK, MOOSE) are more likely to defect early; SLOTH appears particularly likely to persistently defect unless punished. Chats will be sparse and functional (requests to cooperate, brief justifications or complaints after being punished). Punishment will be concentrated and moderate (mostly single-unit punishments to signal displeasure), and fall off in the final two rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 players, each given 20 coins per round. Individually, contributing costs 1 coin but returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 coins back to each player, so the private marginal return is well below 1: the dominant short-term incentive is to contribute 0. The group optimum is the opposite (everyone contributing 20). The optional reward stage (cost 4 to give 5 to someone else) makes rewarding slightly pro-social at the group level (net +1 to total earnings per reward unit) but costly to the individual giver. Because players cannot identify which peer gave which reward, rewards mainly function as anonymous signals and incentives: they can encourage cooperators and sustain conditional cooperation, but they are costly and therefore used sparingly.

Given the player personas (some reliably cooperative or sensitive to social framing, others more self-serving or anchor-driven), I expect:
- Early rounds: exploration and heterogeneity. Some high-cooperators (e.g., RABBIT, CHICK, MOOSE, CHICKEN) give sizable amounts; some players free-ride or give little.

Because players don't know the total number of rounds, end-game collapse is less pronounced than in a known finite-horizon game.

The group optimum is the opposite (everyone contributing 20). The optional reward stage (cost 4 to give 5 to someone else) makes rewarding slightly pro-social at the group level (net +1 to total earnings per reward unit) but costly to the individual giver. Because players cannot identify which peer gave which reward, rewards mainly function as anonymous signals and incentives: they can encourage cooperators and sustain conditional cooperation, but they are costly and therefore used sparingly.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game pits 15 anonymous players against the familiar tension: contribute to a shared pot (which is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally) or keep coins to yourself. Because contributions are private when decided but revealed before the reward stage, contributors can be rewarded (not punished) after the fact — creating an incentive to signal cooperation and to reward cooperators to cultivate reciprocal behavior. Uncertainty about the number of rounds encourages cautious reputation-building early on (people expect future interactions), so we should see moderately cooperative contributions at first, targeted positive rewards to high contributors, and some stable cooperators and some persistent low contributors. Rewarding is costly (4 coins per unit) so players use it sparingly and typically favor a few high contributors rather than dispersing many small rewards. Persona priors (anchor sensitivity, tendency to cooperate or defect, framing sensitivity) will nudge certain avatars toward habitual cooperation (e.g., CROCODILE, RABBIT, DUCK) while others (e.g., OWL) are likely low contributors; conditional cooperators (CHICKEN, SLOTH, PARROT) will adjust based on observed averages.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 identifiable players. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or to keep it. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.9 and split evenly, so individual incentives favor free-riding (keep 20 and still receive a share). Because defecting often yields higher immediate payoff, cooperation typically needs enforcement. Here that enforcement comes from costly, targeted punishment: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and deducts 8 coins from the target. Messaging is allowed but sparse.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: mixed contributions. Some players try cooperating; defectors initially earn more, prompting some cooperators to use punishment to enforce norms against obvious free-riders.
- Middle rounds: if punishments target repeat defectors, cooperation can stabilize (often leading to rounds of near-full cooperation).
- Late rounds: toward the end of the 15 rounds, the incentive to cooperate weakens (endgame effect), so contributions tend to fall; punishers may reduce enforcement as the future loses value, producing a collapse in contributions in the final rounds.
- Punishment will be used sparingly and strategically (not everyone punishes every time). Chat is used occasionally to signal intentions or call out defectors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and must either contribute 0 or 20 to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players. Because an individual who contributes 20 receives only 1.9*20/10 = 3.8 back for themselves, contributing is costly from a purely selfish, one-shot perspective (you lose 16.2 coins relative to keeping your 20). That makes "free-riding" (contributing 0) the dominant action absent other considerations. However, some players may be conditional cooperators, anchor-driven, or motivated by norms or signaling; these players may contribute early or try to coordinate via chat. Because the multiplier (1.9) is less than the group size (10), if many contribute, contributors still fare worse than non-contributors; this creates an unstable incentive structure where cooperation tends to start sometimes but typically declines as rounds proceed, especially once players observe low personal returns for contributing. Expect some early attempts to cooperate and a few sporadic revival attempts via messages, but an overall downward trend in contributions, with most rounds ending in defection. Messages will be sparse and strategic: short commitments, brief appeals to coordinate, or comments reacting to payoff summaries.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a classic public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round, and a pot multiplier of 1.9 split equally. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 1.9/10 = 0.19, so each coin you contribute returns only 19% of its value to you personally. Contributing 20 coins therefore costs you 20 but increases your own return by only 3.8 coins — a clear private loss. The group optimum (everyone contributes) yields higher payoffs for all (38 coins each) than universal free-riding (20 each), but individual incentives push strongly toward free-riding.

Given that tension, I expect:
- Early rounds: a mix of cooperation and free-riding. Some players who are dispositionally more willing to "buy in" or who pledge to cooperate will contribute, while others will free-ride and benefit strongly, making their behavior visible via round payoffs.
- Middle rounds: cooperation will tend to decay as contributors observe they were exploited (others received larger payoffs). Conditional cooperators will often stop contributing after seeing many free-riders.
- Occasional recovery: sparse messages (promises, appeals) can trigger short-lived returns to cooperation from a subset of players, but these are fragile.

This is a classic public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round, and a pot multiplier of 1.9 split equally. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 1.9/10 = 0.19, so each coin you contribute returns only 19% of its value to you personally. Contributing 20 coins therefore costs you 20 but increases your own return by only 3.8 coins — a clear private loss. The group optimum (everyone contributes) yields higher payoffs for all (38 coins each) than universal free-riding (20 each), but individual incentives push strongly toward free-riding.

Given that tension, I expect:
- Early rounds: a mix of cooperation and free-riding. Some players who are dispositionally more willing to "buy in" or who pledge to cooperate will contribute, while others will free-ride and benefit strongly, making their behavior visible via round payoffs.
- Middle rounds: cooperation will tend to decay as contributors observe they were exploited (others received larger payoffs). Conditional cooperators will often stop contributing after seeing many free-riders.
- Occasional recovery: sparse messages (promises, appeals) can trigger short-lived returns to cooperation from a subset of players, but these are fragile.
- Endgame: either an unraveling toward no contributions (common) or a last-round experiment or reciprocal wave from a few players. Uncertainty about others' tendencies and the visibility of peers' payoffs (which reveal others' behavior indirectly) will shape responses.

Messages can influence expectations but typically do not fully overcome the strong individual incentive to free-ride. I will use the provided personas to predict which players are more likely to contribute early and who will drift toward free-riding.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a social return: every coin contributed is multiplied by 3.7 and then evenly split among the 15 players, so each contributed coin returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 coins to each individual. The dominant private incentive is therefore to free-ride (contribute 0) while hoping others contribute. Social enforcement (punishments/rewards) can sustain cooperation: punishments are costly to administer (4 coins per unit to the punisher) but inflict a larger loss on the target (7 coins per punishment unit), while rewards cost 4 and give the recipient 5 — both alter net payoffs substantially and send social signals.

Because players do not know how many rounds remain and cannot identify who targeted them, behavior typically starts with moderate cooperation (many try cooperative contributions as a social default), then adapts: stable contributors are occasionally rewarded, persistent low contributors attract sparse punishment, and ambiguous cases may provoke small rewards for improvement. Punishment and reward are usually used sparingly in lab data; here I predict mostly modest, targeted uses (single-unit actions), with more rewards to steady high contributors and a few punishments aimed at repeat low contributors. Uncertainty about others' motives and the anonymity of sanction sources will keep most players near mid-range contributions rather than swinging to extremes.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with optional costly peer punishment. Each round players choose to contribute either 0 or 20; contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally across all 10 players. Because the individual return from contributing (1.9/10 = 0.19 per coin) is less than 1, the selfish incentive per round is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, if many players contribute, everyone is better off collectively. The presence of punishment allows contributors to sanction free-riders: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and removes 8 coins from the target, so punishment can enforce cooperation but is itself costly.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: a mix of cooperation and defection. Some players (Rabbit, Dog, Chicken) are more likely to contribute; others (Gorilla, Duck, Chick, Sloth) are more likely to free-ride initially.
- Punishment will be used sparingly and targeted: cooperators punish clear free-riders with small punishments (1 unit) rather than heavy multi-unit punishments. Punishment tends to raise cooperation in subsequent rounds when the punished players switch to contributing to avoid further sanctions.
- Mid-game (rounds ~3–9): cooperation becomes more common as key free-riders are disciplined. Periodic one-off defections still occur and get modest punishment.
- End-game (rounds ~11–15): the incentive to free-ride grows as the horizon closes; cooperation tends to erode a bit and punishers become more selective. Some players revert to defecting in later rounds; punishment continues but remains relatively sparse because it is costly and players may conserve coins late in the game or accept a final free-ride.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This public goods game creates a tension between private incentives (keep coins) and collective benefit (contribute to the pot which is multiplied by 3.7 and split). Typical dynamics: many players start by contributing moderately because of social norms and conditional cooperation. Without strong signaling or binding commitments, cooperation often drifts downward over repeated anonymous rounds as players observe free-riding and adjust. The reward stage allows positive incentives: sending reward units is costly to the sender (4 coins per unit) but beneficial to the recipient (adds 5 coins), so reward can sustain cooperation if enough players are willing to pay to encourage high contributors. However, rewards are often used sparingly in real games because they are costly and the donor's identity is not revealed; players tend to reward only a few clear high contributors or to reciprocate perceived generosity. Given the player personas, we expect a mix of conditional cooperators (increase when others contribute/high rewards) and more selfish/anchor-driven players who maintain a fixed pattern. Over nine rounds we predict moderate initial contributions, some positive but limited rewarding that slightly stabilizes cooperation, and a gradual decline toward the later rounds as the costly nature of rewarding and observed free-riding erode contributions.

- Early rounds: many players start with moderate-to-high contributions; a few low contributors appear. Rewarding is selective and rare, typically coming from players who kept some private funds and directed small rewards (1 unit) toward noticeable cooperators.
- Middle rounds: if several players observe persistent free-riding, average contributions decline; conditional cooperators try to stabilize cooperation by occasional rewarding.
- Later rounds: contributions typically drift downward further, but occasional rewards may still be used to rekindle cooperation or reciprocate past generosity. Because reward is expensive, reward edges remain sparse across rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round repeated public goods game with 10 players. Each round every player privately chooses either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to the common pot or to keep it (0 contribution). The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split evenly across all 10 players. That means each contributor’s gross return from the multiplied pot equals 3.8 coins per contributing player; contributors get 3.8*k back after contributing 20 (net = 3.8*k), while defectors get 20 + 3.8*k. The private incentive in every round is to defect (keep 20) because every other player’s contribution increases everyone’s payoff but contributing reduces your private holding by 20 while giving you only 3.8 per contributor in return.

Likely unfolding and uncertainties:
- Early rounds often see some cooperation as people try to establish norms or experiment; messages (promises, requests) can support small cooperative clusters.
- Because payoffs reveal contributors vs free-riders (contributors' net is exactly 20 coins lower than defectors’ nets in a given round), players quickly learn who cooperated previously and can reward or punish socially via messages or conditional strategies.
- Over repeated rounds, with common knowledge of a finite horizon (15 rounds), cooperation typically declines: promising strategies may produce pockets of cooperation but private incentives and endgame reasoning reduce contributions toward the middle and end.
- Uncertainty remains about which players are conditional cooperators vs persistent free-riders; sparse, purposeful chat can temporarily coordinate revival attempts, but retaliation and temptation usually cause cooperation to fall.
- Expect a few early cooperative attempts, occasional short-lived revivals, and frequent defection by many players—especially in later rounds—though a small subgroup may maintain intermittent contributions.

Players do not know how many rounds remain, so behavior typically shows gradual learning and conditional cooperation: people who are rewarded or see others contributing tend to raise or maintain their contributions; low contributors are rarely rewarded and may drift toward free-riding. Across rounds I predict:
- Moderate initial contributions with some heterogeneity.
- Rare, targeted rewards to high contributors (1-unit or occasionally 2) rather than broad transfers.
- Gradual drift: rewarded players increase or maintain contributions; low contributors often remain low or drop.
- No identification of who gave which reward will keep reward patterns strategic but conservative.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round repeated binary public goods game among 10 players. Each round every player chooses either to contribute their full endowment (20) to the group pot or keep it (0). The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so each contributed coin returns 0.19 to every player. That makes contributing privately costly (you lose 20 but everyone gets only 3.8 from one 20 contribution). The dominant individual incentive is to free-ride each round; however social preferences, trust, and messaging can sustain some contributions early on.

Given the supplied personas, I predict a typical pattern: some players with higher cooperative tendencies (e.g., CHICKEN, MOOSE, DUCK, DOG at times) will attempt contributions early, while many others will defect to exploit those contributions. Seeing exploitation quickly lowers cooperation, producing a decline from an initially mixed cooperation level to frequent defection. Occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation will appear after rounds with low returns, but because the game has a known finite horizon, there's pressure toward defection especially late in the game. Chat will be sparse — short commitments, calls for reciprocity, or complaints — and will occasionally drive small temporary coordination.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 identifiable players. The monetary return to a contributed coin is only 0.19 per coin to the contributor (20 coins → 3.8 coins back) so, absent social preferences or punishment, the individual incentive is to keep your 20 (free-ride). However, the group payoff is higher when many contribute because each contributed 20 produces 38 total group value split across players. The punishment mechanism (4 cost to punisher per unit, 8 deduction from target per unit) allows players to impose a deterrent on persistent free-riders; because punishment disproportionately hurts targets (2× the cost to the punisher), modest, targeted punishment can sustain cooperation early–mid game. Expectations:

- Early rounds: some initial free-riding will occur since defecting is dominant; a handful of cooperative players will contribute.
- After a few rounds, cooperators may use targeted punishment against visible repeat defectors, increasing cooperation among those who care about reputation or fairness.
- Punishment is costly, so it will be used sparingly — just enough to deter repeat defection. Some players will persistently defect and attract punishments.
- Toward the end (final few rounds), the deterrent value of punishment falls (no future rounds), so some players will revert to free-riding and punish less. Final-round spiteful punishments can appear but tend to be sparse.

I simulate these tendencies using the provided player priors: a core of contributors (DOG, MOOSE, RABBIT, PARROT and later SLOTH) will initially cooperate and do some early punishment. Some players (OWL, CHICK, sometimes GORILLA) will be more inclined to defect repeatedly. Punishment stays relatively sparse and targeted.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a standard public-goods game with a subsequent costly reward stage. Incentives: each coin you keep yields full private value (1), while each coin you contribute yields a smaller private return because the pot (multiplied by 3.7) is shared among all 15 players — so the private marginal return on contributing is well below 1. Individually, free-riding (contributing 0) is a dominant short-run strategy, but groups do better if many contribute. The reward stage lets players signal approval (or indirectly punish by withholding reward) at a cost: 4 coins to the giver buys 5 coins for the recipient. Rewards are therefore a blunt, costly instrument to promote cooperation.

Likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds typically show some cooperation from conditional cooperators and intrinsically prosocial players, while selfish players contribute little. Because players see peers’ contributions (and rewards) after each round but not who rewarded them, conditional cooperators will adjust contributions based on observed group behavior.
- Rewards are usually sparse and targeted: given their cost, players reward only a few high contributors or reciprocate perceived cooperation. Anonymity about who rewarded whom reduces direct retaliation and encourages only modest reward use.
- Because players do not know the number of rounds, they do not sharply defect in the very last round but cooperation commonly drifts downward over time as expectations of others' contributions fall.
- Expect an initial moderate average contribution with gradual decline across rounds, punctuated by occasional small rewards to sustain a core of cooperators.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private incentives (keep 20 coins) against a shared benefit: contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across the group (15 players). Incentives and likely dynamics:
- Contributing helps everyone (high marginal benefit when many contribute), but is costly to the individual. Free-riding (giving 0) can be attractive if others contribute.
- Punishment and reward allow peers to impose social costs/benefits after seeing contributions. Punishment is expensive for the sender (4 coins per unit) and very costly to the target (7 coins per unit). Rewards are also costly to send (4 coins per unit) but give a modest boost to the receiver (5 coins per unit).
- Uncertainty: players do not know the number of rounds, cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, and cannot see others' choices in advance. That typically encourages conservative behavior early, with modest cooperation that can rise if people use rewards/punishments selectively.
- Likely unfolding: modest initial contributions, sparse use of sanctions in round 1. If low contributors persist, targeted punishment begins and some players use rewards to reinforce cooperation. Because punishment is costly, it will be used sparingly. Over rounds we expect contributions to rise somewhat (as sanctions/rewards take effect) and then drift down slightly through fatigue or opportunistic declines; punishments remain infrequent, rewards occasional and targeted to reliably cooperative players.

game_explanation:
This is a standard one-shot repeated public-goods environment played anonymously over an unknown number of rounds. Each round every player privately gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all 15 players, so each coin contributed returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 coins to the contributor personally — far less than the 1-coin private cost. That creates a strong individual incentive to free-ride while the group optimum is full cooperation (everyone contributes 20). The reward stage gives players a way to incentivize others: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 5 coins. Rewards are costly, so in practice they are used sparingly to reinforce high contributors or (rarely) to reciprocate.

Because players do not know the total number of rounds, early rounds often feature exploratory, somewhat cooperative contributions (hoping to build norms or elicit reciprocal treatment) followed by adjustments as players learn about others. Given the personas (some players tend to be more pro-social or receptive to social cues, some less so), I predict a heterogeneous but stable pattern: a subset of players will contribute moderately-to-high amounts and receive occasional one-unit rewards from a few appreciative peers; several players will contribute little or nothing across rounds. Overall public contributions will decline slowly across rounds as free-riding pays off and costly rewarding remains sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 anonymous players, each given 20 coins per round and deciding how many to contribute to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly, so the individually selfish incentive is to keep coins, while the group benefit pushes toward positive contributions. Because players don't see others' choices before deciding and cannot identify who punished/rewarded them later, behavior will be driven by social preferences, crude reciprocity, and visible summary outcomes across rounds.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players typically start with moderate contributions (not full cooperation nor extreme free-riding). Those with prior tendencies toward cooperation (high “purchase-yes” or framing sensitivity in the personas) will contribute more; low cooperators will contribute less.
- Punishment and reward are costly and therefore used sparingly. Rewards will be used to encourage high contributors; punishment will be directed at persistently low contributors but at low intensity (e.g., 1 unit) because each unit is expensive to the punisher.
- Over rounds: we expect conditional cooperators to raise contributions in response to rewards and punishments aimed at low contributors; persistent low contributors may adjust upward modestly if targeted. Groups typically converge to a moderate cooperation level (around mid-single-digit to low-double-digit contributions from 20), with occasional sparse sanctioning events sustaining cooperation.
- Uncertainties: exact magnitudes and who punishes/rewards are inherently noisy; players can't identify sources in summaries, so strategic targeted retaliation is limited. Because players do not know when the game ends, there is no final-round “unraveling” predicted here.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-player public goods game where each player chooses 0 or 20 each round. The group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so every contributed 20 increases the per-player return by 3.8. Because that per-player gain (3.8) is much smaller than the private cost of contributing (20), the individual incentive is to free-ride every round: regardless of what others do, a single player always gets higher payoff by contributing 0. That makes "contribute 0" the dominant strategy in one-shot thinking.

Still, people vary. Some players are more likely to try cooperating (conditional cooperators, high anchor followers), others tend to defect (low purchase-yes rates). Communication can shape expectations: non-binding pledges or short social cues may produce temporary pockets of cooperation. Over repeated rounds, typical patterns are: early experimentation / a few pledges and some contributions; when free-riders earn more, many contributors drop out; occasional stubborn cooperators continue to contribute, and intermittent coordination attempts appear in chat. Because players see every peer's net payoff at summary, they can infer whether others contributed and update expectations, which tends to reinforce defection unless a stable cooperative norm is enforced — which is unlikely here given the payoff structure.

The transcript below simulates those dynamics across 15 rounds. I keep chat sparse and purposeful; contributions are either 0 or 20. At each round start I explain the expected short-term dynamics, then show any pre-decision chat, the contributions line and the round summary (net payoffs shown to all players).

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with 16 players for 17 rounds. Each round players choose how many of 20 coins to contribute to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so each coin contributed yields 4.5/16 = 0.28125 coins back to each player (including the contributor). Private incentives favor free-riding (keeping coins) while group incentives favor contributing. Because players can reward others after seeing contributions, rewarding can sustain cooperation by providing direct positive feedback to cooperators (albeit at a cost to the rewarder). Uncertainty at the start of the game is about others' willingness to cooperate; early rounds often show relatively high contributions (optimism or conditional cooperation), then decline if free-riding appears — unless rewards are used to stabilize cooperation.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix: some reliably cooperative types (e.g., DUCK, FROG, COW) will contribute relatively high amounts early; some low-contributing types (e.g., CROCODILE, PARROT, RABBIT) will free-ride. Rewarding will be sparse but purposeful: a few players will pay to reward clear cooperators early on, which signals reciprocity and can sustain contributions for several rounds. As patterns of free-riding appear, some players will reduce contributions; occasional targeted rewards will attempt to restore cooperation. Chat is likely to be scarce and strategic (praise, short calls for fairness).

game_explanation:
This public goods game is a repeated 17-round interaction with simultaneous contributions each round and a punishment/reward stage after contributions are revealed. Early rounds usually see moderate-to-high contributions driven by conditional cooperators giving a positive anchor; visible low contributions often trigger a small number of punishments (cheap for the punisher, costly to the punished here), while consistent high contributors can attract a few rewards. Over repeated rounds players learn who cooperates and who free-rides; conditional cooperators tend to raise contributions after being rewarded or after seeing others punished, and free-riders often increase contributions if they are singled out for punishment. Punishment is used sparingly in most real experiments because it costs the sender, but when it is cheap relative to its effect (as here), a few actors may use it to enforce norms. Toward the end of the game, backward-induction/end-game effects typically cause contributions to decline — players reduce cooperation as the horizon approaches and the effectiveness of future enforcement dwindles. Chat messages are usually sparse and targeted (requests, short promises, or justifications).

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game pits private incentives (each coin kept yields 1 privately) against a social return (every coin contributed is multiplied by 4.5 and then split among 16 players, so each contributor gets 0.28125 back per coin). The individually rational move is to contribute 0; socially efficient is full contribution. Expect heterogeneous behavior: some players (conditional cooperators or those with pro-social priors) start by contributing moderately-high amounts, while others free-ride. Early rounds often show moderate cooperation that can be sustained by a few small rewards and brief messages. As rounds progress, uncertainty about others' future behavior and the endgame (round 17) typically push average contributions down. Rewards (costly to the sender: 4 coins per reward unit, giving 2 coins to the target) will be used sparingly to reinforce high contributors. Chats, when used, will be short moral appeals or brief acknowledgements. Over rounds expect: (1) initial moderate cooperation, (2) mid-game erosion as free-riding spreads, (3) sparse targeted rewarding to sustain cooperators, and (4) a final-round mix of symbolic gestures (some small contributions or concentrated rewards), with overall average contributions lower than at the start.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG </PLAYERS>

This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players each endowed with 20 coins per round. Each player's contribution is private until revealed; contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players may punish or reward others (punishment: costs 4 to punisher, -15 to target per unit; reward: costs 4 to giver, +2 to target per unit). Players can send chat messages at any time. Players know the horizon (17 rounds), so end-game effects (declining cooperation) are likely.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation as many prefer fairness/cooperation, with a few low contributors (free riders). Players with higher cooperative priors (DOG, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN) will tend to contribute more; analytically oriented players (RABBIT, OWL) may contribute less or strategically experiment.
- Mid game: some enforcement appears. Because punishment inflicts a large net hit on targets (-15 per unit), even a single punitive unit is powerful; players will punish sparingly but will target clear free-riders. Rewards are less efficient (giver loses 4 to give target +2) and will be used infrequently to reinforce cooperation or thank contributors.
- Retaliation and targeted responses: punished players can identify who punished them, so occasional retaliation may occur early/mid game; over time players learn the group norms and punish less if cooperation improves.
- End game (last few rounds): contributions tend to fall as future retaliation/incentives vanish; punishments decline because they are costly and have no future enforcement benefit.

I'll simulate a plausible transcript, round-by-round, with sparse, purposeful chat, occasional targeted punishments and a few rewards, showing contributions and only non-zero sanction actions each round.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with optional positive rewards. Early rounds tend to show substantial cooperation: many players contribute a meaningful share of their 20 coins because there's no punishment history yet and expectations of reciprocity are high. Over repeated rounds, however, free-riding incentives push average contributions downward unless players invest in costly rewards to sustain cooperation. Rewarding is expensive (4 coins per reward unit) and gives a small benefit (2 coins per unit) to the recipient; so rewards are used sparingly and only when a giver expects the payoff to be worth it (e.g., to encourage reliable cooperators or reciprocate past generosity). Messages are rare but meaningful — players use chat to praise cooperators, call out freeloaders, or announce reward plans. Toward the middle rounds, I expect cooperation to drift down as some players reduce contributions after observing low peers; consistent high contributors and a few high-anchor personalities will keep contributing relatively more. In late rounds, the “end-game” effect pushes many toward minimal contributions, but a subset of steady cooperators may still be rewarded by others who value fairness or reputation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds, and after each contribution stage players can spend private coins to punish (costly, very damaging to the target) or reward (costly to source, small benefit to target). Typical dynamics: initial rounds show middling-to-high contributions as many players try to cooperate; occasional low contributors (free-riders) get noticed and may be punished or socially called out. Punishment is used sparingly because it is costly to the punisher, but it can effectively deter future defection; small rewards are used occasionally to reinforce high contributions. Over rounds we expect adjustments: punished players increase contributions; reciprocating cooperators reward each other lightly; some players will drift toward lower contributions near the end (final-round effect) because punishment cannot be used afterward. Chat messages will be sparse and mainly requests, reminders, or short protest/thanks.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players, repeated-play incentives, and optional costly punishment and rewarding after contributions. Each coin a player keeps yields immediate private value of 1, while each coin contributed to the group generates 4.5 total to the pot and is split across 16 players (≈0.281 per player per coin). The individually dominant action each round is to contribute 0; group welfare is highest if everyone contributes all 20. Because players can see past behavior only via summaries (not others' full histories) and can identify who punished/rewarded them in the summary, social enforcement can emerge but is costly: a punishment unit is highly punitive to the target (-15) while costing the punisher 4; rewards cost 4 and give the target only +2. That asymmetry makes punishment powerful but expensive to enforce—and so punishments are used sparingly and typically only when some players repeatedly free-ride or to signal that low contributions will be sanctioned. Rewards are used occasionally to reinforce high contributors but are less efficient per cost.

Given the player priors (some players have high tendency toward cooperation / high purchase rates, others low), I expect initial rounds to show a mix: several high contributors (people with strong pro-social priors) and a handful of low contributors/free-riders. Early chat may include appeals to cooperate. Rare punitive actions will appear against obvious low contributors; those targeted tend to raise contributions in subsequent rounds, while some other players drift toward lower contributions unless social enforcement occurs. Rewards will be rare and small, used to reinforce top contributors. Overall trend: contributions drift down slowly unless occasional punishments temporarily re-align incentives and raise contributions for a few rounds. Chat remains sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses a contribution (0–20) to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After contributions are revealed and redistributed, players may send costly rewards: each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Players also can send chat messages at any time.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically show moderate cooperation as many players try contributing a fair share (often around half the endowment). A few players with more selfish priors will start lower, but visible rewards to cooperators can sustain higher contributions for some players.
- Rewarding is sparse and targeted: players usually reward high contributors to signal approval or to sustain cooperation, rather than punishing low contributors (there’s no punishment mechanism here).
- Over the middle rounds we expect conditional cooperators to adjust downward in response to observed low contributions or weak returns; free-riders’ influence grows.
- Near the end (last few rounds) an “end-game” decline is common: contributions fall as future retaliation or reciprocal incentives lose force.
- Chat is used infrequently but can be used to signal intentions, thank rewarders, or ask for cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 16 players, 17 rounds, and an available reward stage after contributions. Each round players privately choose an integer contribution (0–20) from their 20-coin endowment; the group pot is multiplied by 4.5 and divided equally. After seeing the contributions, players may spend coins to reward others (4 coins cost to the giver for +2 coins to the target). Players can send chat messages at any time. Because the multiplier is >1, group contributions increase everyone’s earnings, but individual incentives favor under-contribution (free-riding). Rewards are costly to the giver and only partially benefit the recipient, so they are sparse in practice and used mainly to signal approval or encourage reciprocation rather than to directly boost social welfare. Over 17 rounds we expect:

- Early rounds: moderate cooperation (many mid-to-high contributions) and a few sparse rewards aimed at consistent high contributors.
- Middle rounds: some drift/downward pressure as free-riding appears; occasional targeted rewards try to sustain cooperation.
- Late rounds: mixed behavior — some players reduce contributions (end-game effect), while a subset of consistent cooperators continues contributing and exchanging small rewards. Chat is infrequent but often used to praise or call out cooperation; reward edges are rare and targeted.

This is a 17-round repeated public goods game with 16 identified players who can also punish or reward each other after seeing contributions. Each round players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute. Contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so cooperating benefits the group but costs the individual directly. Punishment is an expensive, asymmetric tool (source pays 4 per unit, target loses 15 per unit) and can deter free-riding; reward is cheaper for the target (source pays 4 per unit, target gains 2 per unit) and can reinforce cooperation. Because players can send short messages and identify who punished/rewarded them, social signaling matters: a few early punishments or rewards can establish norms that other players follow, but endgame incentives (round 17 approaching) create uncertainty and a possible decline in cooperation late in the game.

I predict modest initial contributions with a handful of low contributors (testing or opportunistic). A few cooperators will use selective punishment or rewarding to enforce norms, producing a gradual rise in average contributions mid-game. Punishment will be used sparingly (one or two edges per round typically) because it's costly to the punisher; rewards will be occasional compliments to high contributors. Toward the final rounds contributions may drift down slightly as players anticipate no future repercussions, with a small increase in direct punishment in response to visible free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with costly, positive rewarding after contributions. Each round players choose how much of 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 then split evenly among the 16 players (so each contributed coin returns 4.5/16 ≈ 0.281 to the contributor via the public good). Individually, contributing is costly, but mutual contributions raise total payoffs. Because the game is finite (17 rounds), we expect some tension between short-term incentives to free-ride and longer-run conditional cooperation. Rewarding lets players transfer social payoffs (cost 4 to giver, benefit 2 to receiver) to encourage cooperators or thank them — but rewards are costly so they tend to be used sparingly and mostly to reinforce high contributors.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: a number of players (those with higher prosocial tendencies) start by contributing moderately to highly; others free-ride or contribute little. A few early rewards are directed at the clear cooperators (visible after contributions), creating small pockets of reciprocal reinforcement.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators sustain contributions if they continue being rewarded or see steady cooperation; free-riders may remain low. Overall contributions drift downward but are supported by occasional targeted rewards. Chat is sparse — short coordination or thank-you messages.
- Late rounds: as the horizon nears, strategic incentives to defect grow and overall contributions decline further. Rewarding becomes rarer (costly and less strategic), although some pairwise reciprocal rewards may persist as social gestures.

We will simulate likely contributions, sparse messages, and sparse reward transfers round-by-round.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round, players privately decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After payoffs from the pot are determined, players may punish or reward others (punishment: costs 4 to sender and deducts 15 from the target per unit; reward: costs 4 to sender and gives +2 to the target per unit). Players can also send short chat messages.

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- The social optimum is high contributions from everyone, but the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) and keep private coins. Because the multiplier (4.5) divided among 16 players yields a modest return per contributed coin, individual payoff-maximizing contributions are low.
- Punishment is a very strong deterrent (targets lose 15 per punishment unit), but it is costly (4 to the punisher). Because punishments are identifiable, they can deter low contributions or provoke retaliation. Rewards are relatively weak (cost 4, benefit +2), so rewards primarily serve as signaling/reciprocal gestures rather than strong material incentives.
- Expect a mix of moderate initial cooperation (many players contribute mid-range values like 10–15) as players try to build reciprocity. Occasional low contributions (free-riders) will be met with sparse but targeted punishment by some players. Punishment will be used sparingly—enough to discipline persistent low contributors but not so much as to destabilize the group through mutual retaliation. Rewards will be rarer and used mostly to thank high contributors or to encourage cooperation.
- Over time contributions often dip (end-game effect) unless punishment/reward maintain cooperation; with 17 rounds, we predict some fluctuations: early moderate cooperation, occasional declines midgame, and modest recovery where punishers keep free-riding in check. Chats will be few and purposeful (calls to cooperate, short reprimands, occasional thanks).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot public-goods game played for a known 17 rounds. Each round players choose how much of 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After redistribution, players may spend coins to punish (cost 4 per unit, deducts 15 per unit from the target) or reward (cost 4 per unit, gives +2 per unit to the target). Knowing the game length, standard incentives push toward declining contributions toward the final rounds (backward induction), but real human behavior typically shows initially positive cooperation that erodes over time. Punishment and reward are used sparingly: punishment tends to be used in response to obvious low contributions to enforce norms, reward is used occasionally to reinforce cooperative behavior. Messages are infrequent and usually used to appeal for cooperation, apologize, or signal intent.

I therefore predict:
- Early rounds: moderate-to-high contributions (people hope to build cooperation).
- Occasional defectors (very low contributions) provoke a small number of punishments from norm-enforcers; a couple of players use one-unit rewards to reinforce cooperators.
- Mid game: contributions fluctuate, but remain moderate as punishments keep some norm compliance.
- Late game: contributions decline substantially toward zero as the end approaches; punishment and reward largely fade.
- Chats are sparse and purposeful (requests, short apologies, thanks).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 16 players for 17 rounds. Each round everyone privately gets 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a common pot (0–20). The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split evenly across all 16 players, so each coin contributed returns 4.5/16 ≈ 0.281 to the contributor — individually costly but beneficial for the group. After contributions are revealed, players can spend costly reward units (4 coins per unit) to give others a small transfer (2 coins per unit). Rewards are observable (recipients see who rewarded them). Because rewards are expensive, players use them sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior. With a known finite horizon (17 rounds), we expect:

- Early rounds: a mix of cooperative signals and some free-riding. Prosocial players will contribute moderate amounts to set norms; a few rewards target high contributors to sustain cooperation.
- Middle rounds: contributions may drift downward as free-riders exploit cooperators; targeted rewarding by a few reciprocators can stabilize cooperation among a core subgroup.
- Late rounds: contributions tend to decline further, and in the final rounds many players reduce contributions toward zero (end-game defection). Rewarding becomes rarer; when it occurs it is often directed from loyal cooperators to other cooperators as a moral gesture rather than strategic investment.

Chats tend to be sparse and purposeful (praise, appeals, or short strategy signals). Rewards are also sparse and typically 1 unit at a time; players who reward multiple people in a round are rare due to the high cost.

Rewards are observable (recipients see who rewarded them). Because rewards are expensive, players use them sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior. With a known finite horizon (17 rounds), we expect:

- Early rounds: a mix of cooperative signals and some free-riding. Prosocial players will contribute moderate amounts to set norms; a few rewards target high contributors to sustain cooperation.
- Middle rounds: contributions may drift downward as free-riders exploit cooperators; targeted rewarding by a few reciprocators can stabilize cooperation among a core subgroup.
- Late rounds: contributions tend to decline further, and in the final rounds many players reduce contributions toward zero (end-game defection). Rewarding becomes rarer; when it occurs it is often directed from loyal cooperators to other cooperators as a moral gesture rather than strategic investment.

Chats tend to be sparse and purposeful (praise, appeals, or short strategy signals). Rewards are also sparse and typically 1 unit at a time; players who reward multiple people in a round are rare due to the high cost.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 identified players. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split evenly. After payoffs are known, players may punish (very costly to target: −15 per punishment unit; cost 4 to sender) or reward (adds +2 to target per unit; cost 4 to sender). Messages may be sent anytime.

Likely overall dynamics:
- Early rounds: a mix of conditional cooperators and a few low contributors. Players test others’ behavior; small, targeted punishments or rewards will be used to signal norms.
- Middle rounds: if punishments and occasional rewards successfully discourage free-riding, contributions rise and stabilize at a higher level — many players will reciprocate cooperation. Punishment remains sparse because a single unit is highly punitive.
- Late rounds (endgame): forward-looking players reduce contributions as the horizon approaches; cooperation softens in rounds 16–17. Some last-round punishments/rewards may be used for signaling or retaliation, but overall incentives to sustain cooperation fall.

Uncertainties: how many players are strong conditional cooperators vs. persistent free-riders, and whether players will use the strong punishment technology sparingly (most likely) or overuse it (unlikely because it’s costly to senders regardless). Chats will be rare but used to request contributions, complain about punishment, or thank rewarders.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-player repeated public goods game lasting 17 rounds. Each round players choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split evenly, so the social optimum is full contribution (everyone gives 20) but the individual incentive is to free-ride (keep your 20 while still receiving a share of others' contributions). Because the game is finite and known to end after 17 rounds, we should expect some mix of behaviors:

- Early rounds: many players test each other, with moderately high contributions from conditional cooperators and low contributions from willing free-riders. Small amounts of punishment and reward may be used to signal norms (punish clear free-riders, reward reliable contributors).
- Middle rounds: cooperation often drifts downward as some players reduce contributions in response to observed free-riding and because future retaliation is discounted. Sparse, targeted punishment and occasional rewarding of steady cooperators can stabilize cooperation somewhat.
- Late rounds: because there is no future after round 17, some players shift toward free-riding, and costly punishment becomes less frequent (less future benefit). A few players still contribute out of preference or reciprocity, and isolated rewards/punishments may appear but are rare.

Uncertainty: we do not know exact preferences or risk attitudes beyond the rough personas. That leads to heterogeneous behavior: some avatars repeatedly contribute and sometimes pay to punish defectors; others exploit the group. Messages (chat) are rare, used to signal threats (“I will punish”) or appeals (“please contribute”).

Below is a simulated transcript from round 1 through round 17. Each round begins with a short explanation, then contributions (in the exact avatar order given), sparse chat, the sparse punishment/reward actions (only non-zero edges are listed), and then the per-player round summary (each player sees their own round payoff and their own punishment/reward amounts). Numeric payoffs are rounded to two decimals.

- Rewards let players give positive reinforcement at private cost (cost 4 per reward unit; recipient gains 2 per unit). Rewards typically sustain cooperation by compensating cooperators, but because they are costly to givers, they are used sparingly and strategically.
- Early rounds typically see moderate-to-high contributions as players try cooperation; those who contribute and receive rewards tend to keep cooperating. Persistent low contributors (free-riders) rarely get rewarded and can stay low.
- Knowing the horizon (17 rounds) produces end-game decline: players reduce contributions near the end because reciprocity value falls. Rewards will also taper off late.
- Chat is sparse and mostly used to signal intentions (promises to reward cooperators, mild praise or complaint).
- Expect: an initial cooperative norm supported by occasional small rewards; mid-game stability for many cooperators; gradual unraveling in rounds 15–17 with falling contributions and fewer rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round repeated public goods game with 16 identifiable players. Each round players choose an integer 0–20 to contribute from a private endowment of 20. Contributions are multiplied by 4.5 then split equally among all 16 players. After contributions are revealed, players may spend coins to punish or reward others (each punishment unit costs the sender 4 and subtracts 15 from the target; each reward unit costs the sender 4 and gives the target +2). Players can also send chat messages.

Likely dynamics and incentives
- Early rounds: many players start with moderate contributions (around half the endowment). Some players are inclined to cooperate (higher contributions), others are more self-interested or slow to engage, so contributions will be heterogeneous from the start.
- Social enforcement: because punishments are powerful (targets lose 15 per unit), a few players who value cooperation (or who like policing) will punish low contributors selectively. Punishment is costly, so it will be used sparingly and often targeted at clear free-riders. Rewards are used occasionally to encourage high contributors.
- Conditional cooperation: many players will adjust contributions based on the previous round’s mean and whether they were punished or rewarded. If punishment/reward is effective, contributions can stabilize at moderate-high levels. If enforcement is absent or too costly, contributions tend to drift downward, especially toward the final rounds.
- Endgame: in the final round (17), incentives to contribute drop sharply (no future rounds), so expect many players to reduce their contributions. Punishment also falls off near the end because punishers anticipate fewer long-term benefits from enforcing norms.

Uncertainties
- Who acts as a punisher/rewarder is uncertain; personas suggest a few players (e.g., OWL, MONKEY, DOG, CHICK) are more likely to take active enforcement or write messages, while others (SLOTH, SNAKE) are more likely to defect or contribute less.
- Retaliation cycles are possible if punished players recognize punishers and respond in kind, but because punishment is costly and extremely damaging to targets, most players avoid escalating.

Below is a round-by-round transcript that follows these patterns: mostly moderate contributions early, targeted punishment/reward used sparingly, conditional adjustments, and lower contributions in the final round. Chat is sparse and purposeful.

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game creates tension between private incentives (keep coins) and group benefit (each contributed coin yields more total group return). Individually, players earn less by contributing because each coin returns only ~0.28 coins to each player, so the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride. However, social enforcement via punishment (costly to sender, harsh to target) and occasional rewards can shift behavior: a few early punishments targeted at low contributors tend to raise average contributions as players avoid costly sanctions. Messages can coordinate norms but are noisy and sparse.

Expect an initial phase of mixed contributions (some conditional cooperators try moderate gifts while some free-ride). A few targeted punishments in early rounds will nudge low contributors up; rewards will occasionally reinforce high contributors. Once cooperation becomes common, punishments decrease. Because the horizon is finite and known (17 rounds), expect contributions to hold fairly high through the middle rounds, then decline near the last rounds as backward-looking incentives and "end-game" effects reduce the value of sanctions. Chats are likely infrequent and focused on encouragement, complaints after being punished, or short coordination pleas.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players, private endowments, a shared pot multiplier (4.5) and a separate voluntary positive-reward stage. Because each contributed coin returns less than one coin to its contributor (4.5/16 ≈ 0.281 per coin), the one-shot incentive is to free-ride. Repetition and the reward stage create scope for conditional cooperation: some players will contribute to sustain a high group return and try to encourage others with rewards; others will defect or remain low contributors to exploit cooperators. Expect an initial cluster of relatively high contributors (those with cooperative priors) and a persistent minority of low contributors or free-riders. Early rounds will show more rewarding targeted at visible cooperators; over time, as the game is finite and players update on who reciprocates, contributions will tend to drift downward. Rewarding is costly (4 coins per unit) and modest in impact (+2 to recipient), so it will be used sparingly — often to signal appreciation or to sustain a few core cooperators. Near the end (final rounds) forward-looking players will reduce rewards and contributions because future reciprocity opportunities vanish, producing an unraveling of cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a simultaneous-choice public goods game with 19 players. Each round every player either withdraws the full 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leaves the 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 11.5 and divided equally across all 19 players. That makes the per-player return from the public pot equal to 12.1052631579 times the number of contributors (k). A player who defects (keeps 20) always gets 20 coins more than a cooperator in the same round, so defection is the dominant individual strategy. The group's total payoff is maximized when everyone contributes.

Because players do not see choices before deciding and do not know the number of rounds, we expect some initial optimism and conditional cooperation from a subset of players. Seeing round outcomes (payoffs) afterward provides strong feedback: defectors earn a clear advantage each round, and many players will adjust toward defection over time. Persona-based priors (some players more willing to "purchase/commit," others more strategic or skeptical) introduce heterogeneity: a small core of cooperators may persist for several rounds, but aggregate cooperation is likely to decay toward full defection by later rounds. Uncertainty about the horizon reduces a pure endgame effect, so decline is gradual rather than abrupt only at the end.

Expect moderate cooperation from those with higher cooperation cues; others test by defecting to see payoffs.

After seeing Round 1, some contributors who observed defectors earning more reduce cooperation. A mix of conditional cooperators (who still trust others a bit) and a few experimenters keep contributing; others switch to defection to secure the immediate +20 advantage.

Continuing feedback: a few more players abandon cooperation after seeing rounds where defectors consistently earned more. A smaller subset still contributes (habitual cooperators or those testing whether cooperation can be restored).
Cooperation continues to erode as more players switch to the safer private withdrawal. A small core (some with high pro-cooperation priors) still contribute, but overall k falls noticeably.
By now the majority prefer the private withdrawal. Only a few committed cooperators remain. Payoffs make the tradeoff clear: the immediate +20 for defecting remains compelling, so k drops further.

Most players have moved to defection. Only a very small number of persistent cooperators remain (habitual or strongly pro-social). The returns to cooperation are visible but insufficient to overcome the private incentive for most.
Only one or two very persistent cooperators remain; the majority exploit the public good. With visible past rounds showing defectors consistently ahead, cooperation is close to collapse.

By now cooperation has collapsed completely: all players defect. With no contributors, the public pot is empty and everyone gets only their private withdrawal.
With repeated evidence that defection pays out more, and no end-of-game information to encourage last-round cooperation, the state from Round 9 persists: everyone defects again.

game_explanation:
This is a 19-player one-shot public‑goods decision repeated over unknown rounds. Each player faces a simple binary choice each round: withdraw the whole 20 privately (contribute 0) or leave the 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The group total contribution is multiplied by a large factor (11.5) and shared equally among all players. That creates the classic tension: individual payoff is always higher when you withdraw the 20 privately unless the group is sufficiently cooperative; contributors pay a private cost (give up 20) to raise everyone’s payoff modestly. With 19 players, one person’s contribution only adds 11.5*20/19 ≈ 12.11 to everyone’s payoff — less than the 20 you forgo — so unilateral defection is attractive.

Given heterogenous priors (the provided personas), I expect an initial mix of optimism and conditional cooperation from the higher “purchase-yes” / pro‑cooperation types (e.g., SLOTH, OWL, CHICKEN, ELEPHANT, HORSE). Others with lower cooperative priors (e.g., FROG, MOOSE, several low purchase-yes scorers) tend toward defection. Because players do not know the game length, repeated reciprocity could sustain cooperation, but the per-person benefit from a single contribution is low relative to the private withdrawal, so cooperation should erode: early rounds may show some cooperation, the payoff feedback will highlight that defectors earned more, and cooperation will collapse toward everyone taking the 20. The main uncertainty is how stubborn some high-cooperation types (notably SLOTH) are: they may keep contributing a few rounds even when it’s costly, briefly slowing collapse.

Below is a round-by-round predicted transcript (round explanations, contributions in avatar order, and the round summary each player sees). Contributions are shown as the amount left in the shared pot (0 or 20). Payoffs are net round payoffs = private withdrawal (20 if they withdrew, 0 if they contributed) + equal share of the multiplied pot.

game_explanation:
This is a simultaneous, repeated public goods game with 19 players. Each round every player's decision is binary: either withdraw all 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by a large factor (11.5) and then split equally among all 19 players; that makes the group payoff increase with the number of contributors, but each individual has a constant private incentive to withdraw (defect). Concretely, for k contributors the per-player share from the pot is (11.5 * 20 * k) / 19 ≈ 12.10526316 * k. A single player who defects earns 20 more than a cooperator regardless of others’ choices (defection strictly dominates).

Given the player personas, I expect a few initially cooperative choices from players with stronger pro-social or framing-sensitive tendencies, but those cooperators will earn less than nearby defectors in the first summary screens. Because players see everyone’s round payoffs (their own and peers’), the visible advantage of defecting will tend to accelerate decline in contributions. Also, players do not know the total number of rounds, which reduces end-game cooperation opportunities and further favors defection over time. So the likely unfolding: a small initial cluster of contributors in round 1, followed by rapid erosion across rounds 2–4 as more players switch to the privately better withdrawal, reaching near or full defection for the remainder of the game. Below I simulate one plausible path consistent with those incentives and the personas.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with binary contribution choices each round (contribute 20 coins to the shared pot, or withdraw all 20 for private use). The shared pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally across the (19) players, so if many players contribute everyone benefits; if many defect, public returns shrink. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, forward-looking endgame defection is less obvious — participants tend to behave conditionally on others' past behavior. Punishment is costly (2 coins per punishment unit to the punisher) but effective (removes 3 coins from the target per unit), and players can see how much each peer spent on punishment (but not who punished whom). That tends to create incentives for conditional cooperation enforced by occasional punishment: cooperating players will sometimes punish visible defectors to raise future cooperation; defectors may respond by switching to cooperation if targeted enough. Because punishment is costly, in most rounds punishment will be used sparingly and targeted to a few persistent defectors. Early rounds usually show a fair amount of cooperation, punishments are used to discipline clear free-riders, and cooperation tends to increase and stabilize (with occasional lapses) as players learn peers' patterns.

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round: no history. Many players with pro-social or moderate purchase tendencies will default to contributing (norm compliance), while a few with low purchase-yes rates or strong strategic tendencies may free-ride. Because contributions are binary and punishment is available after contribution revelation, some cooperators will punish obvious defectors to signal that free-riding is costly.

After being punished, a couple of first-round defectors (especially those who were singled out) are likely to switch to contributing to avoid future costs. Other defectors who weren't targeted may continue to free-ride. Punishment remains targeted and modest: cooperators focus on the few persistent non-contributors.

By round 3 there is a clearer picture: a small set of players (SNAKE, MONKEY, COW, WHALE, maybe ELEPHANT) are still defecting. Cooperators concentrate punishment on the most persistent or visible defectors. Because punishment is costly, multiple players may apply small punishments rather than a single large one — SNAKE, as a repeated free-rider, receives somewhat escalated punishment.

Some previously targeted players (like COW) have now felt the cost of punishment and switch to contributing; a few stubborn defectors (SNAKE, MONKEY, WHALE, ELEPHANT) persist. Punishment continues to be directed at those remaining defectors, but overall the group is trending toward higher cooperation.

Repeated punishment has a consolidating effect: a previously persistent defector (SNAKE) now switches to contributing to avoid further hits, leaving only a small handful or a single stubborn free-rider (WHALE). Punishment now is light and targeted.

WHALE, having been singled out and punished, switches to contributing. Group cooperation reaches near-universal levels. With almost everyone contributing, there is little incentive to punish and punishers conserve resources.

Once cooperation is high, occasional lapses can occur from inattention or “slippage” (e.g., a low-engagement player choosing to defect). When that happens, cooperators usually respond with modest punishment to deter repetition. Because punishments are visible as spend amounts, the punished player can infer social disapproval even if they can't identify the specific punisher.

Following the round-7 punishments, the lapse is corrected: the player who slipped returns to contributing. With near-universal cooperation, players conserve punishment resources; punishments fall to zero.

Even in stable cooperation, isolated deviations sometimes happen (random noise, mistakes, or momentary temptation). Cooperators again respond minimally to discourage contagion. Punishment remains selective and small.

With no knowledge that the game is ending, most players stick to the established cooperative norm. Given the recent history of selective punishment maintaining cooperation, players converge on contributing; punishments are unnecessary in a cooperative final round.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits a simple binary cooperation choice (contribute all 20 coins or withdraw them all) against the possibility of costly punishment. With punishment available, we expect an initial mix of cooperation and free-riding, followed by targeted, sparse punishments aimed at persistent defectors. Because punishments cost the sender (2 coins/unit) but reduce a target's payoff more (3 coins/unit), punishers pay a price to impose a stronger penalty; that tends to establish cooperation but only if punishers are willing to bear the cost. Players do not know how many rounds remain, which reduces straightforward end-game defection and favors continued enforcement.

Given the provided personas, many players are predisposed to follow norms or react to framing cues (so a core of cooperators will emerge quickly), while a minority are more likely to defect initially. Punishment is usually used sparingly in real games, directed at a few repeat defectors, after which most players converge to cooperation. Because identities of punishers are hidden, punishment acts as a public deterrent even if the punished cannot single out who acted.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game sets up a strong private incentive to withdraw the full 20 coins each round (so contribute 0) because any single player's private withdrawal yields 20 to them, while the added public return from contributing 20 is shared across all players. Concretely, one player's contribution of 20 raises the multiplied pot by 11.5*20 = 230, which is then split across the group; with 19 players here, the contributor's own share of that marginal addition is 230/19 ≈ 12.11 — well below 20. That means, holding others fixed, withdrawing (defecting) strictly increases your round payoff.

However, conditional and prosocial tendencies (some players have higher “purchase-yes” or framing sensitivity scores in their personas) can lead to some initial cooperation. Because players do not see others' choices before deciding but do see round summaries (everyone's net payoff each round), they can learn about how much others cooperated and update behavior. Given the payoff structure and no communication, the typical dynamic is:
- Early rounds: some mixture of cooperation and defection as prosocial types test cooperation.
- After observing that defectors earn more (they get the private 20 plus the public share), many players switch to withdrawing (defect), so cooperation tends to decline rapidly.
- Occasional attempts to restart cooperation can appear (by players with strong cooperative priors), but the structural incentive usually means those attempts are punished by free-riding and often fail.
- Unknown horizon (players do not know how many rounds remain) sometimes dampens end-game collapse a little, but the dominant incentive to withdraw typically still drives cooperation down over repeated play.

Below is a round-by-round transcript (round explanations show why particular patterns are expected, then the contributions and the summary payoffs that players see). All contribution lists and payoff lists follow the same avatar order given in the scaffold.

After round 2 the pattern is clear: withdrawing gives a higher round payoff than contributing. Many previously cooperative players switch to withdrawing. A very small subset (some persistent cooperators) attempt to contribute again—often those who favor prosocial choices or who keep testing whether cooperation can be sustained.
