After an all-defect round, a few players with persistent cooperative priors (and maybe optimism that others might reciprocate) try to restart cooperation. A small cluster contributes, but defecting still yields the higher immediate payoff.

After seeing the payoff signal that defectors again do better, most players withdraw. The safe, dominant choice dominates expectations; round 9 is all withdrawal.

Final round in this transcript: some players sometimes attempt a last cooperative gesture, or expect others to defect and therefore withdraw. Here one persistent cooperator makes a final contribution; most withdraw, capturing the private benefit.

game_explanation:
This is a large-group public goods game with a strong private incentive to withdraw (defect) because each 20-coin contribution yields only ≈12.11 coins back to each player (230/19 ≈ 12.11), so an individual who contributes loses about 7.89 coins relative to withdrawing. That makes unilateral defection individually attractive. The key mechanisms that can sustain cooperation here are social preferences (some players intrinsically prefer to contribute), conditional cooperation (players matching others' behavior across rounds), and costly punishment: after returns are observed players can spend 2 coins per punishment unit to deduct 3 coins per unit from a target.

Because punishment is private and punishers remain anonymous in the summaries, it can deter free-riding without enabling direct retaliation, but sending punishment still costs the punisher 2 coins each time, so punishment tends to be used sparingly in real PGGs. Uncertainty about the number of rounds (players do not know when the game stops) reduces simple end-game collapse but also encourages experimentation: some players test strategies (defecting briefly) to see whether others punish, and punished defectors often switch back to contributing in subsequent rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 20 players each round deciding whether to leave 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw them for private use (contribute 0). The group pot is amplified by 11.5 and split equally, so each contributor increases everyone's per-player share by 11.5. However, a single player's private incentive is always to withdraw (keep 20) because a withdrawer gets the same shared return as others plus the 20 kept privately — i.e., each withdrawer earns 20 more than a contributor in the same round. That makes contribution individually costly but collectively valuable: full cooperation (all 20 contribute) yields 230 per person; universal withdrawal yields 20 per person.

Players do not know how many rounds remain, see only numeric payoffs (their own and each peer's) after each round, and cannot communicate. Given the provided personas, expect heterogeneous tendencies: a few players (those with higher "purchase-yes" or anchoring tendencies) are more likely to try contributing early or to retry cooperation after being exploited; many will default to withdrawing because it's the dominant single-round payoff-maximizing action. Observability of round payoffs means contributions (contributors vs withdrawers) are easily inferred each round — contributors will show payoffs exactly 20 less than withdrawers in the same round (since everyone gets the same shared share). That clarity tends to produce fast learning: initial cooperative attempts are usually exploited, and most players switch to withdrawing in subsequent rounds. Still, occasional "experiments" or persistent conditional cooperators may reintroduce small levels of contribution intermittently. Over 10 rounds I predict: an initial minority of contributors, quick exploitation and decline in cooperation to near-zero, with a few sporadic cooperative experiments and a small late attempt at higher cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 20 players, each with a binary contribution choice each round: either contribute the full 20 coins to the public pot (withdraw 0) or withdraw the full 20 coins privately (contribute 0). The public pot is amplified by a factor of 11.5 and split equally across all 20 players. Because the private retention of 20 is always kept by a withdrawer while the public return per contributor is 11.5 times the number of contributors divided by 20, the one-shot individual incentive is to withdraw (free-ride) — withdrawing increases your private payoff by 20 while reducing the group share by 11.5, so the net personal benefit to withdrawing instead of contributing is positive (20 − 11.5 = 8.5), holding others’ behavior fixed.

Given players do not know the total number of rounds and cannot communicate, typical dynamics are:
- Early rounds often show some tentative cooperation due to uncertainty, norm sensitivity, or optimism (some personas appear more likely to try cooperating initially).
- After each summary players see payoffs for everyone and can infer who contributed; because defecting yields higher immediate payoff, many players will update toward withdrawing.
- Over repeated play, cooperation typically falls as selfish incentives dominate and conditional cooperators give up.
- A few players with stronger prosocial priors may keep contributing for a while, but by mid-to-late play most players are expected to withdraw every round.

I will simulate a plausible sequence consistent with those incentives and the supplied player priors: an initially mixed contribution profile (some cooperate), then a steady decline to universal withdrawal.

This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with an unusually large multiplication (11.5×) of the shared pot, but a binary choice each round: withdraw all 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave the 20 in the pot (contribute 20). Because the multiplied pot is split equally, everyone's round payoff equals the common public share plus either 0 (if they contributed) or 20 (if they withdrew). That means free-riding always gives you 20 more than contributing in the same round, but group welfare is maximized if many people contribute because the multiplier is large.

Players do not know how many rounds there will be, so standard end‑game incentives (defect at the last round) are muted. However summaries reveal each player's net payoff each round; because contributors and withdrawers differ by exactly 20, players can infer who contributed from the payoff numbers. This creates clear feedback that can produce conditional cooperation (people imitate contributors) or opportunistic free-riding (copy the higher payoff behavior).

Given the provided personas (some more likely to contribute on first impressions, others more likely to free-ride), I expect initial moderate cooperation followed by a gradual decline as players see the temptation payoff of free-riding. Occasional rebounds are possible when a small group of prosocial players keeps contributing and others briefly reciprocate, but the dominant tendency is toward fewer contributors over time.

Some participants with stronger prosocial priors or lower sensitivity to immediate anchors may persist in contributing for a few rounds; others with high “search/anchor” tendencies or high immediate purchase-like preferences are more likely to defect from the start. Given these forces, I predict some modest initial cooperation by a small subset of players, followed by a steady decline toward all-withdraw behavior by the mid-to-late rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a classic tension: contributing the full 20 coins is socially efficient (each contributed pot yields a large multiplied return shared by everyone) but individually costly (a contributor loses private coins compared with withholding). Because contributions are binary (0 or 20) and punishment is available but costly (2 coins per punishment unit to the punisher, 3 coins deducted from the target), we expect mixed behavior early on and modest use of punishment to enforce cooperation. Players do not know the horizon, so there is no guaranteed end-game collapse; instead we expect an initial phase of uncertainty and some free-riding, followed by selective, sparse punishment targeted at repeat defectors. Punishment will be used strategically and sparingly because it is costly and anonymous (players see punishment losses but not who punished them). Over 10 rounds I predict cooperation will increase and stabilize as norm enforcement curbs opportunistic defections, with only a few isolated defections and light punitive responses across rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players. Each round each player either withdraws the entire endowment (20 coins) for private use (contribute 0) or leaves it in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players. Individually, withdrawing is always privately beneficial (you keep 20 and still receive your share of the multiplied pot), while leaving coins in the pot benefits the group more than the individual: cooperation raises the group payoff but costs the individual 8.5 coins in private terms (since your personal return from a 20-coin contribution is 11.5, less than 20). Because players do not know the total number of rounds, early rounds often see some experimentation or conditional cooperation; as players observe round-by-round payoffs (they see everyone’s net payoff each round), they can infer how many cooperated and who effectively cooperated or defected. Over time we therefore expect a tendency toward falling cooperation as the individual incentive to withdraw dominates, but some players with stronger cooperative tendencies (based on the provided personas) are likely to keep contributing, producing a stable minority of cooperators late in the sequence. Uncertainty about game length and heterogeneity in preferences (some players are more inclined to cooperate) means the decline will be gradual and uneven — not everyone switches at once.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players. Each round every player either keeps the full 20 coins for themselves (contribute 0 to the public pot) or leaves all 20 in the common pot (contribute 20). The common pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among the 19 players. A single player's decision to contribute increases the common share for everyone by 11.5*20/19 ≈ 12.105 per contributor; but contributing costs that player 20 coins. That means, holding others' actions fixed, contributing reduces a player's own round payoff by about 20 − 12.105 ≈ 7.895. In game-theoretic terms, defection (keeping the 20 privately) is each player's dominant action, though the group optimum is everyone contributing (which would yield 230 to each if all 19 contributed).

Given these incentives and the provided personas (some players have higher prior tendencies toward cooperative-like responses whereas others are more individualistic or likely to experiment), I expect an initial round with a modest minority of contributors (some pro-social or high “purchase-yes” types experiment by contributing). Once players see the round payoffs (they observe every player's round payoff), they can infer how many people contributed and whether those contributors were rewarded relative to defectors. Because defectors will earn exactly 20 coins more than cooperators in the same round, players will quickly learn that free-riding pays better in this payoff structure and contributions will decline over rounds. A few players with stronger cooperative priors (e.g., ELEPHANT, CROCODILE, COW, WHALE) may contribute across multiple rounds out of intrinsic preference or experimentation, but by mid-game most players will settle on defection. Toward the very end, occasional experimental contributions may reappear but will not sustainably increase cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a one-shot repeated public-goods environment played over an unknown number of rounds where each person either withdraws all 20 coins (defect) or leaves them in the shared pot (cooperate). The pot is multiplied strongly (×11.5) and then split equally among all active players, so group-optimal behavior (many contributors) yields very large per-player returns — but any single player who withdraws 20 earns exactly 20 more than a cooperator given the same total number of contributors. That creates a standard social dilemma: cooperating can be collectively lucrative but is individually exploitable.

Uncertainties that shape behavior here:
- Players do not see others' choices before deciding; they only see peers' round payoffs afterwards, so inference about others' actions is noisy but informative (defectors earn exactly 20 more than cooperators when the number of contributors is fixed).
- Players do not know how many rounds remain, which tends to sustain cooperation longer than a known finite horizon (no guaranteed last-round unraveling), but repeated feedback usually reveals defection profitability and erodes cooperation gradually.
- Individual priors (the supplied personas) make some players more likely to start cooperative (higher "purchase-yes" or prosocial cues) or to be more responsive to payoff information. Expect: an initial moderate level of cooperation from a mix of prosocial and ambiguous players; after the first few rounds many players will shift toward defection upon seeing the direct payoff advantage, leaving a small core of persistent cooperators.

I therefore predict: relatively high cooperation in Round 1, a measurable drop after players observe peer payoffs (defectors' payoffs exceed cooperators' by ~20), and a gradual decline in contributors across rounds, eventually leaving a small set of steady cooperators (people with consistent prosocial priors) by Round 10.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (withdrawing the full 20 coins for yourself) against a group benefit (leaving coins in the shared pot so the total is multiplied by 11.5 and split). Because contributions are binary (0 or 20), choices are stark: either fully contribute or fully free-ride each round. Punishment allows players to impose a cost on suspected free-riders, but punishment itself is costly (2 coins per unit to the punisher) and only removes 3 coins per unit from the target. Players cannot identify who punished them and they do not know how many rounds remain, so reputational considerations are limited to repeated interactions rather than explicit endgame behavior.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds often show substantial cooperation driven by expectations that many others will contribute; a minority may test by defecting.
- Punishment is used sparingly to enforce norms. Because it’s costly, only a few players typically punish, and punishments are small (1–2 units). Repeated defection tends to attract more punishment from several cooperators.
- Over time, punishments (and the cost of being targeted) encourage defectors to switch to contributing, producing higher cooperation rates after a few rounds.
- Because punishments are anonymous, occasional retaliatory cycles are possible but less likely when most players prefer the group benefit. Given players don’t know the final round, broad cooperation tends to be more stable than in known-length games.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This public-goods game pits a private incentive to keep 20 coins (defect) against a group benefit produced only if players put the full 20 into the shared pot (contribute). Because the pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally across the full set of active players, any single player’s private payoff is higher when they withhold (defect): contributing reduces that player's payoff relative to withholding. Without further incentives, the dominant (selfish) move is to withdraw 20.

Punishment changes the strategic environment. Punishment is costly to send (2 coins per unit) and reduces the target by 3 coins per unit; it is typically used sparingly to sanction persistent free-riders. Because players cannot see choices before acting, and cannot identify who specifically punished them later (only how much each peer spent on punishment and how much deduction they themselves received), punishment provides an imperfect but effective signal that can shift behavior: some defectors will convert to contributors after being punished, some cooperators will keep punishing repeat free-riders, and some punished players may retaliate or switch their own strategies.

Given the personas, I predict a mixed-but-enforcing dynamic: a small core of cooperators appears early (those with pro-social or higher "purchase-yes" tendencies), they occasionally punish clear free-riders (sparingly, usually 1 unit). Punishment is concentrated on persistent defectors, who then often switch to contributing in subsequent rounds. Over 10 rounds cooperation slowly rises from a minority to a stable majority, with occasional relapses and a small number of targeted punishments each round. Punishment edges are rare and low-intensity (mostly single-unit sends).

With a fairly stable cooperative majority, punishment events are occasional reminders targeted at sporadic defectors (e.g., SNAKE or a late free-rider). Some previously punished players remain cooperative, showing the deterrent effect.

Compliance remains high but not unanimous; a handful of players exploit occasionally. Punishment continues to be used sparingly against those repeat outliers—senders are typically established cooperators willing to pay a small cost to maintain the norm.

Most players have settled on contribution; a rare relapse can occur when perceived enforcement weakens, but peers quickly react with small penalties. Punishment patterns remain low-intensity—usually individual cooperators applying single-unit signals rather than mass retaliation.

Players do not know the horizon, so choices are driven by ongoing norms rather than endgame rush. Cooperation remains the default for most; a couple of persistent non-cooperators receive one-off sanctions to keep the norm intact. Punishment continues to be rare and low-cost per sender.
